title: Split Plots
text: Split plots produce a separate plot for each value of a categorical variable, with an interactive sidebar for navigation. Use them for subgroup analyses where each subgroup deserves its own focused view.

title: Split Plots
text:
Two Ways to Split







API
When to use




tabviz(..., split_by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)
Quick: split inline as you build the widget


tabviz(...) \|> split_table(by = ..., shared_axis = ...)
Fluent: split after constructing a WebSpec



Both produce a SplitForest object.

title: Split Plots
text:
split_table() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




x
required
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or data frame


by
required
Column name (single string) or character vector for hierarchical splits


shared_axis
FALSE
Use the same axis range across all subgroups


shared_column_widths
FALSE
Stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot (v0.16+). Useful when stacking screenshots in slides / PowerPoint — sub-plot columns won’t shift between tiles. Widths are computed once from the combined data.

title: Split Plots
text:
Basic Split


Code
set.seed(42)
trial_data <- data.frame(
  study = paste0("Study ", 1:30),
  region = sample(c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific"), 30, replace = TRUE),
  or = exp(rnorm(30, log(0.8), 0.3))
)
trial_data$lower <- trial_data$or * exp(-1.96 * 0.25)
trial_data$upper <- trial_data$or * exp(1.96 * 0.25)

tabviz(trial_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Odds Ratio (95% CI)")
  ),
  split_by = "region",
  title = "Treatment Effect by Region"
)






The floating sidebar shows each region. Click to navigate between subgroups.

title: Split Plots
text:
Hierarchical Splits
Pass a character vector to by for nested navigation:


Code
set.seed(123)
n <- 50
subgroup_data <- data.frame(
  study = paste0("Study ", sprintf("%02d", 1:n)),
  sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), n, replace = TRUE),
  age_group = sample(c("18-40", "41-65", "65+"), n, replace = TRUE),
  or = exp(rnorm(n, log(0.75), 0.25))
)
subgroup_data$lower <- subgroup_data$or * exp(-1.96 * 0.2)
subgroup_data$upper <- subgroup_data$or * exp(1.96 * 0.2)

tabviz(subgroup_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Odds Ratio")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))






The sidebar shows “Sex” as the first level header, with “Age Group” nested under each sex.

title: Split Plots
text:
Adding Extra Columns
Columns in the underlying tabviz() carry over into every sub-plot:


Code
tabviz(subgroup_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("sex", header = "Sex"),
    col_text("age_group", header = "Age"),
    col_interval("or", "lower", "upper", header = "OR (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))

title: Split Plots
text:
Shared Axis Range
By default each sub-plot auto-scales its axis. Pass shared_axis = TRUE for consistent visual scale across subgroups:


Code
tabviz(trial_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
) |>
  split_table(by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)

title: Split Plots
text:
Shared Column Widths (v0.16+)
When you plan to stack sub-plot screenshots in slides, PowerPoint, or side-by-side layouts, set shared_column_widths = TRUE. Each sub-plot will render with identical per-column widths, computed once from the combined data, so the columns line up tile-to-tile:


Code
tabviz(trial_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
) |>
  split_table(
    by = "region",
    shared_axis = TRUE,
    shared_column_widths = TRUE
  )






The default is FALSE to preserve the pre-v0.16 behavior where each sub-plot sizes its columns to its own content.
Since v0.19 the sidebar also has an Align column widths checkbox in its footer — users can toggle this behavior at runtime without going back to R.

title: Split Plots
text:
Sidebar Features
The sidebar includes:

Section headers — split-variable names displayed as uppercase labels
Collapsible — click the chevron to collapse the sidebar
Search — filter subgroups by typing
Keyboard navigation — arrow keys move between subgroups
Bold active — currently selected subgroup is highlighted

title: Split Plots
Split Plots
text:
Exporting Split Plots
Pass the SplitForest to save_split_table() (or save_plot(), which dispatches automatically). The directory structure mirrors the split hierarchy:
p <- tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))

save_split_table(p, "output", format = "svg")
# Writes (one file per (sex, age_group) leaf, mirrored as a directory tree):
#   output/Female/Female_18-40.svg
#   output/Female/Female_41-65.svg
#   output/Female/Female_65+.svg
#   output/Male/Male_18-40.svg
#   output/Male/Male_41-65.svg
#   output/Male/Male_65+.svg
See Export for save_plot() / save_split_table() details.

title: Visualization Styling
text: Every viz_* column — viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin — participates in the same color cascade. Markers, bars, boxes, and violins all resolve their fill (and sometimes their outline) by walking four layers from general to specific. Most of the time you only touch the layer that matches your intent and let the rest stay defaulted.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
The four layers
Each glyph’s color is decided by the most-specific layer that has a value:








Layer
Source
Wins when…




4. Per-row literal
marker_color = "col" or ~ formula on tabviz()
Value is non-NA for that row.


3. Row semantic class
row_emphasis / row_muted / row_accent / row_bold / row_fill on tabviz()
Layer 4 is NA. Single-effect: replaces fill with the token’s theme color. Multi-effect: preserves per-effect fill, adds an outline in the token’s color.


2. Per-effect literal
effect_*(color = "#hex") inside viz_*()
Layers 3–4 don’t apply. Sets the fill for that effect on every row.


1. Theme palette
theme.shapes.effect_colors[i]
Nothing else is set. Effects cycle through the palette by index.



Two things to internalize before the examples:

NA falls through. A marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "darkred", NA) colors the strict-significant rows red and lets every other row drop to whatever Layer 3 or below would have done.
Single vs. multi-effect matters at Layer 3. A semantic class on a single-effect viz repaints the glyph; on a multi-effect viz it adds an outline so the per-effect color identity (a key visual encoding) survives.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 1 — Theme palette
When you don’t say anything about color, viz cells pick from the active theme’s effect_colors palette. Single-effect viz takes the first color; multi-effect viz cycles by effect index.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  title = "Layer 1: theme palette",
  subtitle = "No color set anywhere — the marker takes the theme's first effect color"
)






Switching themes restyles automatically:


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "Same code, JAMA theme",
  subtitle = "The marker color tracks the active palette"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 2 — Per-effect color
Set color = "#hex" inside an effect_*() to nail down a single series. Useful when the encoding is intrinsic to the metric (“ITT is blue, per-protocol is green”) rather than to a row.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi",
                    label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",
                    label = "Per-protocol", color = "#16a34a")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  title = "Layer 2: per-effect colors",
  subtitle = "ITT and per-protocol get fixed identities across every row"
)






The same idea on viz_bar:


Code
sales <- data.frame(
  region = c("North", "South", "East", "West"),
  q1 = c(245, 189, 312, 98),
  q2 = c(278, 215, 345, 125),
  q3 = c(312, 242, 398, 156)
)

tabviz(sales, label = "region",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("q1", label = "Q1", color = "#3b82f6"),
      effect_bar("q2", label = "Q2", color = "#22c55e"),
      effect_bar("q3", label = "Q3", color = "#f59e0b"),
      header = "Sales ($K)"
    )
  ),
  title = "Per-effect colors on viz_bar",
  subtitle = "Each quarter holds its identity across regions"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 3 — Row semantic cascade
The five semantic tokens — row_emphasis, row_muted, row_accent, row_bold, row_fill — already style row text (see Row Styling). They also reach into viz cells. The behavior depends on whether the viz column has one effect or several.

Single-effect: fill replacement
The marker fill is replaced with the theme color. Below, row_accent highlights strongly significant trials in the theme’s accent color, row_muted dims the non-significant ones.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  row_muted  = ~ pval >= 0.10,
  title = "Layer 3 (single-effect): fill replacement",
  subtitle = "Accented rows take the accent color; muted rows fade out"
)






The cascade is themable — the same code under web_theme_jama() uses JAMA’s accent color instead.


Multi-effect: outline augment
If the column has more than one effect, replacing the fill would destroy the per-effect color encoding. The cascade preserves the fill and adds an outline in the theme color, so each accented row gets a visible “this row is special” cue without losing its ITT/per-protocol identity.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi",
                    label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",
                    label = "Per-protocol", color = "#16a34a")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "Layer 3 (multi-effect): outline augment",
  subtitle = "Per-effect colors preserved; accented rows get an outline"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 4 — Per-row literal marker_color
For exact control — a categorical palette, a colorblind-safe encoding, or matching a primary color — pass a column or formula to marker_color. Returning NA falls through to the layers below.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "#b91c1c", NA),
  title = "Layer 4: per-row literal",
  subtitle = "p < 0.001 rows go dark red; the rest fall through to the theme"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Combined precedence
When multiple layers are active, the most-specific one wins per row. Below: the strictest-significance rows are pinned to dark red (Layer 4), the still-significant rows take the theme accent (Layer 3), and the rest fall through to the theme palette (Layer 1).


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent   = ~ pval < 0.05,                                    # Layer 3
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "#b91c1c", NA_character_), # Layer 4
  title = "All layers together",
  subtitle = "p < 0.001 → red (L4), 0.001–0.05 → accent (L3), > 0.05 → palette (L1)"
)

title: Visualization Styling
section: The cascade is uniform across viz types
text:
The cascade is uniform across viz types
Everything above works identically on viz_bar, viz_boxplot, and viz_violin. A single example with a row_accent on each:

viz_bar


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("n"), header = "Enrollment", axis_label = "N enrolled")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ n > 1000,
  title = "row_accent on viz_bar",
  subtitle = "Bars for large trials take the accent color"
)








viz_violin


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_violin(effect_violin(data = "dist"),
               header = "Effect distribution",
               axis_label = "Posterior HR",
               show_quartiles = TRUE)
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "row_accent on viz_violin",
  subtitle = "Accented rows: the violin fill switches to the accent color"
)








viz_boxplot


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_boxplot(effect_boxplot(data = "dist"),
                header = "Effect distribution",
                axis_label = "Posterior HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "row_accent on viz_boxplot",
  subtitle = "Accented rows: the box fill switches to the accent color"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
When to reach for what

Stick with the theme when the viz is single-purpose and the row’s role isn’t categorical. The palette already does the right thing.
Use effect_*(color = ...) when the color identifies the metric (ITT vs. per-protocol, drug A vs. B). It belongs to the effect, not the row.
Use a row semantic token (row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted / row_bold / row_fill) when the color identifies the row (“this is the primary outcome”, “this is exploratory”). Themable, single-source: the row text and its viz both update.
Use marker_color (column or formula) when you need exact-pixel control — categorical row palettes, primary alignment, colorblind-safe encodings. Use NA to fall through to the cascade for rows you don’t want to override.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
See also

Row Styling — Text-side semantic classes that drive the same cascade
Styling Cascade — Higher-level overview of all four cascade layers across the package
Themes — Where effect_colors, accent, foreground, and muted live
Forest Plots, Bar Charts, Boxplots, Violin Plots — Per-viz feature reference

title: Visualizations
text: tabviz embeds visualizations directly inside table columns. Four viz_*() column types cover the common patterns; each gets its own focused page.

title: Visualizations
text:
The Four viz_* Columns



Column
Use this when…
Guide




viz_forest()
You have point estimates with confidence intervals (meta-analyses, treatment effects)
Forest Plots


viz_bar()
You’re comparing single values across rows (counts, scores, percentages)
Bar Charts


viz_boxplot()
You care about median, spread, and outliers across distributions
Boxplots


viz_violin()
You need to see full distribution shapes (bimodality, skewness)
Violin Plots









Noteviz_* vs col_*



viz_*() columns are focal visualizations with their own axes and scales — designed for direct visual comparison across rows. The simpler col_*() family (col_bar(), col_sparkline()) is for inline cell content that fits in a cell without a dedicated axis.
If you want a quick weight indicator inside a row, reach for col_bar(). If you want a true comparable bar chart, reach for viz_bar().

title: Visualizations
text:
Common Features
All viz_*() columns share:

Axis configuration — axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label
Pan and zoom — mouse wheel, drag, double-click reset (per column)
Theming — colors adapt to the active theme; multi-effect series use the theme’s effect_colors palette
Marker-styling cascade — row-level row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted and per-row marker_color reach into viz cells. Single-effect viz: fill replacement. Multi-effect viz: outline augment. See Visualization Styling for the full 4-layer cascade with rendered examples across viz types.
Annotations — refline() for reference lines (and forest_annotation() on viz_forest). Use null_value as a shortcut for a single themed reference line.
Tooltips — opt in via web_interaction(tooltip_fields = ...)
Export — SVG / PNG / PDF via save_plot()







TipTheme Effect Colors



When you have multiple effects per row (multiple bar series, boxplot groups, or forest effects), colors come from the theme’s series_anchors input. Each anchor expands into a 7-field slot bundle (fill, stroke, fill_muted, etc.) at resolve time:
web_theme_cochrane() |>
  set_inputs(series_anchors = c("#2563eb", "#dc2626", "#16a34a"))
Individual effects can still override the theme via their own color parameter.

title: Visualizations
Visualizations
text:
Exporting Visualizations
p <- tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1)
))

save_plot(p, "figure.svg")                           # vector, no deps
save_plot(p, "figure.png", width = 1200, scale = 2)  # raster, needs rsvg
save_plot(p, "figure.pdf", width = 800)              # vector, needs rsvg
See Export for details.

title: Visualizations
text:
See Also

Forest Plots — Confidence intervals with viz_forest()
Bar Charts — Magnitude comparisons with viz_bar()
Boxplots — Distribution summaries with viz_boxplot()
Violin Plots — Full distributions with viz_violin()
Visualization Styling — The 4-layer color cascade across all viz types
Columns — Inline columns including col_bar() and col_sparkline()

title: Bar Charts
text: viz_bar() creates horizontal bar charts that share a common axis across rows so the eye can compare magnitudes directly. Use it for counts, scores, percentages, or any single-value-per-row metric.

title: Bar Charts
text:
Quick example


Code
bar_data <- data.frame(
  metric = c("Response Rate", "Completion Rate", "Retention Rate", "Satisfaction"),
  value  = c(78, 92, 85, 88),
  category = c("Primary", "Primary", "Secondary", "Secondary")
)

tabviz(bar_data, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_text("category", "Category"),
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("value"),
      header = "Rate (%)",
      width  = 200,
      axis_range = c(0, 100)
    ),
    col_numeric("value", "%", width = 60)
  ),
  title = "Performance Metrics"
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
Multiple effects (grouped bars)
Pass several effect_bar() items to compare values per row with grouped bars:


Code
comparison_data <- data.frame(
  region   = c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific", "Latin America"),
  q1_sales = c(245, 189, 312, 98),
  q2_sales = c(278, 215, 345, 125),
  q3_sales = c(312, 242, 398, 156)
)

tabviz(comparison_data, label = "region",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("q1_sales", label = "Q1", color = "#3b82f6"),
      effect_bar("q2_sales", label = "Q2", color = "#22c55e"),
      effect_bar("q3_sales", label = "Q3", color = "#f59e0b"),
      header = "Quarterly Sales ($K)",
      width  = 250
    )
  ),
  title = "Regional Sales Comparison"
)






When you don’t pass per-effect colors, the theme’s series_anchors fill them automatically — switching themes restyles every bar. See Visualization Styling — Layer 1 for the full palette flow.

title: Bar Charts
text:
viz_bar() arguments



Argument
Default
Purpose




...
required
One or more effect_bar() items, optionally followed by named cell-styling args (see below)


header
NULL
Column header. NULL resolves to the primary effect’s label / field name


show_header
TRUE
Render the header cell above the chart


header_align
"center"
"left" / "center" / "right"


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log" (log requires all-positive values)


axis_range
auto
Numeric c(min, max); auto-computed when NULL


axis_ticks
auto
Numeric vector of explicit tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines at tick positions


axis_label
"Value"
Label rendered below the axis


show_axis
TRUE
Hide the axis with FALSE for compact dashboard tiles


null_value
NULL
Optional reference value — paints a dashed line at this position (a synthetic refline())


annotations
NULL
List of refline() items for additional reference lines (forest_annotation() is forest-specific and ignored here)









NoteCell-styling passthrough



Named arguments that follow the effect_bar() items are forwarded to web_col(): bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent. Cell-level args that don’t make sense for a graphical column (na_text, tooltip, formatter) are silently ignored — rows with no valid data are skipped, not text-filled.

title: Bar Charts
text:
effect_bar() arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




value
required
Column name containing the bar value


label
NULL
Display label (used in legends and as the resolved column header for single-effect viz)


color
NULL
Bar fill color (overrides the theme palette for this effect)


opacity
NULL
Bar opacity in [0, 1]

title: Bar Charts
text:
Axis control
Every axis option lives on viz_bar() directly. Set them per column.

Fixed range


Code
tabviz(bar_data, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("value"),
            header = "Rate (%)",
            axis_range = c(0, 100))
  ),
  title = "Axis pinned to 0–100"
)








Custom ticks and gridlines


Code
tabviz(bar_data, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("value"),
            header = "Rate (%)",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            axis_ticks = c(0, 25, 50, 75, 100),
            axis_gridlines = TRUE)
  ),
  title = "Custom ticks + gridlines"
)








Log scale
scale = "log" requires every bar value to be positive. Useful for population counts, dollar magnitudes, or any quantity that spans orders of magnitude.


Code
log_data <- data.frame(
  city = c("Tokyo", "Delhi", "Shanghai", "São Paulo", "Manila"),
  pop  = c(37e6, 32e6, 28e6, 22e6, 14e6)
)

tabviz(log_data, label = "city",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("pop"), header = "Population",
            scale = "log",
            axis_label = "Metro population (log scale)")
  )
)








Hide the axis for dense dashboards


Code
mini <- bar_data |> head(3)

tabviz(mini, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("value"),
            header = "%",
            show_axis = FALSE,
            width = 120,
            axis_range = c(0, 100))
  )
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
Reference lines
Use null_value for a single dashed line at a baseline / target / threshold. For multiple lines, pass a list of refline() items via annotations.


Code
target_data <- data.frame(
  team = c("Alpha", "Bravo", "Charlie", "Delta"),
  score = c(72, 88, 65, 91)
)

tabviz(target_data, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("score"),
            header = "Score",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            null_value = 80)
  ),
  title = "null_value = 80 — target line"
)








Code
tabviz(target_data, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("score"),
            header = "Score",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            annotations = list(
              refline(60, label = "Floor",  style = "dashed", color = "#dc2626"),
              refline(80, label = "Target", style = "solid",  color = "#16a34a"),
              refline(95, label = "Stretch", style = "dotted", color = "#0369a1")
            ))
  ),
  title = "Custom annotation set"
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
Styling cascade
Bar colors resolve through the same four-layer cascade as every other viz_*() column — the theme palette is the default, per-effect color = "..." pins a series identity, semantic row tokens (row_accent, row_emphasis, row_muted, row_bold, row_fill) reach into bars on tagged rows, and per-row marker_color wins for exact pixel control. See Visualization Styling for the full cascade with rendered examples.


Code
tabviz(target_data, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("score"),
            header = "Score",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            null_value = 80)
  ),
  row_accent = ~ score >= 80,
  title = "row_accent reaches into bars",
  subtitle = "Teams ≥ 80 take the theme accent fill"
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
When to reach for what

viz_bar() when you need a focal axis-backed comparison across rows (counts, scores, percentages).
col_bar() (Columns) when you only need an inline weight indicator inside a cell — no shared axis, no scale, fits next to a label.
viz_boxplot() / viz_violin() when you have a distribution per row, not a single value.
viz_forest() when you have point + lower + upper (confidence intervals).

title: Bar Charts
text:
See also

Visualization Styling — The 4-layer color cascade across all viz types
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, shared features
Columns — col_bar() — Inline cell-level bars
Forest Plots — Confidence intervals
Boxplots / Violin Plots — Distribution viz

title: Titles and Labels
text: Add context to any tabviz table with titles, subtitles, captions, and footnotes.

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Basic Usage


Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes",
    subtitle = "Major randomized controlled trials",
    caption = "HR < 1 favors treatment",
    footnote = "Data from published trial results",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    )
  )

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Label Positions



Argument
Position
Typical Use




title
Top, prominent
Main figure title


subtitle
Below title, smaller
Additional context, date range


caption
Bottom left
Interpretation help, scale explanation


footnote
Bottom right
Data source, methodology notes

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Without Visualizations
Labels work on any tabviz table, not just those with forest plots:


Code
data(nba_efficiency)

nba_efficiency |>
  filter(conference == "West") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "player",
    title = "Top Western Conference Players",
    subtitle = "2023-24 Season Statistics",
    caption = "PER: Player Efficiency Rating (15 = average)",
    columns = list(
      col_text("team", "Team"),
      col_numeric("ppg", "PPG", decimals = 1),
      col_numeric("per", "PER", decimals = 1),
      col_badge("award", "Award", variants = list(
        MVP = "success",
        `All-NBA 1st` = "info",
        `All-NBA 2nd` = "warning"
      ))
    ),
    theme = web_theme_cochrane()
  )

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Styling Labels
Label appearance is controlled by the theme. Different themes style labels differently:


Code
demo_data <- data.frame(
  item = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma"),
  value = c(1.23, 4.56, 7.89)
)

tabviz(demo_data,
  label = "item",
  title = "Presentation Theme",
  subtitle = "Larger fonts for slides",
  caption = "Source: Example data",
  columns = list(col_numeric("value", "Value", decimals = 2)),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |> set_variants(density = "spacious")
)

title: Titles and Labels
section: Axis labels live on the visualization column
text:
Axis labels live on the visualization column
Axis labels (the text below a forest / bar / box / violin chart’s x-axis) are a property of the viz column, not of the table. Set them inside the viz_*() constructor with axis_label = "...":
viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
           scale = "log", null_value = 1,
           axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)")
See Forest Plots — Axis Control for the full set of axis options (axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label).

title: Titles and Labels
text:
See Also

Themes — Control label typography and colors
Visualizations — Forest plots and other graphical columns
Export — Save figures with labels intact

title: Row Groups
text: Organize your forest plot into collapsible sections using the group parameter. Ideal for meta-analyses with natural categories like study design, region, or subpopulation.

title: Row Groups
section: What you can pass to group
text:
What you can pass to group







Form
Effect




group = "region"
Single flat grouping — one collapsible header per unique value


group = c("region", "country")
Hierarchical nesting — outer level collapses inner levels


group = list(web_group("North", collapsed = FALSE), web_group("South", collapsed = TRUE))
Explicit definitions; lets you set initial collapse state and custom labels









Noteweb_group(id, ...) contract



The id of each web_group() must match a value present in the corresponding data column — that’s how tabviz knows which rows belong to which group. With web_group("North", ...) and web_group("South", ...), the data must have a column whose values include "North" and "South" (tabviz auto-detects which column). Use label = if you want the displayed header to differ from the data value.

title: Row Groups
text:
Basic Grouping
Pass a column name to group to create collapsible sections:


Code
grouped_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A1", "Study A2", "Study B1", "Study B2", "Study B3"),
  region = c("North", "North", "South", "South", "South"),
  or = c(0.8, 0.9, 1.1, 1.3, 0.95),
  lower = c(0.6, 0.7, 0.85, 1.0, 0.7),
  upper = c(1.1, 1.15, 1.4, 1.7, 1.3)
)

tabviz(grouped_data,
  label = "study",
  group = "region",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1)
  )
)






Click the group headers to collapse or expand sections. Groups are rendered with:

Distinct header rows with chevron indicators
Automatic indentation of child rows
Subtle background shading for visual hierarchy

title: Row Groups
text:
Hierarchical Grouping
For multi-level organization, pass multiple column names as a vector. tabviz creates nested, collapsible hierarchies:


Code
hierarchical_data <- data.frame(
  study = c(
    "Northeast Trial", "Southeast Trial", "Midwest Trial",
    "Ontario Study", "Quebec Study",
    "England RCT", "Scotland RCT",
    "Bavaria Trial", "Berlin Trial"
  ),
  region = c(
    rep("Americas", 5),
    rep("Europe", 4)
  ),
  country = c(
    rep("USA", 3), rep("Canada", 2),
    rep("UK", 2), rep("Germany", 2)
  ),
  or = c(0.82, 0.91, 0.78, 0.85, 0.88, 0.92, 0.87, 0.79, 0.84),
  lower = c(0.68, 0.75, 0.62, 0.70, 0.72, 0.76, 0.71, 0.64, 0.68),
  upper = c(0.99, 1.10, 0.98, 1.03, 1.07, 1.11, 1.07, 0.98, 1.04)
)

tabviz(hierarchical_data,
  label = "study",
  group = c("region", "country"),
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
)






Each level of the hierarchy:

Can be collapsed independently
Has progressively deeper indentation
Uses subtle visual differentiation (font weight, background)

title: Row Groups
Row Groups
text:
Starting Collapsed
To have groups start in a collapsed state, you’ll need to use explicit group definitions with web_group():


Code
tabviz(grouped_data,
  label = "study",
  group = list(
    web_group("North", collapsed = FALSE),
    web_group("South", collapsed = TRUE)
  ),
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1)
  )
)


This is useful for large datasets where you want users to expand only the sections they’re interested in.

title: Row Groups
text:
Grouping vs Row Styling
tabviz provides two complementary ways to organize data:








Feature
Purpose
Best For




group
Automatic collapsible sections from column values
Categorical data with natural groupings


row_type
Manual section headers, summaries, spacers
Structured layouts with subtotals



Use group when your data has a grouping column. Use row_type when you need precise control over the layout structure, such as meta-analysis summary rows. See Row Styling for the manual approach.
You can combine both approaches—use group for automatic organization and row_type for summary diamonds within each group.

title: Row Groups
text:
See Also

Row Styling — Manual row types, headers, summaries, and visual styling
Themes — Customize group header appearance
tabviz() — Complete argument reference

title: Guide Overview
text: This guide takes you from your first table to advanced styling, interactivity, and Shiny integration. Pages are grouped into the same sections you see in the sidebar — read top-to-bottom for the curriculum, or jump to whichever section you need.

title: Guide Overview
text:
Get going



Page
What you’ll learn

text:

Median + spread + outliers with viz_boxplot()


Violin Plots

Interactivity
Sort, filter, drag, in-place edit, tooltips


Shiny
