title: Guide Overview
text:
Two entry points
tabviz() is the general-purpose constructor. forest_plot() is a thin convenience wrapper for the common case where you only need point + lower + upper.
# Full control
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("category"),
    col_numeric("value"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"),
    col_sparkline("trend")
  )
)

# Quick forest plot
forest_plot(data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
)






TipWhen to reach for forest_plot()



Use forest_plot() when the table is “labels + a forest plot + maybe a CI string.” The moment you need extra columns, badges, or column groups, switch to tabviz().

title: Guide Overview
text:
Where else to look

Cheatsheet — one-page lookup for functions and arguments.
Concepts — architecture, mental models, and the styling cascade.
Reference — full API documentation.
Gallery — runnable examples by domain.
Troubleshooting — common issues and fixes.

title: Styling Overview
text: tabviz styling is a four-layer cascade — each layer targets a more specific scope and overrides the layer above. This page is the map; follow the links for the details.

title: Styling Overview
text:
The Cascade
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Theme        — base colors, fonts, spacing         │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Row         — bold, color, background per row │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │  Cell      — per-column formatting       │  │  │
│  │  │  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐  │  │  │
│  │  │  │  Marker  — forest-plot points      │  │  │  │
│  │  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘  │  │  │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘  │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘








Layer
Where you set it
Use it for




Theme
theme = web_theme_*() on tabviz()
Overall look and feel


Row
row_bold, row_color, row_bg, … on tabviz()
Whole-row conditional formatting


Cell
bold, color, bg, … inside col_*()
Per-column overrides


Marker
marker_color, marker_shape, marker_size, marker_opacity on tabviz()
Forest-plot point appearance



More specific layers win: marker beats cell, cell beats row, row beats theme. Formula expressions and column-name strings are equivalent at every layer — pick whichever reads better at the call site.






TipSemantic shortcuts



Theme-aware semantic tokens — row_emphasis, row_muted, row_accent, row_bold, row_fill (and the matching emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill cell-level args) — let you say “this row/cell is special” without picking literal colors. They restyle automatically when you swap themes. See Themes — Semantic tokens for the full list.

title: Styling Overview
text:
1. Theme Level
Themes set the foundational palette, typography, spacing, and shapes.


Code
data(glp1_trials)
sample_data <- head(glp1_trials[glp1_trials$row_type == "data", ], 4)

tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
2. Row Level
Row styling applies formatting to entire rows based on data conditions.


Code
tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_bold = ~ upper < 1,
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
3. Cell Level
Cell styling targets individual columns. Use .x inside formulas to refer to the column’s own value.


Code
tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", decimals = 2,
                bold = ~ .x < 1,
                color = ~ ifelse(.x < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"))
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
4. Marker Level
Marker styling controls forest-plot point appearance: color, shape, size, opacity.


Code
tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  marker_shape = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "diamond", "square"),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

title: Styling Overview
text:
Formula Expressions
tabviz uses R’s formula syntax (~ expression) for conditional styling.

Row and marker formulas can reference any column in the data:
row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05
marker_color = ~ ifelse(group == "Treatment", "blue", "gray")
Cell formulas use .x for the current column’s value:
col_numeric("hr", "HR", bold = ~ .x < 1)
col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", color = ~ ifelse(.x < 0.05, "red", "black"))

title: Styling Overview
text:
Next Steps

Row Styling — Row-level conditional formatting
Cell Styling — Per-column formatting
Themes — Built-in themes and customization
Fluent API — Pipe-based modifiers for complex cases

title: Quick Start
text: Build your first interactive forest plot in about 30 lines, then layer in styling, themes, and conditional formatting.

title: Quick Start
text:
Installation
# Install from GitHub using pak
install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("kaskarn/tabviz")






NoteKey concepts




Label column: the row identifier (study name, category, item).
Column types: col_*() for data columns, viz_*() for visualizations.
Row types: "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".
Column mapping: pass column names as strings; values are computed per row.

title: Quick Start
text:
Your first table


Code
data(glp1_trials)

meta_data <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5)

tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)












TipBuilt-in datasets



This example uses glp1_trials, one of five datasets shipped with the package. See Package Data for all available datasets.

title: Quick Start
text:
The core pattern
tabviz(data,
  label = "label_column",    # row labels
  columns = list(...)        # what columns to show
)
Each col_*() function defines a column. The most common helpers:








Function
Purpose
Example




col_text()
Text
col_text("category")


col_numeric()
Numbers
col_numeric("value", decimals = 1)


col_n()
Sample sizes
col_n("n")


viz_forest()
Forest plot
viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")


col_interval()
Point (CI)
col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR")


col_pvalue()
P-values
col_pvalue("pval")



See Columns for the full catalog.

title: Quick Start
text:
Adding more columns


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_numeric("events", header = "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)

title: Quick Start
text:
Applying themes
tabviz ships with seven publication-ready themes — four journal-style presets and three illustrative LOTR-flavored themes:

Code
tabviz(meta_data[1:3, ],
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama(), title = "JAMA"
)
tabviz(meta_data[1:3, ],
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_lancet(), title = "Lancet"
)













Journal presets: web_theme_cochrane() (default), web_theme_lancet(), web_theme_jama(), web_theme_dark(). LOTR presets: web_theme_dwarven(), web_theme_elvish(), web_theme_hobbit().
See Themes for customization, the set_*() modifier family, and how to register your own preset.

title: Quick Start
text:
Row styling
Build visual hierarchy with row types and styling:


Code
styled_data <- data.frame(
  label = c("Primary Endpoint", "  CV Death", "  MI", "Summary"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.82, 0.79, 0.78),
  lower = c(NA, 0.72, 0.68, 0.65),
  upper = c(NA, 0.94, 0.92, 0.93),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "summary"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

tabviz(styled_data,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold"
)












NoteRow types






Type
Behavior




"data"
Standard row with interval marker


"header"
Section header, no marker


"summary"
Summary row with diamond marker


"spacer"
Empty row for separation



Header and spacer rows should have NA for point, lower, and upper.

title: Quick Start
text:
Conditional styling
Style rows based on data values using formula expressions:


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_bold = ~ upper < 1,
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
)






Or use pre-computed columns:


Code
direction_data <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    marker_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"
    )
  )

tabviz(direction_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  marker_color = "marker_color"
)

title: Quick Start
text:
Next steps



Topic
What you’ll learn




Columns
The full col_*() / viz_*() catalog


Row Styling
Headers, summaries, indentation, badges, semantic tokens


Cell Styling
Conditional formatting for individual cells


Row Groups
Collapsible groups, hierarchical nesting


Forest Plots
Advanced forest column options


Themes
Preset themes (4 journal + 3 LOTR) and customization


Export → Pagination
Break long tables across multiple PDF pages

title: Recipes
text:
Short, self-contained snippets for specific patterns that aren’t covered on a single dedicated page. Each recipe is a single drop-in pattern. For broader topics see the Fluent API guide; for col_* / viz_* / set_* argument detail see the reference section.
For recipes that are the bread and butter of one page (significance highlighting, p-value formatting, split-by with shared axis), follow the link to that page directly — those topics have full examples there.

title: Recipes
text:
Composition

Build a table-only view (no visualization column)
Drop viz_forest() (or any viz_*) — any table works without a viz column.


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pval", "P")
  )
)




Show multiple effect sizes side-by-side
Stack viz_forest() columns with a shared axis_range so the eye can compare across them.


Code
axis_range <- c(0.4, 2.0)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr_primary", lower = "primary_lo", upper = "primary_hi",
               header = "Primary outcome",
               axis_range = axis_range, null_value = 1, scale = "log"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr_secondary", lower = "secondary_lo", upper = "secondary_hi",
               header = "Secondary outcome",
               axis_range = axis_range, null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  )
)




Compose a reusable house-style function
Wrap your organization’s standards in a function. Works on both WebSpec and htmlwidget inputs because every set_*() returns the same type it received.


Code
house_style <- function(x) {
  x |>
    set_marker_style(color = "direction_color") |>
    set_row_style(bold = "is_primary", type = "row_type") |>
    set_theme(web_theme_jama() |>
                set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"),     TRUE) |>
                set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridline_style"), "dotted"))
}

tabviz(trial_a, ...) |> house_style()
tabviz(trial_b, ...) |> house_style()

title: Recipes
text:
Styling & annotations

Badge the top-N rows
Rank by weight, tag the top 3 with a position badge.


Code
data |>
  mutate(
    rank  = rank(-weight),
    badge = ifelse(rank <= 3, paste0("#", rank), NA_character_)
  ) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 null_value = 1, scale = "log")
    ),
    row_badge = "badge"
  )




Pin a custom reference line on a forest
null_value = 1 already paints the “no effect” line; add refline() entries to layer extra references such as a pooled estimate.


Code
viz_forest(
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1,
  annotations = list(
    refline(1,    label = "No effect",  style = "solid",  color = "#111"),
    refline(0.85, label = "Pooled HR",  style = "dashed", color = "#0066cc")
  )
)

title: Recipes
Recipes
text:
Export

Save at retina (2×) resolution
scale controls the pixel density of raster output.


Code
plot <- tabviz(data, ...)

save_plot(plot, "forest.svg")                            # vector
save_plot(plot, "forest.png", scale = 2)                 # retina PNG
save_plot(plot, "forest.pdf", width = 800)               # vector PDF


For long tables that need to span multiple PDF pages, see Export → Pagination.

title: Recipes
text:
See also
These topics have full coverage on a dedicated page rather than a recipe here:

Conditional row styling by significance → Row Styling — Expression-Based Styling
P-value formatting with thresholds and stars → Columns — col_pvalue()
Split by subgroup with shared axis → Split Plots — Shared Axis Range
All the proxy verbs → Fluent API guide / Data operations reference

title: Cell Styling
text: This guide covers per-cell styling — formatting that applies to individual cells inside a specific column, with optional conditions on the cell’s own value or other columns in the row.

title: Cell Styling
text:
Cell Style Parameters
Every col_*() function accepts these styling parameters. The first six are literal — you set the value directly. The last five are theme-aware semantic tokens: tag a cell with accent and the theme paints it in its own accent color, restyling automatically when you swap themes.








Parameter
Type
Description




bold
Column name or formula
Bold text when TRUE


italic
Column name or formula
Italic text when TRUE


color
Column name or formula
Text color (literal CSS color)


bg
Column name or formula
Background color (literal CSS color)


badge
Column name or formula
Badge label


icon
Column name or formula
Emoji/unicode icon


emphasis (semantic)
Column name or formula
Bold + theme primary color


muted (semantic)
Column name or formula
Lighter color, reduced prominence


accent (semantic)
Column name or formula
Bold + theme accent color


bold (semantic)
Column name or formula
Weight bump only, no color change. Same name as the literal bold above; pass via the style_bold arg if you need to disambiguate.


fill (semantic)
Column name or formula
Bold + strong row-fill background (auto-contrasting fg)



The literal color / bg win over semantic tokens on the same cell — they’re more specific. See Themes — Semantic tokens for the underlying RowSemantic bundles.
Each parameter accepts either:

A column name (string): The values in that column control styling per row
A formula expression (~ ...): Evaluated on the fly to compute styling

title: Cell Styling
text:
Formula Expressions for Cell Styling
Instead of pre-computing style columns, you can use formulas that reference your data. For cell-level styling, use .x to refer to the column’s own values:


Code
# Data without any pre-computed style columns
pval_demo <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C", "Trial D"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91, 0.68),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75, 0.52),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10, 0.89),
  pval = c(0.001, 0.1, 0.3, 0.02)
)

tabviz(pval_demo,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_pvalue("pval",
      # .x refers to the pval column values
      bold = ~ .x < 0.05,
      color = ~ ifelse(.x < 0.05, "#16a34a", "#71717a")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)







The .x pronoun
The pronoun rule depends on where you write the formula:








Where
What .x means
Bare names available?




Row level (tabviz(row_bold = ~ ...))
Not used — write bare column names
Yes, e.g. ~ pval < 0.05


Cell level (col_*(bold = ~ ...))
The current column’s values
Yes — bare names also resolve





Code
# Cell level: .x is this column's values
col_pvalue("pval", bold = ~ .x < 0.05)

# Cell level: you can also reference other columns by bare name
col_numeric("hr", color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.05, "green", "gray"))

# Row level: always use bare column names
tabviz(data, ..., row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05)




When to Use Formulas vs Columns







Approach
Best For




Formulas (~ .x < 0.05)
Simple conditions, self-referential logic, one-off styling


Column names ("is_sig")
Complex logic computed separately, reused across plots









TipFormula Advantage



Formulas keep styling logic close to where it’s used, making your code more readable:
# Without formulas - need to pre-compute
data <- data |> mutate(pval_bold = pval < 0.05)
col_pvalue("pval", bold = "pval_bold")

# With formulas - logic inline
col_pvalue("pval", bold = ~ .x < 0.05)

title: Cell Styling
text:
Basic Example


Code
# Data with styling columns
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("ACCORD", "ADVANCE", "SPRINT", "ONTARGET"),
  hr = c(0.65, 1.15, 0.82, 0.98),
  lower = c(0.50, 0.95, 0.68, 0.85),
  upper = c(0.85, 1.40, 0.99, 1.14),
  pval = c(0.002, 0.18, 0.04, 0.62),
  # Styling columns (computed from data)
  sig_bold = c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE),          # Bold if p < 0.05
  dir_color = c("#22c55e", "#ef4444", "#22c55e", "#71717a")  # Green/red/gray
)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", color = "dir_color", bold = "sig_bold"),
    col_pvalue("pval", bold = "sig_bold"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Cell Styling
text:
Conditional Formatting Patterns

Color by Effect Direction


Code
styled <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C", "Trial D"),
  hr = c(0.72, 1.25, 0.88, 1.05),
  lower = c(0.58, 1.02, 0.74, 0.92),
  upper = c(0.89, 1.53, 1.05, 1.20)
) |>
  mutate(
    # Determine direction and significance
    effect_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",  # Significant benefit (green)
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",  # Significant harm (red)
      TRUE ~ "#71717a"        # Non-significant (gray)
    )
  )

tabviz(styled,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", color = "effect_color"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)








Highlight Significant Results


Code
trials <- data.frame(
  study = c("EMPA-REG", "CANVAS", "DECLARE", "CREDENCE", "DAPA-HF"),
  hr = c(0.62, 0.86, 0.93, 0.70, 0.74),
  lower = c(0.49, 0.75, 0.84, 0.59, 0.65),
  upper = c(0.77, 0.97, 1.03, 0.82, 0.85),
  pval = c(0.0001, 0.02, 0.17, 0.0001, 0.0001)
) |>
  mutate(
    is_sig = pval < 0.05,
    sig_bg = if_else(is_sig, "#fef3c7", NA_character_),  # Yellow highlight
    sig_badge = if_else(pval < 0.001, "***", if_else(pval < 0.01, "**", if_else(pval < 0.05, "*", NA_character_)))
  )

tabviz(trials,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", bold = "is_sig", bg = "sig_bg"),
    col_pvalue("pval", badge = "sig_badge"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)








Traffic Light Styling


Code
outcomes <- data.frame(
  endpoint = c("Primary", "CV Death", "HF Hosp", "All-cause Death"),
  hr = c(0.74, 0.82, 0.65, 0.88),
  lower = c(0.66, 0.72, 0.55, 0.76),
  upper = c(0.83, 0.94, 0.77, 1.02)
) |>
  mutate(
    status = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "benefit",
      lower > 1 ~ "harm",
      TRUE ~ "neutral"
    ),
    icon = case_when(
      status == "benefit" ~ "\u2705",   # Green check
      status == "harm" ~ "\u274C",      # Red X
      TRUE ~ "\u2796"                   # Dash
    ),
    text_color = case_when(
      status == "benefit" ~ "#16a34a",
      status == "harm" ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#525252"
    )
  )

tabviz(outcomes,
  label = "endpoint",
  columns = list(
    col_text("icon", " ", width = 30),
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", color = "text_color"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", color = "text_color"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Cell Styling
text:
Combining Row and Cell Styling
Row styling applies to the entire row, while cell styling allows overrides for specific columns:


Code
combined <- data.frame(
  label = c("Primary Endpoints", "  CV death or HF hosp", "  CV death",
            "Secondary Endpoints", "  All-cause mortality"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.74, 0.82, NA, 0.88),
  lower = c(NA, 0.66, 0.72, NA, 0.76),
  upper = c(NA, 0.83, 0.94, NA, 1.02),
  # Row styling
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "header", "data"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE),
  rcolor = c("#2563eb", NA, NA, "#2563eb", NA),
  rbadge = c(NA, "Primary", NA, NA, NA),
  # Cell styling (overrides for specific cells)
  hr_color = c(NA, "#16a34a", "#16a34a", NA, "#71717a")
)

tabviz(combined,
  label = "label",
  row_type = "rtype", row_bold = "rbold", row_color = "rcolor", row_badge = "rbadge",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", color = "hr_color"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Cell Styling
text:
dplyr Workflow for Style Columns
A typical workflow computes style columns with dplyr before plotting:


Code
meta_results <- data.frame(
  study = c("SHIFT", "PARADIGM-HF", "DAPA-HF", "EMPEROR-Reduced"),
  drug_class = c("Ivabradine", "ARNI", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i"),
  hr = c(0.82, 0.80, 0.74, 0.75),
  lower = c(0.75, 0.73, 0.65, 0.65),
  upper = c(0.90, 0.87, 0.85, 0.86),
  pval = c(0.0001, 0.0001, 0.0001, 0.0001)
)

styled_results <- meta_results |>
  mutate(
    # Significance styling
    is_sig = pval < 0.05,
    sig_stars = case_when(
      pval < 0.001 ~ "***",
      pval < 0.01 ~ "**",
      pval < 0.05 ~ "*",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    # Effect direction
    effect_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#71717a"
    ),
    # Drug class styling
    class_color = case_when(
      drug_class == "SGLT2i" ~ "#2563eb",
      drug_class == "ARNI" ~ "#7c3aed",
      TRUE ~ "#525252"
    )
  )

tabviz(styled_results,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug_class", "Class", color = "class_color"),
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", bold = "is_sig", color = "effect_color"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", color = "effect_color"),
    col_pvalue("pval", badge = "sig_stars"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Cell Styling
text:
Style Column Naming Conventions
Keep your data organized by using consistent naming:



Style Type
Suggested Suffix
Example




Bold
_bold
hr_bold, is_bold


Color
_color
effect_color, pval_color


Background
_bg
sig_bg, row_bg


Badge
_badge
sig_badge, status_badge


Icon
_icon
status_icon



This makes it clear which columns are data vs styling, and helps when debugging formatting issues.

title: Cell Styling
text:
See Also

Styling Overview — Where this layer fits in the cascade
Row Styling — Whole-row formatting and semantic-token shortcuts
Themes — Theme-aware color tokens (accent, emphasis, etc.)
Columns — col_*() argument reference

title: Styling Cascade
text:
tabviz has two cascades, working at different layers.
Knowing which cascade a property lives in tells you exactly where to set it — and which other layers it will override.
flowchart LR
    subgraph TC["Theme cascade (build time)"]
      T1[Inputs] --> T2[Semantic roles] --> T3[Component bindings]
    end

    subgraph RC["Runtime cascade (render time)"]
      D[Theme defaults] --> R[Row styling] --> C[Cell styling] --> M[Marker styling]
    end

    T3 --> D

    style T1 fill:#dbeafe
    style T2 fill:#bfdbfe
    style T3 fill:#93c5fd
    style D fill:#e5e7eb
    style R fill:#86efac
    style C fill:#6ee7b7
    style M fill:#fbbf24
The theme cascade hands a fully resolved visual specification to the runtime cascade. The runtime cascade then specializes per row / cell / marker.

title: Styling Cascade
text:
Cascade A — the theme

What a theme is, ontologically
A theme is a small set of customer choices plus the rules for turning those choices into a fully specified visual surface. Think of it as having three concentric rings:
       ┌─ Tier 3: Component bindings (header, row, cell, plot, marks…) ─┐
       │   ┌─ Tier 2: Semantic roles (surface, content, divider…) ──┐   │
       │   │   ┌─ Tier 1: Inputs (~15 customer choices) ─────────┐  │   │
       │   │   │   neutral, primary, secondary, accent,           │  │   │
       │   │   │   slot_style, header_style, series, fonts, …     │  │   │
       │   │   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │   │
       │   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
       └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                      ↓
                          resolve_theme(theme)
                                      ↓
                       fully populated WebTheme
Only Tier 1 is authored. Tier 2 and Tier 3 are derived by resolve_theme(). Customers (and presets) can also reach into Tier 2 / Tier 3 to pin individual fields when the derivation isn’t quite right; resolution leaves those overrides alone.


Two ontologies, sharing only Tier 1
The cascade splits into two independent vocabularies:









Subsystem
Inputs
Tier 2 roles
What they describe




Chrome
neutral, primary, primary_deep, secondary, secondary_deep, accent, accent_deep, slot_style, header_style
surface, content, divider, accent.*, status, semantic
The table’s scaffolding — the surface a row sits on, the tone of body text, the rule between cells


Data
series_anchors, status_*, slot_style (shared)
per-series SlotBundle (the first slot also drives the pooled-effect diamond)
The data marks — what a forest point looks like, how a row’s “muted” state mutes its mark



They never cross at the chrome/data boundary. Changing primary cannot affect a series fill (other than the seeded series[1]). Changing series_anchors[3] cannot affect a row-group divider. The two cascades meet only at theme reassembly. This isolation is what makes themes predictable: you can swap a primary color and know the data marks will not silently shift hue, and vice versa.
slot_style (fill_with_darker_stroke | flat_fill | outlined) is the one input both cascades read — chrome doesn’t use it, but it determines the fill/stroke pairing convention for every series SlotBundle. See the Theming Manifesto for the full two-knob principle (identity + engagement) and why the contract is shaped this way.


The vocabulary at Tier 2
Tier 2 names matter because they’re how Tier 3 (and overrides) refer to the system. Chrome:







Role
Use




surface.base
Row background, default canvas


surface.muted
Banding partner, sunken regions


surface.raised
Cards, popovers


content.primary
Body text, cell values


content.secondary
Subtitles, captions


content.muted
Axis labels, footnotes


content.inverse
Text on dark fills (bold-mode header)


divider.subtle
Cell hairlines, gridlines (10% mix of secondary_deep into neutral)


divider.strong
Header rule, group L1 underline, axis line, tick marks (5% mix of secondary_deep)


accent.default
Selection bar, hover focus


accent.muted
Hover row fill, selected row fill


accent.tint_subtle
Subtle accent-tinted treatments


accent.tint_medium
Cascading bold-mode tints


semantic.fill
Soft row-fill tint (80% mix of accent into neutral)



Data:



Role
Use




series[i].fill
Mark body for the i-th effect


series[i].stroke
Companion outline / CI line


series[i].fill_muted
The same effect on a muted row


series[i].fill_emphasis
The same effect on an emphasized row


series[i].text_fg
Data labels associated with the slot



The pooled-effect diamond reads from series[1] (the primary effect’s slot bundle) — there is no separate summary slot. To customize the diamond fill alone, override series_anchors[1]; to give it a journal-specific outline, set theme@series[[1]]@stroke directly with set_theme_field().
A SlotBundle carries every state for one slot. The renderer always reads the right field for the current row state — you don’t author each state by hand.


Variants pick, they don’t synthesize
header_style ∈ {light, tint, bold} and first_column_style ∈ {default, bold} are per-table toggles. All three header variants are always pre-resolved on every theme: theme$header$light, theme$header$tint, and theme$header$bold all carry full bg/fg/rule values. Flipping the variant at render time is a cheap dispatch — the renderer reads theme$header[theme$variants$header_style].
The three header variants:

light — bare surface bg, content.primary text, divider.strong rule. The publication default.
tint — light primary-tinted bg (12% mix of primary_deep into surface.base), content.primary text, divider.strong rule. A middle ground for editorial section headers.
bold — primary_deep bg, content.inverse text, component-local rule (40% mix of inverse into bg). Maximum chrome.

header_style also gates the row-group L1 bg strength: 16% mix of secondary_deep into surface under light, 24% under tint/bold. Row-group bars track the leaf header’s visual weight automatically — single rule from a single T1 input.
density ∈ {compact, comfortable, spacious} is similar but generates a full SpacingTokens table at resolve time. Per-token set_spacing() overrides land on top of the density preset.


Resolution is a function
resolve_theme(theme) -> WebTheme is idempotent and pure. Run it twice, get the same answer. Override any field, re-run resolution, your override survives (resolution only fills NA-valued fields).
This means the cascade is debuggable. Inspect any resolved field directly via S7 accessors:


Code
t <- web_theme_lancet()
t@inputs@primary       # "#00407A"        (Tier 1)
t@accent@tint_subtle   # derived blend    (Tier 2)
t@row_group@L1@bg      # secondary-tinted (Tier 3)
t@series[[1]]@fill     # series anchor 1
t@series[[1]]@stroke   # OKLCH-paired companion (per slot_style)


Or use inspect_resolved() for a value + cascade + derivation summary:


Code
inspect_resolved(t, "header.bold.bg")
#> $path: "header.bold.bg"
#> $value: "#002D54"
#> $cascade: c("inputs$primary_deep", "inputs$primary")
#> $derivation: "inputs$primary_deep (auto: oklch_darken(primary, 0.15))"


inspect_resolved() returns the resolved hex alongside the cascade chain that produced it — useful for debugging custom themes and for surfacing cascade tooltips in the widget’s Theme tab. The same registry shape feeds both the R REPL and the panel UI.
OKLCH is the underlying color algebra. All blends, lightenings, and darkenings happen in OKLCH (perceptually uniform), with a final clip to sRGB. Quantitative claim: a 12% blend toward surface.base looks 12% blended whether the starting hue is navy, gold, or magenta.


Construction-time validation
resolve_theme() runs a final pass that checks contrast invariants on the fully-resolved theme — bold-band header text reading on its band, body text reading on the surface, etc. WCAG AA Large (3.0) for chrome bold text; AA Normal (4.5) for body text. Failures abort at construction (not at render) with a structured cli message naming the broken invariant and the cascade path the user can override to fix it.
# Hypothetical broken theme: pale primary auto-darkens to a primary_deep
# that doesn't carry enough contrast against the default inverse text.
resolve_theme(WebTheme(inputs = ThemeInputs(primary = "#C8D4E8")))
#> Error in `validate_resolved_theme(theme)`:
#> Theme failed 1 contrast invariant.
#> i Each violation lists the cascade path you can override to fix it.
#> x header bold band: header.bold.fg must read on header.bold.bg
#>   fg=#FFFFFF on bg=#99A4B7 -> contrast=2.52 (need >=3.0)
#>   cascade: header.bold.fg <- header.bold.bg <- content.inverse <- inputs$primary_deep
Tests using synthetic high-saturation colors to verify cascade mechanics can opt out: resolve_theme(theme, .validate = FALSE).


What this is not
It’s worth saying what the model deliberately does not do:

No global “muted” or “primary” color. v1 had a single colors$muted that meant both “subdued text” and “deemphasized data.” v2 splits these: content.muted for text, slot.fill_muted for data marks. They share Tier 1 (the primary/secondary/accent inputs) but never share a Tier 2 role.
No third identity tier. Identity is two tiers — primary plus optional secondary (mirrors primary when NA), with orthogonal accent for engagement. A tertiary knob was added on 2026-04-28 and removed on 2026-04-29 — see the Theming Manifesto for the postmortem.
No mode-pair within one theme. A theme is single-mode (typically light). Dark themes are paired presets (web_theme_dark()), not a theme.dark slot.
No variant baked into the inputs. Density, header style, first-column style are orthogonal axes. The same primary can ship as comfortable-light-default OR compact-bold-default OR any other combination.
No customer-tunable tints. accent.tint_subtle is derived; it’s not in the input list. If you need a specific tint, override the Tier 2 field directly with set_theme_field(). Don’t add it as a Tier 1 knob.
No accent on default ornament. Accent owns engagement (hover, selected, callouts). Glyph col defaults (badges, pictograms, rings, stars) read secondary, not accent — so a hovered row never reads the same color as a regular badge two rows down.

title: Styling Cascade
text:
Cascade B — runtime style application
The theme provides defaults. The runtime cascade then specializes them per row, cell, and marker.





flowchart TB
    A["Theme defaults"] --> B["Row styling"]
    B --> C["Cell styling"]
    C --> D["Marker styling"]
    D --> E["Final appearance"]

    style A fill:#e5e7eb
    style B fill:#86efac
    style C fill:#6ee7b7
    style D fill:#fbbf24
    style E fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff






Later styles override earlier ones. A cell style beats a row style, which beats a theme default.

Layer 1: Theme defaults
The theme establishes the baseline appearance for all elements:


Code
tabviz(data,
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                           scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)


The theme defaults cover:

Chrome: surfaces, content tones, dividers, accent
Data marks: per-series slot bundles, summary slot
Typography: 8 named text-role bundles
Spacing: 12 density-derived tokens

Modify any of them with the v2 modifiers:


Code
web_theme_jama() |>
  set_inputs(
    primary          = "#2563EB",
    series_anchors = c("#2563EB", "#16A34A", "#DC2626")
  ) |>
  set_variants(density = "comfortable") |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 28)




Layer 2: Row Styling
Row styling applies to entire rows, overriding theme defaults:


Code
meta_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Primary Endpoint", "  Subgroup A", "  Subgroup B", "Summary"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.72, 0.85, 0.78),
  lo = c(NA, 0.55, 0.70, 0.65),
  hi = c(NA, 0.95, 1.03, 0.93),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "summary"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                           scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold"
)













Argument
Effect




row_type
Controls row behavior (header, data, summary, spacer)


row_italic
Italic text


row_color
Literal text color


row_bg
Literal background color


row_indent
Indentation level


row_badge, row_icon
Badge / icon on the leftmost text cell


row_emphasis (semantic)
Bold + theme primary text/marker (reads theme$row$emphasis)


row_muted (semantic)
Lighter color, reduced prominence (reads theme$row$muted)


row_accent (semantic)
Bold + theme accent text/marker (reads theme$row$accent)


row_bold (semantic)
Weight bump only, no color change (reads theme$row$bold)


row_fill (semantic)
Bold + strong row-fill background (reads theme$row$fill); fg auto-contrasts



The five semantic flags (row_emphasis, row_muted, row_accent, row_bold, row_fill) each reach into the matching RowSemantic bundle on theme$row. Each bundle carries bg, fg, border, marker_fill, font_weight, font_style — so flipping row_accent = "is_primary" restyles the row text and its viz cells in lockstep. See Themes — Semantic tokens for the full bundle structure and per-token customization.


Layer 3: Cell Styling
Cell styling targets specific columns, overriding row styles:


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_pvalue("pval",
      bold  = ~ .x < 0.05,
      color = ~ ifelse(.x < 0.05, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
    ),
    col_numeric("hr",
      color = ~ ifelse(.x < 1, "#16a34a", "#dc2626")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)


Cell styling uses .x to reference the column’s own values:
col_numeric("hr", color = ~ ifelse(.x < 1, "green", "red"))


set_column_style()
Apply styling to any column after creation:


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(...)) |>
  set_column_style("hr",
    bold = ~ .x < 1,
    color = ~ case_when(
      .x < 0.8 ~ "#16a34a",
      .x < 1.0 ~ "#86efac",
      TRUE     ~ "#64748b"
    )
  )




Layer 4: Marker Styling
Marker styling controls forest plot points and lines, overriding all other layers:


Code
library(dplyr)

marker_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C", "Trial D"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.95, 0.68, 1.15),
  lo = c(0.55, 0.78, 0.52, 0.90),
  hi = c(0.94, 1.15, 0.89, 1.47),
  design = c("RCT", "RCT", "Observational", "Observational")
) |>
  mutate(
    marker_color = case_when(
      hi < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lo > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE   ~ "#64748b"
    ),
    marker_shape = ifelse(design == "RCT", "circle", "square")
  )

tabviz(marker_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                           scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  marker_color = "marker_color",
  marker_shape = "marker_shape"
)









Argument
Effect




marker_color
Fill color


marker_shape
Shape (circle, square, diamond, triangle)


marker_opacity
Transparency (0-1)


marker_size
Size multiplier

title: Styling Cascade
text:
The viz-cell color cascade
Inside any viz column (viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin), the fill color of a single glyph is resolved by walking a four-layer precedence stack. Most specific wins; NA passes through.








Layer
Source
Behavior




4. Per-row literal
tabviz(marker_color = "col" \| ~ formula)
Wins when value is non-NA. NA falls through to Layer 3.


3. Row semantic class
row_emphasis / row_muted / row_accent / row_bold / row_fill = "col" \| ~ formula
Single-effect viz: fill replaced with the token’s theme color (theme@row@<token>@marker_fill). Multi-effect viz: per-effect fill preserved, outline (stroke) added in the token color. FALSE/absent falls through to Layer 2.


2. Per-effect literal
effect_forest(color = "#hex") (and effect_bar/effect_boxplot/effect_violin)
Sets fill for that effect on all rows.


1. Theme palette
theme$series[[i]]@fill
Invisible default; effect index cycles.



Semantic class → theme field mapping (v2):



Class
Theme path




row_emphasis
theme@row@emphasis@marker_fill


row_muted
theme@row@muted@marker_fill


row_accent
theme@row@accent@marker_fill


row_bold
theme@row@bold@marker_fill


row_fill
theme@row@fill@marker_fill



The cascade applies uniformly to all four viz_* columns. The single-effect vs multi-effect distinction is automatic — based on length(effects).

Why the multi-effect rule?
In a single-effect viz, the marker color isn’t carrying any per-effect information, so replacing it with the row’s semantic color is unambiguous. In a multi-effect viz, each effect’s color carries identity (e.g. blue = ITT, green = Per-Protocol, orange = Safety). Replacing the fill would erase that identity for accented rows. The “outline augment” rule keeps the per-effect color encoding intact while still flagging the row visually.


Examples
The following examples render the cascade across each viz type:







Example
Demonstrates




gallery_17_cascade_single_forest
Single-effect forest: row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted replace marker fill


gallery_18_cascade_multi_forest
Multi-effect forest: row_accent adds outline; per-effect fills preserved

title: Styling Cascade
text:
Cascade Examples
These examples show how multiple cascade layers compose:


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_pvalue("pval",
      bold  = ~ .x < 0.05,                          # cell layer
      color = ~ ifelse(.x < 0.05, "#16a34a", NA)    # cell layer (NA inherits)
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  row_emphasis = ~ pval < 0.001,    # row layer
  theme = web_theme_jama()          # theme layer (chrome + data)
)


In this example: 1. web_theme_jama() sets all theme defaults (Tier 1 + Tier 2 + Tier 3 internally). 2. row_emphasis = ~ pval < 0.001 reads theme@row@emphasis for matching rows. 3. col_pvalue(..., bold = ~ .x < 0.05) overrides text weight on specific cells. 4. col_pvalue(..., color = ~ ...) overrides cell color where the formula returns non-NA.

title: Styling Cascade
text:
Debugging Styles
If something doesn’t look right, walk the cascade in reverse:


Code
# Inspect the resolved theme
t <- web_theme_jama()
t@row@emphasis@marker_fill   # what semantic emphasis evaluates to
t@series[[1]]@fill           # default marker fill
t@cell@fg                    # default cell color

# Debug a formula expression
debug_color <- function(x) {
  res <- ifelse(x < 0.05, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
  cat("Input:", x, "\nOutput:", res, "\n")
  res
}

col_numeric("hr", color = ~ debug_color(.x))

title: Concepts
text: The mental models that make tabviz easier to use. Read these once and the rest of the API will feel obvious.

title: Concepts
text:
Pages



Page
What you’ll learn




Data Flow
How a data.frame becomes JSON, then a Svelte widget — what column references resolve to, when formulas evaluate, the actual wire shape (rows / styles / paginate), and how grouping and pagination flow through.


Styling Cascade
The four-layer precedence model: theme → row → cell → marker. The five semantic tokens (emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill), how they map to theme paths, and how row_* semantic flags reach into viz-cell colors.


Theming Manifesto
Why themes are shaped the way they are. Two-knob principle (identity + engagement), three-axis contract, anti-patterns, the seven shipped presets, and an append-only decision log. Read this before proposing a fourth identity tier.

title: Concepts
text:
At a glance





flowchart LR
    A["data.frame"] --> B["tabviz()"]
    B --> C["WebSpec (S7)"]
    C --> D["JSON"]
    D --> E["Svelte widget"]

    style B fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff3e00,color:#fff






tabviz() is the only entry point you need. Everything else — col_*(), viz_*(), web_theme_*(), set_*() — builds the spec that tabviz() consumes.

title: Concepts
section: Two ways to configure a plot
text:
Two ways to configure a plot
You can pass everything to tabviz() directly:
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05,
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)
Or build it up with fluent modifiers:
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1))
) |>
  set_row_style(bold = ~ pval < 0.05) |>
  set_marker_style(color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")) |>
  set_theme("jama")
Both produce the same widget. Pick whichever reads better at the call site.

title: tabviz
text: tabviz turns a data frame into a publication-quality, fully interactive htmlwidget — sortable, filterable, draggable, editable — with SVG / PNG / PDF export that captures exactly what you see on screen.

title: tabviz
text:
Hero example












TipTry the interactivity




Sort — click any column header.
Filter — click the funnel icon and pick a text / numeric / categorical filter.
Reorder rows — hover a row, grab the drag handle, drop within the same group.
Reorder columns — drag column headers; columns stay within their column group.
Edit values — double-click a cell (or a column-group header) to edit inline.
Export — the download button writes SVG / PNG matching your current view exactly.

title: tabviz
text:
Selling points


Declarative columns. The columns = list(...) API mirrors what you see — column types, groups, and visualizations compose with no hidden state.


WYSIWYG export. The same SVG engine drives both the browser and headless export, so PDFs match the screen pixel-for-pixel after sort / filter / edit.


Built for science. Forest plots, multi-arm effects, p-values, intervals, journal themes (Cochrane, JAMA, Lancet, dark mode), and multi-page PDF pagination for long meta-analyses — all first-class.


Fully interactive. Sorting, filtering, drag-to-reorder, inline editing, and Shiny proxy updates ship in the box.

title: tabviz
section: Build any table in a few lines
text:
Build any table in a few lines
library(tabviz)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Demographics",
      col_text("site"),
      col_n("n")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
    )
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full()
)

text:

Row groups + column groups together








Multiple effects per row








Rich dashboards — heatmaps, progress, sparklines, badges
