title: Domain Dashboards
text: tabviz() works as well outside the journal as inside. This page collects dense, information-rich dashboards from six domains — sports analytics, customer feedback, portfolio performance, product metrics, airline ops, climate science — built with the same columns = list(...) API. Each example leans into the column types that fit the domain (rings for percentages, pictograms for counts and ratings, sparklines for trends, viz_forest for confidence intervals on any interval-shaped metric).

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Sports analytics — NBA player efficiency
Three dimensions in one row: shooting efficiency (rings — FG% / 3P% / FT%), accumulated honors (pictograms in rating mode for All-Star appearances), and a focal forest plot for Player Efficiency Rating with 95% CIs. Row groups by conference; row accent on All-Stars.


Code
data(nba_efficiency)

# Synthesize a few realistic-feeling secondary stats so we can exercise
# col_ring / col_pictogram / viz_bar on top of the per-CI forest.
set.seed(2024)
nba_dash <- nba_efficiency |>
  mutate(
    fg_pct      = round(runif(n(), 0.42, 0.58), 3),
    three_pct   = round(runif(n(), 0.30, 0.42), 3),
    ft_pct      = round(runif(n(), 0.70, 0.92), 3),
    all_star_n  = pmin(15L, sample(0:9, n(), replace = TRUE) +
                            ifelse(all_star, 1L, 0L)),
    is_mvp      = award == "MVP"
  )

tabviz(nba_dash,
  label = "player",
  group = "conference",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Player",
      col_text("team", "Team", width = 50),
      col_text("position", "Pos", width = 40)
    ),
    col_group("Volume",
      col_numeric("games", "GP", decimals = 0),
      col_numeric("ppg", "PPG", decimals = 1)
    ),
    col_group("Shooting",
      col_ring("fg_pct",    header = "FG%",
               thresholds = c(0.45, 0.52)),
      col_ring("three_pct", header = "3P%",
               thresholds = c(0.34, 0.39)),
      col_ring("ft_pct",    header = "FT%",
               thresholds = c(0.75, 0.85))
    ),
    col_pictogram("all_star_n", header = "All-Star apps",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 15,
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer"),
    viz_forest(point = "per", lower = "per_lower", upper = "per_upper",
               null_value = 15,
               axis_label = "Player Efficiency Rating (95% CI)"),
    col_badge("award", "Award",
      variants = list(
        MVP                = "success",
        `All-NBA 1st`      = "info",
        `All-NBA 2nd`      = "warning",
        `All-NBA 3rd`      = "muted",
        `Defensive POY`    = "info",
        `6th Man`          = "muted",
        `Most Improved`    = "muted",
        `Rookie of Year`   = "muted",
        `All-Star`         = "muted"
      ))
  ),
  row_accent = "all_star",       # bold + theme accent on All-Stars
  row_fill   = "is_mvp",         # strong fill on MVP row
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |>
    set_inputs(primary = "#1d428a", accent = "#c8102e"),  # NBA blue + red
  title    = "NBA Player Efficiency Dashboard",
  subtitle = "PER = 15 is league average. Rings show shooting efficiency by typical-range thresholds.",
  caption  = "All-Star rows accented; MVP row gets the row.fill token."
)












TipWhat this exercises




col_ring() with thresholds: each shooting-percentage column flips color at a typical-range cutoff (e.g. FG% green ≥ 0.52, amber 0.45–0.52, red < 0.45). No hex literals — the cascade resolves to the theme’s status palette.
col_pictogram() rating mode: max_glyphs = 15 with value_label = "trailing" shows accumulated All-Star appearances visually + numerically.
row_accent and row_fill together: the 26 All-Stars take the accent token (bold + accent color), and the single MVP row layers the fill token on top — strongest visual hierarchy reserved for the rarest case.
Custom-tinted theme via set_inputs() — NBA blue + red without rebuilding from scratch.

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Customer satisfaction survey
Likert-style satisfaction scores as rings (with auto-defaulted positive / warning / negative palette), detractor counts as pictograms, response progress as bars. Row fill flags the segment with the strongest result; row accent flags any with concerning scores.


Code
survey <- data.frame(
  segment      = c("Enterprise — strategic", "Enterprise — mid-market",
                   "SMB — paid",             "SMB — self-serve",
                   "Education",              "Public sector"),
  responses    = c(412, 287, 1820, 4560, 198, 142),
  response_rate= c(0.71, 0.58, 0.32, 0.18, 0.62, 0.49),
  csat         = c(0.87, 0.82, 0.74, 0.66, 0.89, 0.78),         # 0–1
  nps          = c( 62,  48,  31,   8,  74,  44),                # -100 to 100
  ces          = c(0.82, 0.76, 0.68, 0.55, 0.85, 0.71),
  detractors   = c(  2,   3,   8,  14,   1,   4),
  promoters    = c(  9,   8,   6,   3,  11,   7),
  trend        = I(list(
    c(78, 80, 82, 85, 87), c(72, 75, 78, 80, 82),
    c(65, 68, 70, 72, 74), c(60, 62, 64, 65, 66),
    c(82, 85, 87, 88, 89), c(70, 73, 75, 77, 78)
  )),
  is_top       = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE),     # row_fill
  is_concern   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE, FALSE)     # row_accent
)

tabviz(survey,
  label = "segment",
  columns = list(
    col_n("responses", "N"),
    col_progress("response_rate", "Response rate", max_value = 1,
                 show_label = TRUE),
    col_group("Satisfaction",
      col_ring("csat", header = "CSAT", thresholds = c(0.70, 0.85)),
      col_ring("ces",  header = "CES",  thresholds = c(0.65, 0.80))
    ),
    col_numeric("nps", "NPS", decimals = 0),
    col_pictogram("detractors", header = "Detractors",
                  glyph = "person", layout = "row"),
    col_pictogram("promoters",  header = "Promoters",
                  glyph = "person", layout = "row"),
    col_sparkline("trend", "5q trend")
  ),
  row_fill   = "is_top",
  row_accent = "is_concern",
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title    = "Customer satisfaction by segment",
  subtitle = "CSAT and CES bands set per the program's typical-range thresholds; trend on the right.",
  caption  = "SMB self-serve flagged for response-rate concern; Education leads on every metric."
)












NoteWhen to reach for what




Use col_ring() when the metric has a natural percentage / fraction shape and falls into typical “good / OK / concern” bands. The threshold-driven palette tells the story at a glance.
Use col_pictogram() when the metric is a count that benefits from visual heft (one icon per detractor, one star per appearance) — it reads faster than a bare number for small counts.
Use col_sparkline() when you need direction without precision; for axis-comparable trends across rows reach for viz_bar() or viz_forest() instead.

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Portfolio performance
Twelve-month return with a 95% CI as the focal forest column, allocation share as a ring, position counts as a pictogram, and a Sharpe-ratio bar. Row-level accent on assets that beat their benchmark; row fill on the overall portfolio summary.


Code
portfolio <- data.frame(
  asset        = c("US Large Cap", "US Small Cap", "International Dev",
                   "Emerging Mkts", "US IG Bonds",  "TIPS",
                   "Gold",         "Real Estate",   "Cash",
                   "PORTFOLIO"),
  category     = c("Equity", "Equity", "Equity", "Equity",
                   "Fixed Income", "Fixed Income",
                   "Alternative", "Alternative", "Cash",
                   "Total"),
  allocation   = c(0.32, 0.10, 0.12, 0.06, 0.22, 0.05,
                   0.04, 0.06, 0.03, 1.00),
  positions    = c(  4,    3,    5,    3,    6,    1,
                     1,    2,    0,   25L),
  return_pct   = c(0.183, 0.122, 0.094, 0.072, 0.041, 0.025,
                   0.158, 0.108, 0.043, 0.118),
  return_lo    = c(0.142, 0.062, 0.038, 0.005, 0.020, 0.008,
                   0.090, 0.058, 0.041, 0.092),
  return_hi    = c(0.224, 0.182, 0.150, 0.139, 0.062, 0.042,
                   0.226, 0.158, 0.045, 0.144),
  sharpe       = c(1.45, 0.86, 0.72, 0.41, 1.02, 0.62,
                   0.94, 0.81, NA,   1.18),
  beats_bench  = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE,
                   FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE),
  row_type     = c(rep("data", 9), "summary"),
  is_total     = c(rep(FALSE, 9), TRUE)
)

tabviz(portfolio,
  label = "asset",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("allocation", header = "Alloc",
             thresholds = c(0.05, 0.20)),     # tiny / typical / heavy
    col_pictogram("positions", header = "Positions",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 6),
    viz_forest(point = "return_pct", lower = "return_lo", upper = "return_hi",
               null_value = 0,
               axis_label = "12-month return (95% CI)"),
    col_percent("return_pct", "Return", multiply = TRUE),
    col_numeric("sharpe",     "Sharpe", decimals = 2),
    col_badge("beats_bench", "vs Benchmark",
      variants = list(`TRUE` = "success", `FALSE` = "muted"))
  ),
  row_type   = "row_type",
  row_accent = "beats_bench",     # bold + accent on outperformers
  row_fill   = "is_total",        # strong fill on overall portfolio row
  theme = web_theme_lancet(),
  title    = "Portfolio performance — trailing twelve months",
  subtitle = "Allocation share, position count, return CI, and Sharpe ratio per holding category.",
  caption  = "Outperforming holdings highlighted with the accent token; overall portfolio row uses the fill token."
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Product metrics
Five product variants tracked across revenue, growth, margin, units sold, and a 12-month trend:


Code
product_metrics <- data.frame(
  product = c("Widget Pro", "Widget Lite", "Widget Plus", "Widget Max", "Widget Mini"),
  revenue = c(2450000, 1890000, 3200000, 1560000, 980000),
  growth = c(0.12, -0.05, 0.28, 0.08, 0.15),
  margin = c(0.35, 0.42, 0.28, 0.38, 0.45),
  units_sold = c(12500, 9800, 18200, 7800, 15600),
  satisfaction = c(4.2, 3.8, 4.5, 4.1, 4.3),
  trend = I(list(
    c(180, 195, 210, 205, 225, 240, 235, 250, 260, 275, 290, 305),
    c(220, 215, 205, 210, 200, 195, 190, 185, 188, 182, 180, 178),
    c(200, 220, 240, 260, 290, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 440),
    c(120, 125, 130, 128, 135, 140, 138, 145, 150, 148, 155, 160),
    c(60, 68, 75, 82, 88, 95, 100, 108, 115, 122, 128, 135)
  ))
)

tabviz(product_metrics, label = "product",
  columns = list(
    col_currency("revenue", "Revenue", decimals = 0),
    col_percent("growth", "YoY"),
    col_percent("margin", "Margin"),
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("units_sold"),
      header = "Units Sold",
      axis_range = c(0, 20000)
    ),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12M Trend"),
    col_stars("satisfaction", "Rating", max_stars = 5)
  ),
  row_color = ~ ifelse(growth < 0, "#dc2626", "#64748b"),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Product Performance Dashboard"
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
A/B test results
Conversion lift with a 95% CI per variant, p-value, and a verdict badge. The control row is a row_type = "summary" baseline.


Code
ab_test_results <- data.frame(
  variant = c("Control", "Variant A", "Variant B", "Variant C"),
  visitors = c(50000, 48500, 51200, 47800),
  conversions = c(2500, 2910, 2816, 2629),
  rate = c(0.050, 0.060, 0.055, 0.055),
  lift = c(0, 0.20, 0.10, 0.10),
  lower = c(NA, 0.08, -0.02, -0.03),
  upper = c(NA, 0.32, 0.22, 0.23),
  pvalue = c(NA, 0.002, 0.12, 0.15),
  verdict = c("Baseline", "Winner", "Inconclusive", "Inconclusive"),
  row_type = c("summary", "data", "data", "data"),
  row_bold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(ab_test_results, label = "variant",
  columns = list(
    col_n("visitors", "Visitors", abbreviate = TRUE),
    col_n("conversions", "Conv"),
    col_percent("rate", "CVR"),
    viz_forest(
      point = "lift", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
      null_value = 0,
      axis_label = "Lift vs Control"
    ),
    col_percent("lift", "Lift"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE),
    col_badge("verdict", "Verdict",
      variants = list(
        Baseline = "info",
        Winner = "success",
        Inconclusive = "warning"
      ))
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(is.na(lower), "#64748b",
                          ifelse(lower > 0, "#16a34a", "#64748b")),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Homepage Redesign A/B Test"
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Airline performance
Carrier delays with a heatmap on on-time percentage, a progress bar for satisfaction, and a 12-month delay trend:


Code
data(airline_delays)

airline_summary <- airline_delays |>
  filter(month == "Apr") |>
  select(carrier, carrier_type, on_time_pct, delay_vs_avg, satisfaction, flights, trend)

tabviz(airline_summary, label = "carrier",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("carrier_type", "Type",
      variants = list(Legacy = "info", `Low-Cost` = "success",
                      `Ultra Low-Cost` = "warning")),
    col_n("flights", "Flights", abbreviate = TRUE),
    col_heatmap("on_time_pct", "On-Time",
      palette = c("#fee2e2", "#dcfce7"),
      min_value = 0.65, max_value = 0.95, decimals = 2, show_value = TRUE),
    col_progress("satisfaction", "Satisfaction", max_value = 5),
    col_numeric("delay_vs_avg", "Delay vs Avg", decimals = 1),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12-Month Trend")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Airline Performance — April"
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Climate — regional temperature anomalies
The same interval-estimate structure that runs clinical forest plots maps cleanly onto temperature anomalies vs. a pre-industrial baseline. Marker shape encodes confidence; marker color signals severity:


Code
data(climate_temps)

tabviz(climate_temps,
  label = "region",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_text("period", "Period"),
    col_text("certainty", "Confidence"),
    col_interval("anomaly", "lower", "upper", header = "Anomaly °C (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "anomaly", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 0, axis_label = "Temperature Anomaly (°C vs. pre-industrial)")
  ),
  marker_shape = ~ ifelse(certainty == "High", "circle", "square"),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(anomaly > 1.5, "#dc2626", ifelse(anomaly > 1.0, "#f59e0b", "#3b82f6")),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE),
  title    = "Regional Temperature Anomalies",
  subtitle = "Grouped by geographic category, shaped by confidence level",
  caption  = "Red ≥ 1.5 °C, amber 1.0–1.5 °C, blue < 1.0 °C; circle = high confidence."
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
See also

Long Meta-Analysis — pooled summaries + pagination across 70 studies.
Pictogram & Ring Deep Dive — every mode of col_pictogram() and col_ring().
Compose Your Own Brand — building the NBA / Lancet / journal themes from scratch.
Visualization Styling — the four-layer marker-color cascade across viz_* columns.
Reference → Columns — every col_* and viz_* argument.

title: Interactivity Showcase
text: Every widget below is live. Sort, filter, drag, edit — any change flows through to the SVG/PNG export at the click of the download button (WYSIWYG).

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
The whole kitchen sink
Sort, filter, column reorder, row reorder, double-click edit, hover tooltips — one plot, every feature on.


Code
data(glp1_trials)

tabviz(
  glp1_trials,
  label = "study",
  group = "group",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Study Info",
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      col_n("year", "Year"),
      col_n("n"),
      col_events("events", "n", "Events")
    ),
    viz_forest(
      point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)",
      axis_range = c(0.4, 1.5),
      axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25),
      axis_gridlines = TRUE,
      annotations = list(
        refline(0.85, label = "Pooled HR", style = "dashed", color = "#00407a")
      )
    ),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
    )
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes",
  subtitle = "All interactivity on — try everything"
)












TipHow to try each feature




Sort → click a column header. Click again for descending, again to clear.
Filter → click the funnel icon next to a header. You’ll get a text / numeric / categorical UI depending on the column.
Reorder rows → hover a row, grab the drag handle on the left, drop within the same group.
Reorder columns → hover a header, grab its drag handle. Columns inside “Study Info” stay inside “Study Info”.
Edit → double-click an editable cell (text, numeric, percent, p-value, date, interval) to edit inline, or double-click a column-group header to rename the span.
Export → download SVG or PNG; the file reflects your current state exactly.

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
Filter + sort on a dashboard
Large-ish dashboard where filters really pay off — apply any combination of filters and the export matches the filtered view.


Code
data(airline_delays)

tabviz(airline_delays, label = "carrier",
  group = "month",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Carrier",
      col_badge("carrier_type", "Type",
        variants = list(
          Legacy = "info",
          `Low-Cost` = "success",
          `Ultra Low-Cost` = "warning"
        )),
      col_n("flights", "Flights", abbreviate = TRUE)
    ),
    col_group("Performance",
      col_heatmap("on_time_pct", "On-Time",
        palette = c("#fee2e2", "#dcfce7"),
        min_value = 0.65, max_value = 0.95,
        decimals = 2, show_value = TRUE),
      col_progress("satisfaction", "Satisfaction", max_value = 5),
      col_numeric("delay_vs_avg", "Delay", decimals = 1)
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "delay_vs_avg", lower = "delay_lower", upper = "delay_upper",
               null_value = 0,
               axis_label = "Delay vs avg (min)"),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12-month trend")
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Airline Performance by Month",
  subtitle = "Try filtering by carrier_type or on_time_pct; export matches the view"
)

title: Interactivity Showcase
section: Drag to reorder within column groups
text:
Drag to reorder within column groups
Columns inside “Baseline” and “Efficacy” swap cleanly; the forest column stays where it is. Whole column groups can also be dragged among top-level siblings.


Code
set.seed(42)
n_studies <- 8
trial_data <- data.frame(
  study = paste0("Trial-", LETTERS[1:n_studies]),
  arm = rep(c("Active", "Control"), length.out = n_studies),
  n_baseline = sample(200:800, n_studies),
  age_mean = round(runif(n_studies, 55, 72), 1),
  female_pct = round(runif(n_studies, 0.35, 0.65), 2),
  response_rate = round(runif(n_studies, 0.3, 0.8), 2),
  median_duration_months = round(runif(n_studies, 8, 24), 1),
  hr = round(runif(n_studies, 0.55, 1.05), 2)
) |>
  mutate(
    lo = pmax(0.3, hr - 0.2),
    hi = pmin(1.4, hr + 0.2)
  )

tabviz(trial_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("arm", "Arm",
      variants = list(Active = "success", Control = "muted")),
    col_group("Baseline",
      col_n("n_baseline", "N"),
      col_numeric("age_mean", "Age"),
      col_percent("female_pct", "Female")
    ),
    col_group("Efficacy",
      col_percent("response_rate", "Response"),
      col_numeric("median_duration_months", "Dur (mo)", decimals = 1)
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "HR"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Reorder within column groups",
  subtitle = "Try dragging columns inside \"Baseline\" — they stay inside"
)

title: Interactivity Showcase
section: Edit forest cell values in place
text:
Edit forest cell values in place
Double-click any marker to pop up the estimate / lower / upper editor. The marker moves, and the export picks up the edit.


Code
set.seed(11)
effects <- data.frame(
  metric = c("Mortality", "MI", "Stroke", "HF Hospitalization",
             "CV Death", "All-cause Death"),
  hr = c(0.74, 0.81, 0.92, 0.68, 0.71, 0.85),
  lo = c(0.62, 0.68, 0.77, 0.54, 0.58, 0.72),
  hi = c(0.88, 0.95, 1.10, 0.85, 0.87, 0.99),
  events = c(412, 289, 174, 356, 298, 512),
  n = c(12800, 12800, 12800, 12800, 12800, 12800),
  pvalue = c(0.001, 0.02, 0.22, 0.0004, 0.002, 0.04)
)

tabviz(effects, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n", "N"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_range = c(0.4, 1.4),
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "Double-click a forest marker to edit",
  subtitle = "Estimate / Lower / Upper popover; export reflects edits"
)

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
Proxy-Driven Updates
Inside a Shiny app, tabviz_proxy() exposes a fluent verb API — add_column(), sort_rows(), filter_rows(), set_theme(), set_zoom(), and more — that mutates the live widget without triggering a full re-render. The snippet below is excerpted from inst/examples/fluent_shiny_demo.R; Shiny apps can’t execute inside Quarto, so the code is shown for reference only.


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

trial_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("EMPA-REG", "CANVAS", "DECLARE", "CREDENCE", "LEADER", "SUSTAIN-6"),
  hr    = c(0.86, 0.86, 0.93, 0.80, 0.87, 0.74),
  lower = c(0.74, 0.75, 0.84, 0.67, 0.78, 0.58),
  upper = c(0.99, 0.97, 1.03, 0.95, 0.97, 0.95),
  n     = c(7020, 10142, 17160, 4401, 9340, 3297),
  year  = c(2015, 2017, 2019, 2019, 2016, 2016)
)

ui <- fluidPage(
  fluidRow(
    column(3,
      actionButton("sort_desc",    "Sort HR desc"),
      actionButton("filter_hr",    "Filter HR < 0.9"),
      actionButton("clear_flt",    "Clear filter"),
      actionButton("theme_jama",   "Theme: JAMA"),
      actionButton("theme_lancet", "Theme: Lancet"),
      actionButton("add_col",      "Add 'year' column"),
      actionButton("rename_hr",    "Rename HR to 'Hazard'")
    ),
    column(9, tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px"))
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(trial_data, label = "study",
      columns = list(
        col_numeric("hr", "HR", decimals = 2),
        col_numeric("n",  "N"),
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   header = "HR (95% CI)",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })

  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")

  # Each verb sends a custom message to the live widget — no re-render.
  observeEvent(input$sort_desc,    sort_rows(proxy, "hr", direction = "desc"))
  observeEvent(input$filter_hr,    filter_rows(proxy, "hr", operator = "lt", value = 0.9))
  observeEvent(input$clear_flt,    clear_filters(proxy))
  observeEvent(input$theme_jama,   set_theme(proxy, "jama"))
  observeEvent(input$theme_lancet, set_theme(proxy, "lancet"))
  observeEvent(input$add_col,      add_column(proxy, col_numeric("year", "Year"), after = "n"))
  observeEvent(input$rename_hr,    update_column(proxy, "hr", header = "Hazard"))
}

shinyApp(ui, server)


What the demo shows:

Sort / filter / clear → row operations applied client-side via the proxy; the ${id}_filtered_rows input updates so the server knows which rows are visible.
Theme switch → swaps colors, typography, and spacing in place without losing scroll or edit state.
Add / rename / resize columns → structural mutations with no full render.
Zoom → live scale change (0.8, 1.0, …) on the whole widget.

The same verbs (add_column, sort_rows, set_theme, …) work on static WebSpec and htmlwidget objects too; on a proxy they dispatch a custom message, otherwise they mutate the spec in place.
To run locally:
shiny::runApp(system.file("examples/fluent_shiny_demo.R", package = "tabviz"))

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
Enable only what you need
web_interaction() takes any combination of flags. Use web_interaction_full() for everything on, web_interaction_minimal() for a read-only widget, web_interaction_publication() for no UI chrome at all, or dial it in yourself:
web_interaction(
  enable_sort          = TRUE,
  enable_filters       = TRUE,
  enable_reorder_rows  = TRUE,   # drag rows within their group
  enable_reorder_columns = FALSE,
  enable_edit          = TRUE,   # double-click to edit
  enable_select        = TRUE,
  enable_hover         = TRUE,
  enable_resize        = TRUE,
  enable_export        = TRUE
)
All reorder / edit state lives in the browser session. SVG/PNG exports capture the current view, but a page reload returns to the server-supplied data. For Shiny round-trips, the ${id}_selected and ${id}_hover inputs fire on selection/hover changes, and the tabviz-proxy message handler accepts applyFilter, clearFilter, sortBy, and toggleGroup methods.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
The v2 theme system is built so authoring a branded theme is a sequence of single-input edits — each one obviously about one thing — rather than a big monolithic web_theme_acme() wrapper to bisect when something goes wrong. This page walks the build end-to-end for a fictional brand, Acme Cardio Research, with the rendered table at every step so you can see exactly which input controls what.
Each step is one named input on web_theme() (or one set_*() call). At the end, we wrap the result as a one-shot constructor.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
The shared dataset
Every step renders the same eight-row meta-analysis so the only thing changing across the renders is the theme:


Code
acme_data <- data.frame(
  study  = c("ACME-101", "PROVE-IT", "CADENCE", "VANGUARD-2",
             "STELLAR", "ENDEAVOUR", "SUMMIT", "Pooled"),
  cohort = c("Phase III", "Phase III", "Phase III",
             "Real-world", "Real-world", "Real-world",
             "Registry",   "Pooled"),
  n      = c(2400, 1850, 3120, 980,  4200, 2180, 1640,  16370),
  hr     = c(0.78, 0.82, 0.74, 0.85, 0.79, 0.81, 0.88,  0.79),
  lower  = c(0.65, 0.71, 0.62, 0.69, 0.69, 0.70, 0.74,  0.74),
  upper  = c(0.93, 0.95, 0.88, 1.05, 0.91, 0.94, 1.05,  0.85),
  pvalue = c(0.005, 0.008, 0.0001, 0.13, 0.0002, 0.005, 0.18, NA),
  row_type = c(rep("data", 7), "summary"),
  is_pooled = c(rep(FALSE, 7), TRUE)
)

render_acme <- function(theme, title = NULL, subtitle = NULL) {
  tabviz(acme_data,
    label = "study",
    group = "cohort",
    columns = list(
      col_n("n"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1,
                 axis_label = "Hazard ratio (95% CI)"),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    row_type = "row_type",
    row_fill = "is_pooled",
    theme = theme,
    title = title %||% "Acme Cardio Research — pooled meta-analysis",
    subtitle = subtitle
  )
}

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 0 — start from web_theme_cochrane()
The package default is the cleanest, broadest-use preset; everything that follows is a delta from this baseline.


Code
render_acme(
  theme    = web_theme_cochrane(),
  subtitle = "Step 0 — package default"
)

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 1 — pick the neutral ramp
Five greys, lightest to darkest. This drives every surface tone (base, muted, raised), every divider, and every body-text color in the chrome cascade. Acme leans slightly cool with a hint of blue undertone so the chrome reads as quietly editorial.


Code
acme_t1 <- web_theme(
  inputs = list(
    neutral = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFBFE", "#EEF1F7", "#5C6675", "#1A2230")
  )
)

render_acme(acme_t1, subtitle = "Step 1 — cool-leaning neutrals")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 2 — pick the primary identity
Acme’s primary is #1E3A5F — a deep editorial navy. primary_deep auto-derives by darkening; we let it.


Code
acme_t2 <- web_theme(
  inputs = list(
    neutral = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFBFE", "#EEF1F7", "#5C6675", "#1A2230"),
    primary = "#1E3A5F"
  )
)

render_acme(acme_t2, subtitle = "Step 2 — primary navy")






Notice the marker fill, the title color, and the cell-hairline tint all picked up the new identity at once. The chrome cascade does this automatically — there’s no per-component knob to flip.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 3 — pick the accent (engagement layer)
accent is reserved for engagement: hover, selected rows, semantic row callouts (row_accent), the selection edge, and the status.info fallback. Never identity — keep it visually distinct from primary. Acme picks a warm amber to counterpoint the cool navy.


Code
acme_t3 <- web_theme(
  inputs = list(
    neutral = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFBFE", "#EEF1F7", "#5C6675", "#1A2230"),
    primary = "#1E3A5F",
    accent  = "#C8893F"          # amber engagement
  )
)

render_acme(acme_t3, subtitle = "Step 3 — amber accent")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
section: Step 4 — series anchors for multi-effect plots
text:
Step 4 — series anchors for multi-effect plots
series_anchors is the per-effect color pool for multi-effect viz columns (multi-arm forest, grouped bars, multiple boxplots per row). The first anchor also drives the pooled-effect diamond fill. Each anchor expands into a 7-field SlotBundle with derived muted / emphasized variants, so you only pin the anchor.


Code
acme_t4 <- web_theme(
  inputs = list(
    neutral        = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFBFE", "#EEF1F7", "#5C6675", "#1A2230"),
    primary        = "#1E3A5F",
    accent         = "#C8893F",
    series_anchors = c("#1E3A5F", "#C8893F", "#5A8B71", "#A4456E", "#7E5A99")
  )
)

render_acme(acme_t4, subtitle = "Step 4 — five-anchor series palette (mostly invisible here — single-effect)")






The single-effect forest in this example only consumes the first anchor; switch to a multi-effect effect_forest() list and the rest cycle in.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 5 — fonts
Acme picks an editorial serif for display copy and a humanist sans for body. font_display falls back to font_body when NA, so we name both explicitly.


Code
acme_t5 <- web_theme(
  inputs = list(
    neutral        = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFBFE", "#EEF1F7", "#5C6675", "#1A2230"),
    primary        = "#1E3A5F",
    accent         = "#C8893F",
    series_anchors = c("#1E3A5F", "#C8893F", "#5A8B71", "#A4456E", "#7E5A99"),
    font_body      = "'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, sans-serif",
    font_display   = "'IBM Plex Serif', Georgia, serif"
  ),
  web_fonts = list(
    web_font("IBM Plex Sans",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600&display=swap"),
    web_font("IBM Plex Serif",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Serif:wght@500;600&display=swap")
  )
)

render_acme(acme_t5, subtitle = "Step 5 — IBM Plex Sans + Serif via web_fonts")






The widget injects <link rel="stylesheet"> tags on mount for any web_fonts declared on the theme; SVG / PNG / PDF export embeds the @font-face rules into the SVG so the static output carries its glyphs (when a fallback is needed, the browser substitutes — see Themes guide for details).

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 6 — pick variants
Variants are the per-table toggles that don’t change identity. Acme picks comfortable density and a tint header — a primary-tinted band that sits halfway between the default light header and the maximum-chrome bold header.


Code
acme_t6 <- web_theme(
  inputs = list(
    neutral        = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFBFE", "#EEF1F7", "#5C6675", "#1A2230"),
    primary        = "#1E3A5F",
    accent         = "#C8893F",
    series_anchors = c("#1E3A5F", "#C8893F", "#5A8B71", "#A4456E", "#7E5A99"),
    font_body      = "'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, sans-serif",
    font_display   = "'IBM Plex Serif', Georgia, serif"
  ),
  variants = list(
    density       = "comfortable",
    header_style  = "tint",
    first_column_style = "default"
  ),
  web_fonts = list(
    web_font("IBM Plex Sans",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600&display=swap"),
    web_font("IBM Plex Serif",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Serif:wght@500;600&display=swap")
  )
)

render_acme(acme_t6, subtitle = "Step 6 — comfortable density, tint header")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
section: Step 7 — pin a single field with set_theme_field()
text:
Step 7 — pin a single field with set_theme_field()
Almost every theme can be expressed entirely through Tier 1 inputs. The one place you reach for set_theme_field() is when you want to override a single derived field without disturbing the rest of the cascade — e.g., turning gridlines on, forcing a specific axis tick color, or pinning the row-group L1 background to a brand-specific shade.
Here, Acme wants visible axis gridlines (default off) and a slightly stronger tick label:


Code
acme_t7 <- acme_t6 |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE) |>
  set_theme_field(c("plot", "tick_label", "weight"), 500)

render_acme(acme_t7, subtitle = "Step 7 — gridlines on, slightly stronger tick label")






These overrides survive subsequent set_inputs() calls — the resolver fills NA-valued fields and leaves explicit pins alone.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 8 — wrap as a function
Once you’re happy, wrap the whole theme in a named function for team-wide use. Anyone on your team gets a one-line theme without learning the input model.


Code
web_theme_acme <- function() {
  web_theme(
    name = "acme",
    inputs = list(
      neutral        = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFBFE", "#EEF1F7", "#5C6675", "#1A2230"),
      primary        = "#1E3A5F",
      accent         = "#C8893F",
      series_anchors = c("#1E3A5F", "#C8893F", "#5A8B71", "#A4456E", "#7E5A99"),
      font_body      = "'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, sans-serif",
      font_display   = "'IBM Plex Serif', Georgia, serif"
    ),
    variants = list(
      density            = "comfortable",
      header_style       = "tint",
      first_column_style = "default"
    ),
    web_fonts = list(
      web_font("IBM Plex Sans",
               "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600&display=swap"),
      web_font("IBM Plex Serif",
               "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Serif:wght@500;600&display=swap")
    )
  ) |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE) |>
    set_theme_field(c("plot", "tick_label", "weight"), 500)
}

# Now the whole team writes:
render_acme(web_theme_acme(), subtitle = "Step 8 — wrapped as web_theme_acme()")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
section: Putting it on a different table
text:
Putting it on a different table
Same theme, different data — column types and content shift, the brand stays consistent:


Code
sales <- data.frame(
  region   = c("Americas", "Europe", "Asia-Pac", "MENA"),
  q1       = c(2.4, 1.9, 2.1, 0.7),
  q2       = c(2.7, 2.0, 2.3, 0.8),
  q3       = c(3.1, 2.2, 2.5, 0.9),
  q4       = c(3.4, 2.4, 2.8, 1.0),
  yoy      = c(0.18, 0.11, 0.16, 0.22)
)

tabviz(sales, label = "region",
  columns = list(
    col_currency("q1", "Q1", decimals = 1),
    col_currency("q2", "Q2", decimals = 1),
    col_currency("q3", "Q3", decimals = 1),
    col_currency("q4", "Q4", decimals = 1),
    col_percent("yoy", "YoY"),
    viz_bar(effect_bar("q4"), header = "Q4 ($B)", axis_range = c(0, 4))
  ),
  theme = web_theme_acme(),
  title = "Acme Cardio — regional revenue",
  subtitle = "Same theme function, different schema."
)

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
See also

Themes — Guide — the conceptual model of inputs / variants / overrides.
Themes — Reference — every web_theme() argument and set_*() modifier.
Theming Manifesto — why the cascade is shaped this way (two-knob principle, three-axis contract, anti-patterns).
LOTR (Easter Egg) — three real shipped examples of theme functions wrapped this same way.

title: Feature Showcases
text: Spotlights for individual tabviz features. Each example highlights one feature family — column types, statistical models, distribution viz, theming, conditional styling, hierarchical splits — and combines them in the closing “Full Monty” smoke test.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Rich Column Types
A comprehensive showcase of column types — heatmaps, progress bars, currency, dates, badges, stars, sparklines, bars, ranges, and icons — using a project management dataset:


Code
project_data <- data.frame(
  task = c("Backend API", "Frontend UI", "Database Migration",
           "Auth System", "Deployment Pipeline", "Documentation"),
  status = c("Active", "Active", "Completed", "At Risk", "On Hold", "Active"),
  priority = c(5, 4, 3, 5, 2, 3),
  budget = c(85000, 62000, 41000, 55000, 28000, 15000),
  spent_pct = c(0.65, 0.42, 1.00, 0.88, 0.15, 0.30),
  health = c(88, 75, 100, 45, 60, 82),
  start_date = as.Date(c("2025-01-15", "2025-02-01", "2024-11-01",
                          "2025-03-10", "2025-04-01", "2025-03-15")),
  est_low = c(30, 20, 15, 25, 10, 8),
  est_high = c(45, 35, 20, 40, 18, 12),
  velocity = I(list(
    c(8, 10, 12, 14, 15, 18, 20, 22),
    c(5, 8, 10, 12, 10, 8, 12, 14),
    c(15, 18, 20, 22, 25, 28, 30, 32),
    c(12, 10, 8, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2),
    c(2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1, 0),
    c(3, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 15)
  )),
  flag = c("ok", "ok", "done", "blocked", "paused", "ok")
)

tabviz(project_data, label = "task",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("status", "Status",
      variants = list(
        Active = "success", Completed = "info",
        `At Risk` = "error", `On Hold` = "warning"
      )),
    col_stars("priority", "Priority", max_stars = 5),
    col_currency("budget", "Budget", decimals = 0),
    col_progress("spent_pct", "Spent", max_value = 1),
    col_heatmap("health", "Health",
      palette = c("#fee2e2", "#dcfce7"),
      min_value = 0, max_value = 100, decimals = 0),
    col_date("start_date", "Started", format = "%b %Y"),
    col_range("est_low", "est_high", header = "Est. Days"),
    col_sparkline("velocity", "Velocity"),
    col_icon("flag", "Flag",
      mapping = list(ok = "\u2714", done = "\u2605", blocked = "\u2716", paused = "\u23f8"))
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Rich Column Types Showcase"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Statistical Models
Forest plots aren’t just for hazard ratios — any point estimate with a confidence interval fits. Here we visualize regression coefficients from a linear model of fuel economy on mtcars, with null_value = 0 and a linear scale:


Code
model <- lm(mpg ~ cyl + disp + hp + wt + am, data = mtcars)
coefs <- summary(model)$coefficients
ci <- confint(model)

reg_data <- data.frame(
  term = rownames(coefs),
  estimate = coefs[, "Estimate"],
  std_error = coefs[, "Std. Error"],
  conf.low = ci[, 1],
  conf.high = ci[, 2],
  p.value = coefs[, "Pr(>|t|)"],
  row.names = NULL
) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

tabviz(reg_data,
  label = "term",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("estimate", "Estimate", decimals = 3),
    col_numeric("std_error", "SE", decimals = 3),
    viz_forest(point = "estimate", lower = "conf.low", upper = "conf.high",
               scale = "linear", null_value = 0,
               axis_label = "Coefficient (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("estimate", "conf.low", "conf.high", header = "95% CI"),
    col_pvalue("p.value", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  marker_color = ~ case_when(
    conf.high < 0 ~ "#dc2626",
    conf.low > 0 ~ "#16a34a",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Linear Regression Coefficients",
  subtitle = "lm(mpg ~ cyl + disp + hp + wt + am, data = mtcars)",
  footnote = "Red = negative effect, green = positive effect, gray = not significant at alpha = 0.05."
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Distribution Visualizations
All three visualization types — viz_bar, viz_boxplot, and viz_violin — in one combined clinical site dashboard:


Code
set.seed(654)
clinical_dashboard <- data.frame(
  site = c("Boston", "London", "Tokyo", "Sydney", "Toronto"),
  enrollment = c(245, 189, 312, 156, 198),
  completion_rate = c(0.87, 0.92, 0.78, 0.95, 0.89),
  response_times = I(list(
    rnorm(50, 5.2, 1.1),
    rnorm(50, 4.8, 0.9),
    rnorm(50, 6.1, 1.5),
    rnorm(50, 4.5, 0.8),
    rnorm(50, 5.5, 1.2)
  )),
  satisfaction = I(list(
    c(rnorm(60, 8.0, 1.0), rnorm(20, 5, 1.5)),
    rnorm(80, 8.5, 0.7),
    c(rnorm(50, 7.0, 1.5), runif(20, 3, 9)),
    rnorm(75, 9.0, 0.5),
    c(rnorm(40, 7.5, 1.2), rnorm(30, 6, 1.8))
  ))
)

tabviz(clinical_dashboard, label = "site",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("enrollment"),
      header = "Enrolled",
      axis_range = c(0, 350)
    ),
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("completion_rate"),
      header = "Completion",
      axis_range = c(0, 1)
    ),
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "response_times"),
      header = "Response Time (days)",
      show_outliers = TRUE
    ),
    viz_violin(
      effect_violin(data = "satisfaction"),
      header = "Satisfaction (1-10)",
      show_median = TRUE,
      show_quartiles = TRUE
    )
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Clinical Site Performance Dashboard"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Column Groups & Split Plots
Combine col_group() headers with split_by for faceted views with organized columns:


Code
regional_data <- data.frame(
  study = rep(paste0("Study ", 1:4), 3),
  region = rep(c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific"), each = 4),
  n = rep(c(1250, 980, 1560, 1100), 3),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.68, 0.91,
         0.78, 0.82, 0.75, 0.88,
         0.65, 0.71, 0.62, 0.79),
  lo = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.52, 0.75,
         0.62, 0.68, 0.58, 0.72,
         0.48, 0.55, 0.45, 0.62),
  hi = c(0.95, 1.03, 0.89, 1.10,
         0.98, 0.99, 0.97, 1.08,
         0.88, 0.92, 0.86, 1.01)
)

tabviz(regional_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Study Info",
      col_n("n")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    )
  ),
  split_by = "region",
  shared_axis = TRUE,
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "Treatment Effect by Region"
)

title: Feature Showcases
section: Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
text:
Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
Long meta-analyses or multi-cohort registries often need to span more rows than fit on a printed page. paginate = paginate_spec() (or one of the presets — paginate_letter(), paginate_a4(), paginate_slide()) breaks the table into pages: the HTML viewer renders one page at a time with prev/next controls and a continuous-mode toggle, and save_plot(*.pdf) emits a true multi-page PDF.


Code
set.seed(2026)
n_studies <- 72
cohorts <- c("Phase III RCT", "Real-world evidence", "Registry")
counts  <- c(24, 30, 18)

meta <- data.frame(
  study = c(
    sprintf("Trial %02d", 1:counts[1]),
    sprintf("RWE %02d",  1:counts[2]),
    sprintf("Reg %02d",  1:counts[3])
  ),
  cohort = rep(cohorts, times = counts),
  year   = sample(2010:2024, n_studies, replace = TRUE),
  n      = sample(c(120, 240, 480, 960, 1900, 3800), n_studies, replace = TRUE),
  hr     = round(rnorm(n_studies, 0.92, 0.18), 2),
  se     = round(runif(n_studies, 0.06, 0.20), 2)
)
meta$lower <- pmax(0.20, round(meta$hr - 1.96 * meta$se, 2))
meta$upper <- round(meta$hr + 1.96 * meta$se, 2)

tabviz(
  meta,
  label = "study",
  group = "cohort",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("year", "Year", decimals = 0),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               header = "HR (95% CI)", scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "GLP-1 receptor agonists — pooled cardiovascular meta-analysis",
  subtitle = "72 studies across three cohorts; paginate at 30 rows per page",
  caption = "Use the prev/next controls below the plot to walk through pages, or switch to continuous mode to scroll.",
  paginate = paginate_letter()
)






paginate cascades into every split_by subview — each subview owns its own page set, paginating independently:


Code
set.seed(404)
regions <- c("North America", "Europe", "Asia-Pacific", "Latin America")
sites_per_region <- 18
n <- length(regions) * sites_per_region

split_data <- data.frame(
  site = unlist(lapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) {
    sprintf("%s site %02d", substr(regions[i], 1, 4), seq_len(sites_per_region))
  })),
  region = rep(regions, each = sites_per_region),
  arm    = rep(c("Treatment", "Placebo"), length.out = n),
  n      = sample(c(80, 120, 240, 400), n, replace = TRUE),
  rr     = round(rnorm(n, 0.85, 0.14), 2)
)
split_data$lower <- pmax(0.30, round(split_data$rr - 0.18, 2))
split_data$upper <- round(split_data$rr + 0.18, 2)

tabviz(
  split_data,
  label = "site",
  group = "arm",
  split_by = "region",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "rr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               header = "Risk ratio (95% CI)",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Risk ratio"),
    col_interval("rr", "lower", "upper", header = "RR")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Multi-region efficacy",
  subtitle = "Each region paginates independently at 10 rows per page",
  paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 10)
)






See Export → Pagination for the full configuration surface and PDF export details.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
All Theme Variants
Compare the seven built-in themes — four journal presets and three LOTR-flavored editorial themes:

Code
sample_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("A", "B", "C"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
  lo = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
  hi = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10)
)

make_plot <- function(theme_fn, name) {
  tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
    columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                             scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
    theme = theme_fn(), title = name
  )
}

# Journal presets
make_plot(web_theme_cochrane, "Cochrane")
make_plot(web_theme_lancet,   "Lancet")
make_plot(web_theme_jama,     "JAMA")
make_plot(web_theme_dark,     "Dark")
# LOTR presets
make_plot(web_theme_dwarven,  "Dwarven")
make_plot(web_theme_elvish,   "Elvish")
make_plot(web_theme_hobbit,   "Hobbit")

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Conditional Styling
One comprehensive example showing formulas for row_bold, row_color, marker_shape, marker_color, and cell-level color/bold on columns:


Code
styling_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("RCT Alpha", "RCT Beta", "Cohort Gamma", "Cohort Delta",
            "Registry Epsilon", "RCT Zeta", "Cohort Eta"),
  design = c("RCT", "RCT", "Cohort", "Cohort", "Registry", "RCT", "Cohort"),
  hr = c(0.65, 0.78, 0.95, 1.12, 0.88, 0.72, 1.05),
  lo = c(0.50, 0.62, 0.78, 0.95, 0.72, 0.58, 0.88),
  hi = c(0.84, 0.98, 1.15, 1.32, 1.08, 0.89, 1.25),
  pval = c(0.001, 0.02, 0.15, 0.03, 0.08, 0.005, 0.22),
  quality = c(1.0, 0.9, 0.7, 0.6, 0.5, 0.95, 0.65)
)

tabviz(styling_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("design", "Design"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_numeric("hr", "HR",
      color = ~ ifelse(.x < 1, "#16a34a", "#dc2626"),
      bold = ~ .x < 0.8 | .x > 1.1
    ),
    col_pvalue("pval", "P",
      stars = TRUE,
      bold = ~ .x < 0.05
    ),
    col_badge("design", "Type")
  ),
  # Row-level styling
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.01,
  row_color = ~ case_when(
    hi < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
    lo > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  # Marker styling by study design
  marker_shape = ~ case_when(
    design == "RCT" ~ "circle",
    design == "Cohort" ~ "square",
    TRUE ~ "diamond"
  ),
  marker_color = ~ case_when(
    hi < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
    lo > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  marker_opacity = "quality",
  marker_size = ~ ifelse(design == "RCT", 1.2, 1),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Conditional Styling Showcase",
  subtitle = "Row, cell, and marker styling with formulas"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Fluent Theme Customization
Build a fully customized theme by chaining v2 modifiers off a base preset:


Code
custom_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "warm",
  inputs = list(
    neutral        = c("#FEFCE8", "#FEFCE8", "#FAF6D4", "#A0855A", "#422006"),
    primary          = "#CA8A04",
    accent         = "#CA8A04",
    series_anchors = c("#CA8A04", "#16A34A", "#DC2626", "#3B82F6", "#7C3AED"),
    font_body      = "Georgia, serif"
  ),
  variants = list(density = "spacious")
) |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 32, cell_padding_x = 12) |>
  set_theme_field(c("plot", "point_size"), 8) |>
  set_theme_field(c("plot", "line_width"), 2) |>
  set_theme_field(c("layout", "container_border_radius"), 8) |>
  set_theme_field(c("row", "banding"), "row")

theme_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("PIONEER-1", "SUMMIT-2", "HORIZON-3", "ELEVATE-4", "APEX-5"),
  n = c(2450, 1890, 3200, 1560, 2100),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.68, 0.91, 0.78),
  lo = c(0.58, 0.70, 0.52, 0.75, 0.62),
  hi = c(0.89, 1.03, 0.89, 1.10, 0.98),
  status = c("Significant", "Non-significant", "Significant",
             "Non-significant", "Significant")
)

tabviz(theme_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_badge("status", "Result",
      variants = list(
        Significant = "success",
        `Non-significant` = "warning"
      ))
  ),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(hi < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  theme = custom_theme,
  title = "Custom Warm Theme via the v2 Inputs API",
  subtitle = "web_theme(inputs = ...) + set_spacing() + set_theme_field()"
)












TipTheme modifiers (v2)



Four modifiers cover the surface: set_inputs() (Tier 1 inputs), set_variants() (per-table toggles), set_spacing() (per-token spacing), and set_theme_field(theme, path, value) (generic deep setter). See Fluent API and Themes.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Publication Templates
A single drop-in journal-style template demonstrating a row-grouped, column-grouped efficacy figure for a Lancet-style submission. To produce a JAMA / Cochrane / dark / LOTR variant, swap the theme = web_theme_lancet() line — every other element survives unchanged.


Code
lancet_data <- tibble(
  outcome = c(
    "CV death or HF hospitalization",
    "Cardiovascular death",
    "HF hospitalization",
    "All-cause mortality",
    "Change in KCCQ score"
  ),
  category = c("Primary", "Components", "Components", "Secondary", "Secondary"),
  hr = c(0.74, 0.82, 0.70, 0.88, 0.85),
  lower = c(0.66, 0.72, 0.61, 0.76, 0.74),
  upper = c(0.83, 0.94, 0.81, 1.02, 0.98),
  events_tx = c(447, 156, 291, 222, NA),
  events_ctrl = c(606, 199, 407, 253, NA),
  n_tx = c(2373, 2373, 2373, 2373, 2200),
  n_ctrl = c(2371, 2371, 2371, 2371, 2180)
)

tabviz(
  lancet_data,
  label = "outcome",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Treatment",
      col_n("events_tx", "Events"),
      col_n("n_tx", "N")
    ),
    col_group("Control",
      col_n("events_ctrl", "Events"),
      col_n("n_ctrl", "N")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_lancet(),
  title    = "Figure 3: Efficacy Outcomes",
  subtitle = "Row groups by endpoint type + column groups by treatment arm",
  caption  = "HR<1 favours treatment.",
  footnote = "Cox proportional hazards model stratified by region."
)






For a worked walkthrough of building a custom branded theme from scratch — neutrals → primary → accent → series anchors → fonts → variants → field pins → wrapped function — see Compose Your Own Brand. For a full long-table treatment with pagination and pooled summaries see Long Meta-Analysis.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Hierarchical Split Navigation
split_by with multiple variables turns the sidebar into an expandable tree (Outcome > Treatment here), letting you drill into nested subgroups without cluttering the main view.


Code
data(effect_sizes)

tabviz(
  effect_sizes,
  label = "study",
  split_by = c("outcome", "treatment"),
  columns = list(
    col_text("region", "Region"),
    col_text("phase", "Phase"),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Hierarchical Subgroup Analysis",
  subtitle = "Outcome > Treatment navigation tree",
  caption = "Expand tree nodes to drill into subgroups"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
The Full Monty
Everything at once — nested row groups, three stacked effects, sparklines, weight bars, annotations, badges, and a custom dark theme. Useful as a smoke test and as a reference for how features compose.


Code
full_monty <- tibble(
  study = c(
    "ALPHA-01", "ALPHA-02",
    "BETA-01", "BETA-02", "BETA-03",
    "GAMMA-01", "GAMMA-02"
  ),
  program = c(rep("program_a", 2), rep("program_b", 3), rep("program_c", 2)),
  phase = c("Phase_II", "Phase_II", "Phase_III", "Phase_III", "Phase_III", "Phase_II", "Phase_III"),
  # Three effects
  primary_hr = c(0.68, 0.72, 0.65, 0.71, 0.74, 0.78, 0.69),
  primary_lo = c(0.52, 0.56, 0.50, 0.55, 0.58, 0.62, 0.53),
  primary_hi = c(0.89, 0.93, 0.85, 0.92, 0.95, 0.98, 0.90),
  secondary_hr = c(0.72, 0.76, 0.69, 0.75, 0.78, 0.82, 0.73),
  secondary_lo = c(0.56, 0.60, 0.53, 0.59, 0.62, 0.66, 0.57),
  secondary_hi = c(0.93, 0.96, 0.90, 0.95, 0.98, 1.02, 0.94),
  safety_hr = c(0.85, 0.88, 0.82, 0.86, 0.89, 0.92, 0.84),
  safety_lo = c(0.68, 0.72, 0.65, 0.69, 0.72, 0.75, 0.67),
  safety_hi = c(1.06, 1.08, 1.04, 1.07, 1.10, 1.13, 1.05),
  n = c(420, 380, 1250, 980, 1100, 560, 890),
  weight = c(8, 7, 22, 18, 20, 10, 15),
  pvalue = c(0.008, 0.015, 0.001, 0.004, 0.012, 0.042, 0.003),
  trend = list(
    c(0.85, 0.78, 0.72, 0.68), c(0.88, 0.82, 0.76, 0.72),
    c(0.82, 0.75, 0.69, 0.65), c(0.86, 0.80, 0.75, 0.71),
    c(0.88, 0.82, 0.78, 0.74), c(0.92, 0.88, 0.84, 0.78),
    c(0.84, 0.78, 0.73, 0.69)
  ),
  badge = c("Lead", NA, "Pivotal", NA, NA, NA, "Fast Track")
)

# Custom theme
monty_theme <- web_theme_dark() |>
  set_inputs(primary = "#f59e0b") |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 38) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridline_style"), "dotted")

tabviz(
  full_monty,
  label = "study", group = c("program", "phase"),
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_bar("weight"),
    viz_forest(
      effects = list(
        effect_forest("primary_hr", "primary_lo", "primary_hi", label = "Primary", color = "#22c55e"),
        effect_forest("secondary_hr", "secondary_lo", "secondary_hi", label = "Secondary", color = "#3b82f6"),
        effect_forest("safety_hr", "safety_lo", "safety_hi", label = "Safety", color = "#f59e0b")
      ),
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      axis_range = c(0.4, 1.2),
      axis_label = "Hazard Ratio",
      annotations = list(
        refline(0.75, label = "Target", style = "dashed", color = "#a855f7")
      )
    ),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("primary_hr", "primary_lo", "primary_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
    ),
    col_sparkline("trend", "Trend")
  ),
  row_badge = "badge",
  theme = monty_theme,
  title = "The Full Monty",
  subtitle = "Nested groups + 3 effects + sparklines + weights + annotations + custom theme",
  caption = "Every major feature combined in one visualization",
  footnote = "Green=Primary, Blue=Secondary, Orange=Safety. Purple line=Target."
)

title: Cheatsheet
text: A one-page lookup card for tabviz. Pair this with the Reference for full argument lists.

title: Cheatsheet
text:
tabviz() core arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




data
required
Source data frame


label
NULL
Column with row labels


group
NULL
Column(s) for collapsible row groups


columns
required
List of col_*() / viz_*() specs


theme
web_theme_cochrane()
Visual theme


interaction
web_interaction()
Interactivity preset


title, subtitle, caption, footnote
NULL
Header / footer text


marker_size
NULL
Constant or column for marker scaling (e.g. "weight")



tabviz(
  data,
  label = "study",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_text("category"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

All accept column names or formula expressions: row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05. The five semantic flags map to bundles on theme$row so they restyle automatically when you swap themes.

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Theme modifiers (fluent)
tabviz(...,
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |>
    set_inputs(
      primary          = "#2563EB",
      accent         = "#1E40AF",
      series_anchors = c("#2563EB", "#16A34A", "#DC2626"),
      font_body      = "Georgia, serif"
    ) |>
    set_variants(density = "comfortable", header_style = "bold") |>
    set_spacing(row_height = 28, header_height = 32) |>
    set_theme_field(c("plot", "point_size"), 6) |>
    set_theme_field(c("plot", "line_width"), 1.5) |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
)







Modifier
What it edits




set_inputs()
Tier 1 inputs (primary / secondary / accent + their _deep companions, status_*, series_anchors, slot_style, fonts)


set_variants()
Per-table toggles (density, header_style light/tint/bold, first_column_style)


set_spacing()
Per-token spacing override on top of the density preset


set_theme_field(theme, path, value)
Generic deep setter; path walks the theme tree


set_theme()
Swap to a different preset (any of the 7 by name) or a WebTheme object




Build a custom theme from scratch
web_theme(
  name = "my-brand",
  inputs = list(
    neutral        = c("#FFF","#FAFAFA","#EEE","#888","#222"),
    primary          = "#1F3A5F",
    accent         = "#B08938",
    series_anchors = c("#1F3A5F", "#B08938", "#6B8E7F"),
    font_body      = "Charter, Georgia, serif"
  ),
  variants = list(density = "comfortable", header_style = "bold")
)

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Formula expressions (NSE)
Row / marker styling — reference any column:
row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05
marker_color = ~ dplyr::case_when(
  upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
  lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
  TRUE      ~ "#64748b"
)
Cell styling — use .x for the column’s own value:
col_pvalue("pvalue", bold = ~ .x < 0.05)

Distribution visualizations

Annotations

  refline(0.8, label = "Threshold", style = "dashed", color = "#e11d48")

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Split plots
# Single variable split
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(...)),
       split_by = "region")

# Hierarchical split with shared axis
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(...)),
       split_by = c("region", "country"),
       shared_axis = TRUE)

# Identical column widths across sub-plots (v0.16+)
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(...)),
       split_by = "region",
       shared_column_widths = TRUE)

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Exporting
p <- tabviz(data, ...)
save_plot(p, "plot.svg")              # Vector
save_plot(p, "plot.png", scale = 2)   # Raster (2x resolution)
save_plot(p, "plot.pdf")              # Print

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Pagination
Break long tables across multiple pages. HTML viewer renders one page at a time with prev/next; save_plot(*.pdf) emits a true multi-page PDF.
# Defaults (30 rows / page, group integrity, orphan_min = 3)
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = TRUE)

# Rows-per-page shorthand
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = 50)

# Format presets
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_letter())
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_a4(orient = "landscape"))
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_slide())

# Full control
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 25, orphan_min = 5,
                                           break_on = "split"))

# Override per render (keep the spec, change for one save)
save_plot(p, "out.pdf", paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 50))

# Flatten a paginated spec for a PNG without a warning
save_plot(p, "out.png", paginate = NULL)

# Fluent modifier — set / clear pagination on an existing spec
spec |> paginate(rows = 30)
spec |> paginate(NULL)              # turn off
Multi-page PDF merges per-page PDFs via qpdf::pdf_combine() (in Suggests). Missing qpdf falls back to a numbered series with a warning.

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Quick patterns
# Log scale for ratios
viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
           scale = "log", null_value = 1)

# Linear scale for differences
viz_forest(point = "md", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
           scale = "linear", null_value = 0)

# Significant rows in bold
row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05

# Color markers by direction
marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8")

# Scale marker size by sample size
tabviz(data, ..., marker_size = "n")

# Column groups
col_group("Treatment", col_n("n_tx"), col_events("ev_tx", "n_tx"))

# Hierarchical grouping
group = c("region", "country")

# Interval column (always explicit)
col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)", separator = " ")
