title: tabviz()
text: tabviz() is the main entry point for building interactive table visualizations. It returns an htmlwidget that renders in R Markdown, Quarto, Shiny, or the RStudio Viewer. Use tabviz() whenever you want full control over which columns appear (text, numeric, badges, forest plots, sparklines, etc.). For a quick forest-only plot driven by point / lower / upper arguments, see forest_plot().

title: tabviz()
text:
Signature
tabviz(
  data,
  label = NULL,
  label_header = "Study",
  group = NULL,
  columns = NULL,
  extra_columns = NULL,
  available_exclude = NULL,
  title = NULL, subtitle = NULL, caption = NULL, footnote = NULL,
  # Row styling (column name or formula)
  row_bold = NULL, row_italic = NULL,
  row_color = NULL, row_bg = NULL,
  row_badge = NULL, row_icon = NULL,
  row_indent = NULL, row_type = NULL,
  row_emphasis = NULL, row_muted = NULL, row_accent = NULL,
  # Marker styling (column name or formula)
  marker_color = NULL, marker_shape = NULL,
  marker_opacity = NULL, marker_size = NULL,
  # Theme & interaction
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  interaction = NULL,
  plot_position = NULL,
  row_height = NULL,
  banding = NULL,
  width = NULL, height = NULL, elementId = NULL,
  # Splitting
  split_by = NULL, shared_axis = FALSE, shared_column_widths = FALSE,
  # Pagination
  paginate = NULL,
  .spec_only = FALSE
)

title: tabviz()
text:
Arguments

Data



Argument
Type
Default
Description




data
data.frame
required
A data.frame, data.table, or tibble.


label
character(1)
NULL
Column name for row labels. When NULL, row numbers are used.


label_header
character(1)
"Study"
Header text for the label column. The literal default "Study" is auto-prettified from the label field name when applicable.


group
character / list
NULL
Grouping spec. A single column name (flat), a vector (hierarchical), or a list of web_group() objects. See Grouping.


columns
list
NULL
List of column specs from col_*() / viz_*() helpers.


extra_columns
list
NULL
Pre-configured columns hidden by default but available in the interactive column picker.


available_exclude
character
NULL
Data columns to omit from the column picker (e.g. internal/sensitive fields).





Titles









Argument
Type
Default
Description




title
character(1)
NULL
Main title (above the plot).


subtitle
character(1)
NULL
Subtitle (below title).


caption
character(1)
NULL
Caption (below plot).


footnote
character(1)
NULL
Footnote (below caption, italicized).





Row styling
All row-styling arguments accept either a column name (string) that holds the per-row value, or a formula evaluated against data (e.g. ~ pvalue < 0.05).









Argument
Value type
Default
Description




row_bold
logical
NULL
Bold whole row.


row_italic
logical
NULL
Italicize row.


row_color
CSS color
NULL
Row text color.


row_bg
CSS color
NULL
Row background color.


row_badge
character
NULL
Badge text on the label cell.


row_icon
emoji/unicode
NULL
Icon on the label cell.


row_indent
numeric
NULL
Indentation level.


row_type
character
NULL
One of "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".


row_emphasis
logical
NULL
Bold + theme primary color.


row_muted
logical
NULL
Lighter, reduced prominence.


row_accent
logical
NULL
Bold + theme accent color (most common).


row_fill
logical
NULL
Bold + a strong row fill (auto-contrasting fg).





Marker styling
Same column-name-or-formula pattern as row styling:









Argument
Value type
Default
Description




marker_color
CSS color
NULL
Marker fill color.


marker_shape
character
NULL
One of "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle".


marker_opacity
numeric [0,1]
NULL
Marker opacity.


marker_size
numeric
NULL
Driver for marker size — pass raw weights (e.g. study weights 0–100). The renderer normalizes via 0.5 + sqrt(w/100) * 1.5.





Theme, interaction, layout









Argument
Type
Default
Description




theme
WebTheme
web_theme_cochrane()
Theme object (use a web_theme_*() builder).


interaction
InteractionSpec
NULL
Use web_interaction(). When NULL, default_interaction_for_theme(theme) is used (full interactivity).


plot_position
character(1)
NULL
 No effect. Column order in columns = list(...) controls placement.


row_height
numeric(1)
NULL
Row height in pixels.


banding
character(1)
NULL
Row banding mode: "none", "row", "group" (deepest level, default), or "group-n" (e.g. "group-1" for outermost). Overrides the banding on theme. Use the viewer’s settings panel (gear icon) to adjust interactively.


width
character/numeric
NULL
Widget width (auto).


height
character/numeric
NULL
Widget height (auto).


elementId
character(1)
NULL
HTML element id.





Splitting









Argument
Type
Default
Description




split_by
character
NULL
Column name(s) to split into one plot per unique value/combination, returning a SplitForest widget.


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
When split_by is set, share an axis range across all sub-plots.


shared_column_widths
logical(1)
FALSE
When split_by is set, stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot so columns line up when screenshots are stacked. Added in v0.16.





Pagination



Argument
Type
Default
Description




paginate
PaginateSpec / shortcut
NULL
Break a long table across multiple pages. Accepts NULL (off), TRUE (default paginate_spec()), a positive integer (rows-per-page shorthand), or a PaginateSpec from paginate_spec() / paginate_letter() / paginate_a4() / paginate_slide(). Cascades into every split_by subview. See Export → Pagination.





Other









Argument
Type
Default
Description




.spec_only
logical(1)
FALSE
When TRUE, returns the WebSpec object instead of an htmlwidget — useful for programmatic manipulation before rendering.

title: tabviz()
text:
Deprecated arguments






ImportantRemoved from the tabviz() surface in 0.9.0



The arguments below are still accepted for one cycle but emit lifecycle::deprecate_warn(). They have moved to dedicated, more discoverable homes.





Old argument
Replacement




tabviz(axis_range = ...)
Pass axis_range to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(axis_ticks = ...)
Pass axis_ticks to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(axis_gridlines = ...)
Pass axis_gridlines to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(zoom = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(zoom = ...).


tabviz(auto_fit = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(auto_fit = ...).


tabviz(max_width = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(max_width = ...).


tabviz(max_height = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(max_height = ...).


tabviz(show_zoom_controls = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(show_controls = ...).


tabviz(weight = ...)
Use marker_size = ....

title: tabviz()
text:
Return value
An htmlwidget. When .spec_only = TRUE, returns a WebSpec instead. When split_by is set, returns a split-table widget backed by a SplitForest.

title: tabviz()
text:
Grouping
Three modes of group:
# Flat grouping
tabviz(data, group = "category", ...)

# Hierarchical grouping
tabviz(data, group = c("region", "country"), ...)

# Explicit group definitions
tabviz(data,
  group = list(
    web_group("cardio", label = "Cardiovascular"),
    web_group("renal",  label = "Renal Outcomes", collapsed = TRUE)
  ),
  ...)

title: tabviz()
text:
Examples


Code
meta <- data.frame(
  study = c("Smith 2020", "Jones 2021", "Lee 2022"),
  drug  = c("A", "A", "B"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
  lo    = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
  hi    = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10),
  n     = c(245, 180, 320)
)

# Minimal: forest column + a couple of data columns
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)








Code
# Row styling: bold significant rows, color the favourable ones green
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  row_bold  = ~ hi < 1,
  row_color = ~ ifelse(hi < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
)








Code
# Spec-only: build, mutate fluently, then render
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")),
  .spec_only = TRUE
) |>
  set_theme("jama") |>
  set_marker_style(color = "drug") |>
  forest_plot()

title: tabviz()
text:
Related

forest_plot() — convenience wrapper around tabviz() + viz_forest()
Columns — every col_*() and viz_*() helper
Styling modifiers — set_row_style(), set_marker_style(), set_zoom()
Themes — web_theme_*() builders and set_*() modifiers
Interaction — web_interaction()
Export — save_plot() for SVG / PNG / PDF, plus pagination for long tables

title: Function Reference
text: Complete reference for the tabviz public API. Functions are grouped by purpose; each name links to its detail page.

title: Function Reference
text:
Entry points



Function
Description




tabviz()
Main entry point — interactive table with any mix of columns.


forest_plot()
Convenience wrapper that auto-builds a forest column from point / lower / upper (or effects).


web_spec()
Alias of tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE) — return a WebSpec instead of a widget.


split_table()
Build a SplitForest from a spec / widget / data frame.



# Build a table with a forest plot column
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"),
  col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
  col_pvalue("pval")
))

# Quick forest plot
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi", label = "study")

title: Function Reference
text:
Troubleshooting
Troubleshooting guide — common errors and recipes.

title: save_plot()
text:
Export a tabviz widget, WebSpec, or SplitForest to a static SVG, PDF, or PNG.
The pipeline runs entirely in R: the spec is serialized to JSON, the bundled JavaScript SVG generator (inst/js/svg-generator.js) is run via V8, and PDF/PNG outputs are produced from that SVG via rsvg.

title: save_plot()
text:
save_plot()
save_plot(x, file,
          width = NULL, height = NULL, ratio = NULL,
          flex = TRUE, scale = 2,
          which = NULL, paginate = NULL,
          ...)



Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / SplitForest
required
The plot to export. SplitForest is dispatched to save_split_table() unless which is set.


file
character(1)
required
Output file path (or directory for SplitForest without which). Extension determines format: .svg, .pdf, or .png.


width
numeric(1) / NULL
NULL
Display width in pixels. NULL preserves natural. With height or ratio, triggers Mode 3 relayout.


height
numeric(1) / NULL
NULL
Display height in pixels. NULL preserves natural. With width or ratio, triggers Mode 3 relayout.


ratio
numeric(1) / NULL
NULL
Target aspect ratio (width / height). When set with one of width / height, the other is computed. When set alone, target width = natural width and target height = natural width / ratio. Falls back to the spec’s target_aspect (set via set_aspect_ratio()) when NULL. Mutually exclusive with passing both width and height.


flex
logical / numeric(1)
TRUE
Lever-ladder cap for flex-column absorption. TRUE = cap of 2 (each flex column may grow / shrink within [0.5×, 2×] of natural). FALSE = disable flex absorption (cap of 1; aspect change goes entirely through row-height + non-flex column scaling). A numeric N >= 1 sets a custom cap (e.g. 1.5 is conservative; Inf removes the cap).


scale
numeric(1)
2
Raster fidelity multiplier for PNG output (2 = retina). PNG-only: passing scale to a .svg or .pdf save warns.


which
string / integer / NULL
NULL
For SplitForest only. NULL (default) writes every subview into a directory; pass a key string ("Male__Young") or 1-based integer index to extract a single subview to file and demote the path to a single-file write.


paginate
PaginateSpec / shortcut / NULL
NULL
Override the spec’s pagination at the call site. Accepts NULL (off), TRUE, a positive integer, or a PaginateSpec from paginate_spec(). When set on a .pdf save, emits a multi-page PDF; on a .png / .svg save, warns and flattens (use paginate = NULL to silence). See Pagination in the Export guide.


...
—
—
Reserved for future format-specific options.



Returns: the output file path, invisibly. For paginated PDF output without qpdf, returns the character vector of numbered-series paths.

Sizing modes










width
height
ratio
Mode
Behavior




—
—
—
Natural
Render at the spec’s natural dimensions.


set
—
—
Display-scaled
Render at natural; SVG root width = requested. Height computed from natural aspect.


—
set
—
Display-scaled
Render at natural; SVG root height = requested. Width computed from natural aspect.


set
set
—
Aspect-changed
Relayout: target aspect = width / height. Flex columns absorb width delta (capped at flex); row heights scale for height delta.


set
—
set
Aspect-changed
Target height = width / ratio.


—
set
set
Aspect-changed
Target width = height * ratio.


—
—
set
Aspect-changed
Target width = natural; target height = natural width / ratio.


set
set
set
Error
Over-specified — pick at most two.



The flex argument controls what happens only in Aspect-changed mode: it caps how far each flex-eligible column may stretch before the layout falls back to scaling non-flex columns and row heights. Default-natural and display-scaled renders are unaffected by flex.


Dependencies

SVG: V8 (always required for the generator).
PDF / PNG: also requires the rsvg package.
Multi-page PDF (paginated): also requires the qpdf package, used to merge per-page PDFs into one. If qpdf is not installed, save_plot() falls back to writing a numbered series (out_p01.pdf, out_p02.pdf, …) and emits a warning.

There is no headless-browser dependency.


Resolution guide for PNG
scale is a raster-fidelity multiplier — it grows the PNG’s pixel count without changing the layout’s logical size. Vector formats (SVG / PDF) ignore scale; pass width to make a vector output denser instead.



scale
Effective DPI
Use case




1
~72
Quick preview


2
~144
Standard web / screen (default)


3
~216
Print quality


4
~288
High-resolution print





Examples
p <- forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi", label = "study")

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg")                          # natural
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", width = 1200)            # display-scaled
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", ratio = 2)               # 2:1 wide
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", width = 1200, height = 600)  # explicit aspect
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", ratio = 2, flex = FALSE)     # no flex absorption

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.png", width = 1200, scale = 3)     # 3600 px PNG

# Long table — multi-page PDF (requires qpdf in Suggests)
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.pdf", paginate = paginate_letter())

# Force a denser pagination on this render only
save_plot(p, "figures/dense.pdf",
          paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 50))

# Flatten a paginated spec for a single-image PNG without warning
save_plot(p, "figures/single.png", paginate = NULL)

# SplitForest — extract one subview to a single file
save_plot(split_p, "figures/male.svg", which = "Male")

title: save_plot()
text:
set_aspect_ratio()
Pin an aspect ratio on the spec itself, so every render — save_plot(), the htmlwidget, the interactive aspect slider — picks it up by default. Backed by two WebSpec fields:

target_aspect — the numeric ratio (width / height), or NA_real_ for natural.
target_aspect_anchor — "width" / "height" / "auto", controls how target dims fall out of ratio.

save_plot(..., ratio = X, anchor = ...) overrides the spec-level values for one render only.
set_aspect_ratio(x, ratio, anchor = c("width", "height", "auto"))








Argument
Type
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / tabviz_proxy
The spec or live widget to modify.


ratio
numeric(1) / NULL
Target aspect ratio (width / height). NULL clears the target — equivalent to “render at natural aspect”.


anchor
choice
Resolution rule when only ratio is given. "width" (default): target width = natural width, target height = natural width / ratio. "height": anchors height instead — wide ratios grow output width rather than shrinking row height. "auto": picks based on whether the requested ratio is wider or taller than the spec’s natural aspect — readability-first; recommended.



# 2:1 wide, with row height preserved (output grows wider)
spec |> set_aspect_ratio(2, anchor = "auto")
The in-widget aspect-ratio slider (in the toolbar’s zoom dropdown) drives the same fields live; its “Readable rows” toggle flips the anchor between "width" and "auto". See Interactivity for the full surface.

title: save_plot()
text:
tabviz_natural_dimensions()
Compute a spec’s natural width / height / aspect without rendering — useful for sizing Quarto / RMarkdown chunks.
tabviz_natural_dimensions(x)
Returns a named list with three numeric scalars: width, height, aspect (= width / height). Accepts a WebSpec or an htmlwidget produced by tabviz() / forest_plot().
spec <- tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")))
dims <- tabviz_natural_dimensions(spec)

#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: !expr 8 / dims$aspect

title: save_plot()
text:
save_split_table()
Export every sub-plot in a SplitForest to a directory tree mirroring the split hierarchy. Each subview’s aspect contract is independent — width / height / ratio / flex apply per-subview.
save_split_table(x, path,
                 format = c("svg", "pdf", "png"),
                 width = NULL, height = NULL, ratio = NULL,
                 flex = TRUE, scale = 2,
                 paginate = NULL,
                 ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
SplitForest / htmlwidget
required
A split-forest container or a widget produced with split_by.


path
character(1)
required
Output directory; created if it doesn’t exist.


format
choice
"svg"
"svg", "pdf", or "png".


width / height / ratio / flex / scale
—
as in save_plot()
Forwarded to save_plot() for each sub-plot.


paginate
PaginateSpec / shortcut / NULL
NULL
Override the per-spec pagination at the call site. Cascades into every subview. With format = "pdf", each split leaf becomes one combined multi-page PDF (e.g. path/Male.pdf containing all of Male’s pages).


...
—
—
Reserved for future format-specific options.



Returns: a character vector of exported file paths, invisibly.

Output directory structure







Split
Files




split_by = "region"
path/North.svg, path/South.svg, …


split_by = c("region", "age")
path/North/North_Young.svg, path/North/North_Old.svg, …





Example
data |>
  tabviz(label = "study",
         columns = list(viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper"))) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group")) |>
  save_split_table("output/plots", format = "svg")
save_plot() itself accepts a SplitForest and dispatches to save_split_table() automatically — pass either a directory path or a path with an extension to choose the format.

title: save_plot()
text:
Related

Export overview — picking a format
split_table() — produce the SplitForest accepted here
Themes — print-oriented presets (web_theme_jama(), web_theme_lancet(), web_theme_cochrane())
web_interaction_publication() — quiet UI before exporting screenshots

title: Shiny Integration
text: Functions for embedding tabviz plots in Shiny applications and updating them in place from server code via proxies. The proxy pattern lets every fluent verb dispatch transparently — the same sort_rows() call works on a WebSpec, an htmlwidget, or a live tabviz_proxy.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Output and Render

tabvizOutput()
Create a Shiny output element for a tabviz plot.
tabvizOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "400px")









Argument
Type
Default
Description




outputId
character(1)
required
Output variable name.


width
character/numeric
"100%"
Widget width (CSS units).


height
character/numeric
"400px"
Widget height (CSS units).





renderTabviz()
renderTabviz(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




expr
expression
required
Returns a widget from tabviz().


env
environment
parent.frame()
Evaluation environment.


quoted
logical(1)
FALSE
Whether expr is already quoted.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Proxy Updates

tabviz_proxy()
Create a proxy to update a running tabviz widget without re-rendering it. Pass the proxy to any fluent verb (sort_rows(), filter_rows(), add_column(), set_theme(), …) to dispatch an update message to the browser.
tabviz_proxy(id, session = shiny::getDefaultReactiveDomain())









Argument
Type
Default
Description




id
character(1)
required
Widget (output) id.


session
Shiny session
getDefaultReactiveDomain()
Current reactive session.



Returns a tabviz_proxy object. Always create proxies inside a reactive context (observe, observeEvent, …), never at the top of server.


Verbs that accept a proxy
All fluent verbs dispatch on input type: static WebSpec / htmlwidget objects are mutated in R; tabviz_proxy objects send a custom message to the browser. See the full list in Styling and the Fluent API guide.







Category
Verbs




Columns
add_column(), remove_column(), move_column(), resize_column(), update_column()


Rows
sort_rows(), filter_rows(), clear_filters(), select_rows(), move_row()


Data
update_data() (proxy requires a WebSpec)


Groups
toggle_group()


Cells
set_cell(), set_row_label(), clear_edits() (proxy-only)


Global
set_theme(), set_zoom(), paginate(), styling set_* verbs


Labels & watermark
set_title(), set_subtitle(), set_caption(), set_footnote(), set_watermark()


Painter
paint_row(), paint_cell() (apply a semantic token)


Split
set_shared_column_widths() (align column widths across sub-plots)

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Reading widget state
Tabviz widgets emit per-dimension Shiny inputs as users interact (sort, filter, drag, edit, paginate, …). Two helpers wrap the boilerplate of pulling them all together:

tabviz_state()
Returns a named list of every observability dimension the widget exposes. The envelope (source, ts) is stripped — values only.
tabviz_state(input, id)








Argument
Type
Description




input
Shiny input object
Passed through from server


id
character(1)
Widget output id (the same one passed to tabvizOutput())



observe({
  state <- tabviz_state(input, "tbl")
  # state$sort, state$filters, state$collapsed_groups, ...
})


tabviz_state_envelope()
Like tabviz_state() but preserves each dimension’s envelope, so callers see source ("user" / "proxy") and ts alongside the value. Use this to ignore proxy-originated mutations and avoid feedback loops:
observe({
  sort <- input[["tbl_sort"]]
  req(sort, sort$source == "user")
  # react only to user-driven sort changes
})

# Or pull all dimensions at once:
env <- tabviz_state_envelope(input, "tbl")
env$sort$value      # the value
env$sort$source     # "user" or "proxy"
env$sort$ts         # timestamp

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Split-Table Bindings

splitTabvizOutput()
splitTabvizOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "600px")
Same arguments as tabvizOutput() with a taller default height suited to the split-view’s sidebar layout.


renderSplitTabviz()
renderSplitTabviz(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)
expr should return a widget created from a split_table() result (or a forest_plot() / tabviz() call with split_by).


split_tabviz_proxy()
split_tabviz_proxy(id, session = shiny::getDefaultReactiveDomain())
Returns a split_tabviz_proxy object.


split_tabviz_select()
Select a sub-plot in a split-table tabviz.
split_tabviz_select(proxy, key)








Argument
Type
Description




proxy
split_tabviz_proxy
From split_tabviz_proxy().


key
character(1)
Sub-plot key (e.g. "Americas" or "Male__Young").

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Examples

Basic Shiny app


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Forest Plot Demo"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput("theme", "Theme:",
        choices = c("Default", "JAMA", "Lancet", "Modern")
      )
    ),
    mainPanel(
      tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  data <- data.frame(
    study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C"),
    hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
    lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
    upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10)
  )

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    theme <- switch(input$theme,
      "Default" = web_theme_cochrane(),
      "JAMA"    = web_theme_jama(),
      "Lancet"  = web_theme_lancet(),
      "Modern"  = web_theme_cochrane()
    )

    tabviz(data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      ),
      theme = theme
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)




Proxy updates (no re-render)


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tabvizOutput("plot"),
  actionButton("filter_btn", "Filter HR < 1"),
  actionButton("clear_btn",  "Clear Filter"),
  actionButton("sort_btn",   "Sort HR desc")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  data <- data.frame(
    study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
    hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 1.15, 0.91),
    lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.90, 0.75),
    upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.45, 1.10)
  )

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })

  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")

  observeEvent(input$filter_btn, filter_rows(proxy, "hr", "lt", 1))
  observeEvent(input$clear_btn,  clear_filters(proxy))
  observeEvent(input$sort_btn,   sort_rows(proxy, "hr", "desc"))
}




Replacing the dataset
update_data() on a proxy requires a WebSpec (not a raw data frame) so the new columns/types can be serialized. Build one with tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE).


Code
observeEvent(input$switch_dataset, {
  new_spec <- tabviz(
    get_dataset(input$dataset_name),
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ),
    .spec_only = TRUE
  )
  update_data(tabviz_proxy("plot"), new_spec)
})

title: Shiny Integration
text:
See Also

Shiny guide — reactive patterns and a complete example.
Fluent API guide — full verb list and static/proxy dispatch.
tabviz() — main entry point.

title: forest_plot()
text: forest_plot() is a convenience wrapper around tabviz() that auto-builds a forest column from point / lower / upper (or an effects list) and forwards everything else to tabviz(). Use it for quick forest plots; reach for tabviz() directly when you want full control over column ordering, multiple viz columns, or column groups.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Signature
forest_plot(
  x,
  point = NULL, lower = NULL, upper = NULL,
  effects = NULL,
  label = NULL,
  group = NULL,
  columns = NULL,
  scale = NULL,            # "linear" or "log"
  null_value = NULL,       # 0 (linear) or 1 (log) by default
  axis_label = NULL,
  axis_range = NULL,
  axis_ticks = NULL,
  axis_gridlines = NULL,
  theme = NULL,
  ...,                     # forwarded to tabviz()
  split_by = NULL,
  shared_axis = FALSE,
  width = NULL, height = NULL, elementId = NULL
)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Arguments



Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
data.frame / WebSpec / SplitForest
required
Input. A data frame builds a fresh widget; a WebSpec is rendered directly; a SplitForest is rendered as a split widget.


point
character(1)
NULL
Column name for point estimates. Required when x is a data frame and effects is not provided.


lower
character(1)
NULL
Lower-bound column. Required with point / upper.


upper
character(1)
NULL
Upper-bound column.


effects
list of effect_forest()
NULL
Multi-effect spec; when provided, point / lower / upper are ignored.


label
character(1)
NULL
Column name for row labels.


group
character / list
NULL
Same shape as tabviz(group=).


columns
list
NULL
Extra col_*() / viz_*() columns added alongside the auto-built forest column.


scale
character(1)
"linear"
"linear" or "log".


null_value
numeric(1)
0 (linear) / 1 (log)
Reference value for the null line.


axis_label
character(1)
"Effect"
X-axis label.


axis_range
numeric(2)
NULL
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


axis_ticks
numeric
NULL
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


axis_gridlines
logical(1)
FALSE
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


theme
WebTheme
NULL
Theme; NULL falls through to tabviz()’s default.


...
—
—
Forwarded to tabviz(). All tabviz() row/marker styling, titles, and interactivity arguments are accepted.


split_by
character
NULL
Splits the result into a SplitForest.


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
Share axis range across split sub-plots.


width, height, elementId
—
NULL
Standard htmlwidget passthroughs.









Noteaxis_range / axis_ticks / axis_gridlines are native here



In tabviz(), these arguments were deprecated in 0.9.0 and now live on viz_forest(). forest_plot() keeps them as native arguments and forwards them into the auto-built forest column — that’s the whole point of the wrapper.








TipPagination flows through



Pass paginate = paginate_letter() (or any other pagination shortcut) to break a long table across multiple PDF pages — forest_plot() forwards paginate to tabviz() via .... See the Export → Pagination guide for the full surface.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Return value
An htmlwidget. When split_by is set or x is a SplitForest, returns the split-table widget.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Examples


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91, 0.68),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75, 0.52),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10, 0.89)
)

# Quick forest from a data frame
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study")








Code
# Log scale for ratios (HR / OR / RR)
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")








Code
# Add data columns alongside the auto-built forest column
data$n <- c(1250, 980, 1560, 1100)

forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1,
            columns = list(
              col_n("n"),
              col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
            ))








Code
# Multi-effect plot (e.g. ITT vs Per-Protocol)
multi <- data.frame(
  study  = c("TRIAL-1", "TRIAL-2", "TRIAL-3"),
  itt_hr = c(0.78, 0.82, 0.74),
  itt_lo = c(0.65, 0.68, 0.61),
  itt_hi = c(0.94, 0.99, 0.90),
  pp_hr  = c(0.72, 0.78, 0.70),
  pp_lo  = c(0.58, 0.63, 0.56),
  pp_hi  = c(0.89, 0.97, 0.88)
)

forest_plot(multi,
  label = "study",
  effects = list(
    effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", label = "ITT",          color = "#2563eb"),
    effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",  label = "Per-Protocol", color = "#16a34a")
  ),
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
)








Code
# Split view with shared axis
regional <- data.frame(
  study  = rep(c("Study 1", "Study 2", "Study 3"), 2),
  region = rep(c("North America", "Europe"), each = 3),
  hr     = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.78, 0.68, 0.82, 0.75),
  lo     = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.62, 0.52, 0.68, 0.58),
  hi     = c(0.95, 1.03, 0.98, 0.89, 0.99, 0.97)
)

forest_plot(regional, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1,
            split_by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Equivalence with tabviz()
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
            label = "study", scale = "log", null_value = 1)

# Equivalent to:
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Related

tabviz() — full control over columns and layout
viz_forest() — the underlying forest column helper
effect_forest() — define one effect for multi-effect plots
split_table() — build a SplitForest from a spec or widget

title: S7 Class Architecture
text: A reference catalog of every S7 class in tabviz, with the slot names you’ll actually see if you peek inside a WebSpec. Read this when you need to extend the package, debug serialization, or understand why a particular argument resolves the way it does.

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Overview
tabviz uses S7, R’s modern object-oriented system, for its internal data structures. S7 provides:

Type safety — Properties are validated at construction time
Composition — Complex objects built from simpler components
Method dispatch — Generic functions that work across types
Immutability by default — Modifications create new objects

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          WebSpec                                 │
│  The root specification object passed to tabviz()               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  data          │ data.frame with point/interval values          │
│  columns       │ List<ColumnSpec | ColumnGroup>                 │
│  groups        │ List<GroupSpec>                                │
│  theme         │ WebTheme                                       │
│  interaction   │ InteractionSpec                                │
│  annotations   │ List<Annotation>                               │
│  ...           │ Row styling column mappings                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         ├──────────────────────────┐
         │                          │
         ▼                          ▼
┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────────────────┐
│    WebTheme     │      │      ColumnSpec/Group       │
├─────────────────┤      ├─────────────────────────────┤
│ colors          │      │ id, header, field, type     │
│ typography      │      │ width, align, position      │
│ spacing         │      │ style mappings              │
│ shapes          │      └─────────────────────────────┘
│ axis            │
│ layout          │
└─────────────────┘

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Class Hierarchy

Core Classes (R/classes-core.R)







Class
Purpose




WebSpec
Root specification containing all data and configuration


GroupSpec
Row grouping definition with optional nesting


EffectSpec
Multi-effect plot configuration (multiple intervals per row)


GroupSummary
Aggregate statistics for a group


PlotLabels
Title, subtitle, caption, footnote text


Heterogeneity
Meta-analysis heterogeneity statistics (optional)





Theme Classes (R/classes-theme.R)
The v2 theme is a 3-tier S7 hierarchy. Tier 1 holds customer-facing inputs and per-table variants; Tier 2 holds derived semantic role tokens (chrome and data, parallel hierarchies); Tier 3 holds component bindings consumed by the renderer.








Tier
Class
Purpose




1
ThemeInputs
Customer-facing palette + fonts + neutrals


1
ThemeVariants
Per-table toggles (density, header_style, first_column_style)


2 chrome
Surfaces
base / muted / raised


2 chrome
Content
primary / secondary / muted / inverse


2 chrome
Dividers
subtle / strong


2 chrome
AccentRoles
default / muted / tint_subtle / tint_medium


2 chrome
StatusColors
positive / negative / warning / info


2 data
SlotBundle
7-field bundle (used in series list and summary)


2 type
TextRole, TextRoles
Per-role typography bundles (8 named roles)


2 layout
SpacingTokens
12 density-derived numerics


3
AnnotationCluster
title / subtitle / caption / footnote


3
HeaderCluster, HeaderVariant
Light + bold variants of the column-header band


3
ColumnGroupCluster
Column-group header band


3
RowGroupCluster, RowGroupTier
L1 / L2 / L3 nested row-group hierarchy


3
RowCluster, RowState, RowSemantic
Row states (default/alt/hover/selected) + semantic bundles (emphasis/muted/accent) + banding


3
CellCluster
Cell bg / fg / border / text


3
FirstColumnCluster, FirstColumnVariant
Plain + bold variants of the row-label column


3
PlotScaffold
Axis line / tick / gridline / reference + mark sizes


3
MarksRecipes, MarkRecipe
Per-mark slot-bundle wiring


config
AxisConfig
Forest x-axis range / ticks / gridlines


config
Layout
Plot width, container border


top
WebTheme
Composes all of the above



Hard rule: Tier 2 chrome derivation reads only chrome inputs (neutral, brand, primary_deep, accent, accent_deep). Tier 2 data derivation reads only data inputs (series_anchors, status_*) plus surface.base for muted blends. The two cascades meet only at WebTheme reassembly.


Component Classes (R/classes-components.R)







Class
Purpose




ColumnSpec
Single column definition


ColumnGroup
Hierarchical column header grouping


InteractionSpec
UI interaction toggles (sort, collapse, select, etc.)





Annotation Classes (R/classes-annotations.R)



Class
Purpose




ReferenceLine
Vertical reference line at specific x value


CustomAnnotation
Shape annotation on specific rows


RiskOfBias
Cochrane-style risk of bias traffic lights

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Design Patterns

1. Class + Helper Function Pattern
Every S7 class has a corresponding helper function with a user-friendly API:
# S7 class (internal)
GroupSpec <- new_class("GroupSpec", properties = list(
  id = class_character,
  label = class_character,
  collapsed = new_property(class_logical, default = FALSE),
  parent_id = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
))

# Helper function (user-facing)
web_group <- function(id, label = id, parent = NULL, collapsed = FALSE) {
  GroupSpec(
    id = as.character(id),
    label = as.character(label),
    collapsed = collapsed,
    parent_id = if (is.null(parent)) NA_character_ else as.character(parent)
  )
}
This pattern provides:

Type coercion — Helper handles NULL → NA conversion
Defaults — Sensible defaults without cluttering class definition
Documentation — roxygen docs on the helper, not the class
Validation — Additional business logic before construction



2. Composition over inheritance, with explicit tiers
WebTheme composes ~20 sub-classes organized into three tiers. Tier 1 is authored; Tier 2 is derived from Tier 1 by resolve_theme(); Tier 3 is component-binding leaves derived from Tier 2.
WebTheme <- new_class("WebTheme", properties = list(
  name     = new_property(class_character, default = "default"),
  inputs   = new_property(ThemeInputs,    default = ThemeInputs()),
  variants = new_property(ThemeVariants,  default = ThemeVariants()),

  # Tier 2 (derived; NA-default until resolve_theme runs)
  surface = new_property(Surfaces,      default = Surfaces()),
  content = new_property(Content,       default = Content()),
  divider = new_property(Dividers,      default = Dividers()),
  accent  = new_property(AccentRoles,   default = AccentRoles()),
  status  = new_property(StatusColors,  default = StatusColors()),
  series  = new_property(class_list,    default = list()),
  summary = new_property(SlotBundle,    default = SlotBundle()),
  text    = new_property(TextRoles,     default = TextRoles()),
  spacing = new_property(SpacingTokens, default = SpacingTokens()),

  # Tier 3 (component bindings)
  annotation   = new_property(AnnotationCluster, ...),
  header       = new_property(HeaderCluster,     ...),
  column_group = new_property(ColumnGroupCluster, ...),
  row_group    = new_property(RowGroupCluster,   ...),
  row          = new_property(RowCluster,        ...),
  cell         = new_property(CellCluster,       ...),
  first_column = new_property(FirstColumnCluster, ...),
  plot         = new_property(PlotScaffold,      ...),
  marks        = new_property(MarksRecipes,      ...),

  # Config (not part of the tier cascade)
  axis   = new_property(AxisConfig, default = AxisConfig()),
  layout = new_property(Layout,     default = Layout())
))


3. Fluent modifiers
Theme modification uses pipe-friendly functions that return re-resolved copies:
web_theme_jama() |>
  set_inputs(primary = "#0066cc") |>
  set_variants(density = "comfortable") |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 24) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
Implementation pattern (simplified):
set_inputs <- function(theme, ...) {
  stopifnot(S7_inherits(theme, WebTheme))
  args <- list(...)
  for (prop in names(args)) {
    S7::prop(theme@inputs, prop) <- args[[prop]]
  }
  resolve_theme(theme)
}
Each modifier re-runs resolve_theme() before returning. Resolution is idempotent and only fills NA-valued fields, so explicit user overrides survive.


4. NA vs NULL Convention
The package follows a consistent pattern for optional values:








Type
Default
Meaning




Scalar optional (string, number)
NA_character_ / NA_real_
“Not specified”


Complex object optional
NULL
“Not present”



Examples:
# Scalar optional — use NA
label_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
weight_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)

# Complex object optional — use NULL
overall_summary = new_property(
  new_union(GroupSummary, class_missing),
  default = NULL
)
theme = new_property(class_any, default = NULL)
This is checked during serialization:
# NA → NULL for JSON
label = if (is.na(spec@label_col)) NULL else spec@label_col


5. Validators for Business Logic
S7 validators enforce invariants that can’t be expressed through types alone. The real WebSpec validator just checks optional-column references resolve; stronger checks (log-scale positivity, lower <= upper) are done upstream in tabviz() and in viz_forest() / ColumnSpec construction where the column-to-data binding is known.
WebSpec <- new_class("WebSpec",
  properties = list(...),
  validator = function(self) {
    cols <- names(self@data)
    if (!is.na(self@group_col) && !self@group_col %in% cols) {
      return(paste0("Column '", self@group_col, "' not found in data"))
    }
    NULL  # Return NULL if valid
  }
)

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
WebSpec Deep Dive
WebSpec is the central data structure. Understanding it is key to understanding the package.

Core Data Properties
WebSpec <- new_class("WebSpec", properties = list(
  # Source data
  data = class_data.frame,

  # Group column mappings
  group_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  group_cols = new_property(class_character, default = character(0)),

  # Column specifications (a list of ColumnSpec / ColumnGroup; viz_forest()
  # owns its own point/lower/upper/scale/null_value)
  columns = new_property(class_list, default = list()),
  extra_columns = new_property(class_list, default = list()),
  available_exclude = new_property(class_character, default = character(0)),

  # Marker styling column mappings
  marker_color_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  marker_shape_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  marker_opacity_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  marker_size_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),
  weight_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_),  # deprecated alias

  # Configuration
  theme = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  interaction = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  labels = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  ...
))
The forest column’s point / lower / upper / scale / null_value are properties of the viz_forest() ColumnSpec, not of WebSpec. The same is true for viz_bar(), viz_boxplot(), viz_violin() — each carries its own data references inside ColumnSpec@options.


Row Styling Column Mappings
Row-level styling is controlled via column references, not inline values:
# Instead of storing style values directly:
# row_bold = c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)  ← NOT this

# We store the column name containing the values:
row_bold_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_italic_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_color_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_bg_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_badge_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_icon_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_indent_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
row_type_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
marker_size_col = new_property(class_character, default = NA_character_)
Usage in tabviz():
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_bold = "is_summary",        # Column name, not values
  row_badge = "significance",     # Column name
  marker_size = "study_weight"    # Column name (formerly `weight`)
)
This pattern:

Keeps data and configuration separate
Allows dynamic styling from data columns
Avoids duplicating data in the spec



Hierarchical Groups
Groups support nesting via parent_id:
GroupSpec(id = "north_america", label = "North America", parent_id = NA_character_)
GroupSpec(id = "usa", label = "United States", parent_id = "north_america")
GroupSpec(id = "canada", label = "Canada", parent_id = "north_america")
The group_cols property tracks hierarchy for composite ID generation:
# For data grouped by region > country
group_cols = c("region", "country")  # Outer to inner
group_col = "country"                # Deepest level for row assignment

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
ColumnSpec Design
Columns support multiple types with type-specific options:
ColumnSpec <- new_class("ColumnSpec", properties = list(
  id = class_character,
  header = class_character,
  field = class_character,
  type = new_property(class_character, default = "text"),
  width = new_property(class_any, default = NA_real_),  # numeric or "auto"
  align = new_property(class_character, default = "left"),
  options = new_property(class_list, default = list()),

  # Per-cell style mappings
  style_bold = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_italic = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_color = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_bg = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_badge = new_property(class_any, default = NULL),
  style_icon = new_property(class_any, default = NULL)
))

Type-Specific Options
The options property holds type-specific configuration:
# Bar column
col_bar("weight", max_value = 100, show_label = TRUE, color = "#3b82f6")
# Creates: options = list(bar = list(maxValue = 100, showLabel = TRUE, color = "#3b82f6"))

# P-value column
col_pvalue("pval", stars = TRUE, thresholds = c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001))
# Creates: options = list(pvalue = list(stars = TRUE, thresholds = c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001)))

# Sparkline column
col_sparkline("trend", type = "area", height = 24)
# Creates: options = list(sparkline = list(type = "area", height = 24))


ColumnGroup for Hierarchical Headers
ColumnGroup <- new_class("ColumnGroup", properties = list(
  id = class_character,
  header = class_character,
  columns = new_property(class_list, default = list())
))
Usage:
col_group("Results",
  col_interval("or", "lo", "hi", header = "OR (95% CI)"),
  col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
)

title: S7 Class Architecture
text:
Serialization to JavaScript
S7 objects are converted to JSON for the JavaScript frontend via serialize_spec():
R (S7 objects)                    JSON                      TypeScript
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WebSpec                    →    { data: {...},        →    WebSpec interface
  @data                         columns: [...],
  @columns                      theme: {...},
  @theme                        ... }
  ...

WebTheme                   →    { name: "...",        →    WebTheme interface
  @colors                       colors: {...},
  @typography                   typography: {...},
  ...                           ... }
Key transformations:



R Pattern
JSON Pattern




snake_case property
camelCase key


NA_character_
null


NA_real_
null


S7 nested object
Nested JSON object


class_list of S7
Array of JSON objects



Example serialization:
serialize_theme <- function(theme) {
  list(
    name = theme@name,
    colors = list(
      background = theme@colors@background,
      foreground = theme@colors@foreground,
      # snake_case → camelCase
      intervalPositive = theme@colors@interval_positive,
      intervalNegative = theme@colors@interval_negative,
      ...
    ),
    ...
  )
}

title: S7 Class Architecture
S7 Class Architecture
S7 Class Architecture
text:
Best Practices

Creating New Classes

Start with the helper function API, then design the class to support it
Use NA_* for optional scalars, NULL for optional objects
Add validators for invariants that can’t be expressed as types
Document the helper, not the class directly



Modifying Existing Classes

Add new properties with defaults to maintain backward compatibility
Update serialization in utils-serialize.R
Update TypeScript types in srcjs/src/types/index.ts
Update fluent modifiers if the property should be user-configurable



Testing Classes
# Test construction
spec <- web_spec(df, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")
expect_s7_class(spec, WebSpec)

# Test validation
expect_error(
  WebSpec(data = df, point_col = "nonexistent", ...),
  "not found in data"
)

# Test serialization round-trip
json <- serialize_spec(spec)
expect_equal(json$data$pointCol, "hr")

title: Export System Architecture
text: A single TypeScript SVG generator drives every static export, whether triggered from R via save_plot() or from the in-browser download button. This page explains why and how that pipeline works, what the V8 bundle does, and where to extend it.

title: Export System Architecture
text:
Overview
tabviz uses a unified rendering architecture for static image export. The same JavaScript SVG generator powers both:

R-side: save_plot() function (via the V8 package)
Web-side: Download button in interactive plots

This ensures consistent output regardless of where the export is triggered.
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   svg-generator.ts                       │
│         (Pure-data SVG generator, no DOM needed)         │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
       ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
       │                               │
       ▼                               ▼
┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐
│  Web (browser)   │         │   R (via V8)     │
│  DownloadButton  │         │   save_plot()    │
└──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘

title: Export System Architecture
text:
Why This Design?

Single Source of Truth
Instead of maintaining two separate renderers (one in R, one in JavaScript), we have a single TypeScript implementation that:

Reduces code duplication
Guarantees visual consistency between exports
Makes bug fixes and improvements apply to both platforms



No Browser Dependency in R
The SVG generator is DOM-free — it takes WebSpec JSON data and outputs an SVG string through pure computation. This means:

Works in R via the lightweight V8 JavaScript engine
No need for headless Chrome/Chromium
Fast execution without browser overhead
Works offline without any system dependencies beyond V8

title: Export System Architecture
text:
Components

svg-generator.ts
Located at srcjs/src/lib/svg-generator.ts, this is the core rendering engine.
Key functions:
// Main entry point
generateSVG(spec: WebSpec, options?: ExportOptions): string

// Internal helpers
computeLayout(spec: WebSpec): Layout
renderUnifiedTableRow(row, columns, layout, theme): string
renderInterval(row, yPosition, xScale, theme): string
renderDiamond(point, lower, upper, yPosition, ...): string
renderForestAxis(xScale, layout, theme, axisLabel): string
renderVizBar(...) / renderVizBoxplot(...) / renderVizViolin(...): string
Design constraints:

No DOM access — Cannot use document, window, canvas.measureText(), etc.
No external dependencies — Pure TypeScript, bundled as standalone IIFE
Deterministic output — Same input always produces same SVG



Text Measurement and Auto-Width
Since V8 has no DOM, we can’t use canvas.measureText() for precise text width calculation. Instead, width-utils.ts provides character-aware estimation:
function estimateTextWidth(text: string, fontSize: number): number {
  let width = 0;
  for (const char of text) {
    if ("il1.,;:|!()[]{}' ".includes(char)) {
      width += fontSize * 0.35;  // Narrow characters
    } else if ("mwMW@%".includes(char)) {
      width += fontSize * 0.85;  // Wide characters
    } else if (char >= "0" && char <= "9") {
      width += fontSize * 0.6;   // Tabular numbers
    } else {
      width += fontSize * 0.55;  // Average characters
    }
  }
  return width;
}
Auto-width calculation: Both web and SVG renderers now calculate column widths from actual content. For each column with width="auto":

Measure the header text
Measure all cell values using the proper formatted text (e.g., “0.76 (0.68, 0.85)” for intervals)
Apply padding (28px) and constraints (min 60px, max 600px)

This ensures columns are wide enough to display their content without truncation, matching the behavior of the interactive web renderer.


Build Outputs
The SVG generator is bundled twice:








File
Purpose
Size




inst/htmlwidgets/tabviz.js
Full widget bundle (includes Svelte UI)
~142 KB


inst/js/svg-generator.js
Standalone for V8 (export only)
~11 KB



Build commands:
cd srcjs
bun run build          # Both bundles
bun run build:widget   # Widget only
bun run build:v8       # V8 bundle only

title: Export System Architecture
text:
R Integration

save_plot()
save_plot(spec, "output.svg", width = 800, height = NULL)
Process:

Serialize WebSpec to JSON via serialize_spec()
Create V8 context and load svg-generator.js
Call generateSVG(specJson, options)
Write SVG string to file (or convert to PDF/PNG via rsvg)

Dependencies:

V8 — JavaScript engine (Suggests)
rsvg — SVG to PDF/PNG conversion (Suggests, optional)



Code Path
save_plot(spec, file)
    │
    ├── serialize_spec(spec)     # R: WebSpec → JSON
    │
    ├── generate_svg_v8(json)    # R: Load V8, call JS
    │       │
    │       └── generateSVG()    # JS: JSON → SVG string
    │
    └── writeLines() / rsvg      # R: Save to file

title: Export System Architecture
Export System Architecture
Export System Architecture
Export System Architecture
text:
Troubleshooting

“SVG generator JavaScript file not found”
The V8 bundle hasn’t been built. Run:
cd srcjs && bun run build


Text alignment looks off
The text width estimation is character-aware but may still be slightly off for unusual fonts. Solutions:

Explicitly set column widths with col_text("field", width = 150)
Use a standard font family in the theme (Inter, Arial, system-ui)
Increase or decrease padding via the AUTO_WIDTH.PADDING constant in rendering-constants.ts



Colors don’t match the interactive plot
Ensure you’re using the same theme. The generator resolves all CSS variables to literal color values at export time.

title: Troubleshooting
text: This guide covers common issues and their solutions when using tabviz.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Quick Diagnostic Checklist
When something isn’t working, check:

Column names — Are they spelled correctly and exist in the data?
Data types — Are numeric columns actually numeric?
NAs — Are there unexpected missing values?
Scale — Using scale = "log" with non-positive values?
Dependencies — Is V8 installed (always required) and rsvg (for PNG/PDF)?
Deprecated arguments — tabviz(axis_range / axis_ticks / axis_gridlines / zoom / auto_fit / weight) were deprecated in 0.9.0; pass them to viz_forest() (axis_*) or pipe through set_zoom() instead. forest_plot() keeps the axis args native.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Data Issues

“Column not found in data”
Symptom: Error message about missing column.
Cause: Column name typo or the column doesn’t exist.
Solution:


Code
# Check what columns exist
names(your_data)

# Common mistake: using wrong case
forest_plot(data, point = "HR", ...)  # Wrong: "HR"
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", ...)  # Correct: "hr"




Unexpected NAs in Plot
Symptom: Rows appear without intervals or with missing values.
Cause: Columns contain NA values or wrong data types.
Solution:


Code
# Check for NAs
summary(your_data)

# Check column types - character columns won't work for numeric fields
sapply(your_data, class)

# Fix character columns that should be numeric
your_data$hr <- as.numeric(your_data$hr)




Groups Not Displaying Correctly
Symptom: Hierarchical groups appear flat or have wrong nesting.
Cause: Group column contains unexpected values or ID collisions.
Solution:


Code
# Check unique group values
table(your_data$region)
table(your_data$subregion)

# Ensure nested groups have consistent parent values
your_data |>
  group_by(region, subregion) |>
  count()




Data Rows Mixed with Headers
Symptom: Header rows show intervals, or data rows appear as headers.
Cause: row_type column has incorrect values.
Solution:


Code
# Valid row_type values
valid_types <- c("header", "data", "summary", "spacer")

# Check your values
your_data |>
  count(row_type)

# Ensure header/spacer rows have NA for estimates
your_data <- your_data |>
  mutate(
    hr = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, hr),
    lower = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, lower),
    upper = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, upper)
  )

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Scale and Axis Issues

Log Scale with Non-Positive Values
Symptom: Error or unexpected axis range when using scale = "log".
Cause: Data contains zero or negative values, which are undefined on log scale.
Solution:


Code
# Find problematic rows
your_data |>
  filter(hr <= 0 | lower <= 0 | upper <= 0)

# Option 1: Filter out invalid rows
valid_data <- your_data |>
  filter(hr > 0, lower > 0, upper > 0)

# Option 2: Use linear scale instead
forest_plot(your_data, ..., scale = "linear", null_value = 0)




Axis Range Too Wide
Symptom: Data points are compressed into a small portion of the axis.
Cause: Outliers or automatic axis calculation including extreme values.
Solution:


Code
# Check data range
range(your_data$hr, na.rm = TRUE)
range(your_data$lower, na.rm = TRUE)
range(your_data$upper, na.rm = TRUE)

# Option 1: Override axis range
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  axis_range = c(0.5, 2.0)  # Focus on relevant range
)

# Option 2: Filter outliers
filtered <- your_data |>
  filter(between(hr, 0.3, 3))




Axis Range Too Narrow
Symptom: Confidence intervals extend beyond axis bounds.
Cause: Manual axis range doesn’t accommodate all data.
Solution:


Code
# Find the actual data extent
min_val <- min(your_data$lower, na.rm = TRUE)
max_val <- max(your_data$upper, na.rm = TRUE)
message("Data range: ", round(min_val, 2), " to ", round(max_val, 2))

# Adjust axis_range to include all data
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  axis_range = c(min_val * 0.9, max_val * 1.1)
)




Tick Marks Not Appearing
Symptom: Axis shows but has no tick marks or unexpected tick positions.
Solution:


Code
# Specify explicit tick positions
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  scale = "log",
  axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 2)  # Explicit ticks
)

# Or use theme settings
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  theme = web_theme_cochrane() |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "tick_count"), 5) |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
)








NoteOn tabviz() (vs forest_plot())



tabviz() no longer takes axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines (deprecated in 0.9.0). Pass them to viz_forest() on the forest column instead:
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_range = c(0.5, 2.0),
               axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 2))
  )
)
forest_plot() keeps these as native top-level arguments and forwards them to the auto-built forest column.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Export Failures

V8 Not Installed
Symptom: Error when calling save_plot(): “Package V8 is required”
Solution:


Code
# Install V8
install.packages("V8")

# On some systems, you may need system libraries first:
# macOS: brew install v8
# Ubuntu: sudo apt-get install libv8-dev




rsvg Issues
Symptom: PDF/PNG export fails with rsvg errors.
Solution:


Code
# Install rsvg
install.packages("rsvg")

# On macOS, you may need:
# brew install librsvg

# On Ubuntu:
# sudo apt-get install librsvg2-dev




“SVG generator JavaScript file not found”
Symptom: Export fails with message about missing JS file.
Cause: Package installation is incomplete.
Solution:


Code
# Reinstall the package
pak::pak("kaskarn/tabviz", force = TRUE)

# Verify installation
system.file("js/svg-generator.js", package = "tabviz")
# Should return a path, not ""




Colors Differ Between Display and Export
Symptom: Exported SVG/PNG has different colors than the interactive widget.
Cause: Theme not applied consistently to both.
Solution:


Code
# Use the same theme for display and export
my_theme <- web_theme_lancet()

# Interactive
forest_plot(data, ..., theme = my_theme)

# Export
spec <- web_spec(data, ..., theme = my_theme)
save_plot(spec, "output.svg")

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Widget Rendering Issues

Blank Output in RStudio Viewer
Symptom: Plot doesn’t appear in RStudio’s Viewer pane.
Cause: Viewer pane size or rendering issues.
Solution:


Code
# Try in browser instead
plot <- forest_plot(your_data, ...)
htmltools::browsable(plot)

# Or save to file and open
htmlwidgets::saveWidget(plot, "temp.html")
browseURL("temp.html")




Widget Not Sizing Correctly
Symptom: Plot is too small, cut off, or doesn’t fill container.
Solution:


Code
# Specify explicit dimensions
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  width = 900,
  height = 600
)

# For Shiny, use CSS units
tabvizOutput("plot", width = "100%", height = "600px")

# Constrain max size via set_zoom() (replaces deprecated tabviz(max_width=)/(max_height=))
tabviz(your_data, ...) |>
  set_zoom(max_width = 1000, auto_fit = TRUE)




Shiny: Proxy Not Working
Symptom: tabviz_proxy() calls don’t update the plot.
Cause: Proxy created outside reactive context.
Solution:


Code
# Wrong: proxy at top level
# proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")  # This will fail!

# Correct: proxy inside reactive context
observeEvent(input$button, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  sort_rows(proxy, "hr", "asc")
})

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Theme and Styling Issues

Styles Not Applying
Symptom: Bold, color, or other styles don’t appear.
Cause: Passing values directly instead of column names.
Solution:


Code
# Wrong: passing values directly
col_numeric("hr", bold = TRUE, color = "#ff0000")

# Correct: passing column names that contain the values
col_numeric("hr", bold = "is_bold", color = "hr_color")

# The data must have these columns:
data <- data |>
  mutate(
    is_bold = pval < 0.05,
    hr_color = if_else(hr < 1, "#16a34a", "#dc2626")
  )




Font Not Loading
Symptom: Text appears in wrong font.
Cause: Custom font not available on system.
Solution:


Code
# Use web-safe fallback fonts
web_theme_cochrane() |>
  set_inputs(font_body = "Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif")

# For exports, fonts must be installed on the rendering system

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Debug Patterns

Inspecting a WebSpec


Code
# Build a spec without rendering (use .spec_only = TRUE on tabviz())
spec <- tabviz(
  data.frame(
    study = c("A", "B"),
    hr = c(0.8, 1.2),
    n = c(100, 200)
  ),
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
    col_n("n")
  ),
  .spec_only = TRUE
)

# Print summary
print(spec)


<tabviz::WebSpec>
 @ data                :'data.frame':   2 obs. of  3 variables:
 .. $ study: chr  "A" "B"
 .. $ hr   : num  0.8 1.2

Browser Developer Tools
For debugging interactive widgets:

Right-click the widget → “Inspect”
Check Console tab for JavaScript errors
Check Elements tab for DOM structure
Check Network tab for failed resources



Minimal Reproducible Example
When reporting issues, create a minimal example:


Code
# Minimal data that reproduces the problem
test_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Test 1", "Test 2"),
  hr = c(0.8, 1.2),
  lower = c(0.6, 0.9),
  upper = c(1.1, 1.6)
)

# Minimal code
forest_plot(test_data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study"
)

# Include session info
sessionInfo()

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Pagination

“Why didn’t my paginate spec page my PNG?”
save_plot(<paginated spec>, "out.png") warns and flattens by default — single-image formats can’t represent multiple pages. To silence the warning when you want a flattened image:
save_plot(p, "out.png", paginate = NULL)  # explicit "flatten this render"
Or save to PDF for true multi-page output:
save_plot(p, "out.pdf")  # multi-page PDF via qpdf::pdf_combine


Multi-page PDF wrote a numbered series instead of one file
save_plot(*.pdf, paginate = ...) merges per-page PDFs via qpdf::pdf_combine(). When the qpdf package isn’t installed, it falls back to writing out_p01.pdf, out_p02.pdf, … and emits a warning. Install qpdf to merge into one file:
install.packages("qpdf")
qpdf is in Suggests (lightweight C++ binding, ~3 MB).


“My SplitForest paginate didn’t cascade”
Pagination cascades into every subview when set on the underlying tabviz() call. If you constructed the spec without paginate, then piped through split_table(), the subviews carry no pagination. Either set paginate on the original tabviz() call (recommended), or pipe paginate() after split:
sf <- tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_letter(),
             split_by = "region")

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Widget rendering in knitr

“My split_table widget rendered as a wall of @ specs : List of 6 text”
Fixed in this audit cycle. SplitForest (the object returned by split_table() and by tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE) |> split_table(...)) now carries a registered knit_print method, so a chunk whose last value is one renders as the live interactive widget. Same for WebSpec from tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE).
If you still see the structure dump in your own project, you’re likely on an old install. Reinstall:
remotes::install_github("kaskarn/tabviz")

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Getting Help
If you’ve tried the above and still have issues:

Check GitHub Issues for similar problems
Create a minimal reproducible example
Include sessionInfo() output
Open a new issue with your example and session info

title: Data Operations
text:
The data-operation verbs provide a fluent, pipe-friendly interface for adding, removing, rearranging, filtering, sorting, and editing the columns, rows, and cells of a table. Every verb dispatches on input type:
This single surface means the same code can compose a static plot or drive a live Shiny widget.

title: Data Operations
text:
Column Operations

add_column()
Insert a new column into the table.
add_column(x, col, after = NULL)







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


col
A ColumnSpec from col_text(), col_numeric(), col_bar(), etc.


after
Field/id of an existing column to insert after. NULL (default) appends; "__start__" prepends.



Returns: Modified input (same type).


Code
spec <- web_spec(data, columns = list(col_text("study"), col_numeric("hr")))

# Append a column
spec |> add_column(col_numeric("se", "SE"))

# Insert after a specific field
spec |> add_column(col_badge("sig"), after = "hr")

# Prepend
spec |> add_column(col_icon("flag"), after = "__start__")




remove_column()
Remove a column by field or id.
remove_column(x, field)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column to remove



On a proxy this sends hideColumn, which hides the column client-side without destroying its spec — add_column() with the same field will restore it.


Code
spec |> remove_column("notes")




move_column()
Change a column’s position.
move_column(x, field, to)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column to move


to
1-based integer index, or the field name of another column



On the R side, indices are 1-based. The proxy payload converts to 0-based before sending to JS — you do not need to adjust.


Code
# Move to position 3
spec |> move_column("hr", to = 3L)

# Move before the "pval" column
spec |> move_column("hr", to = "pval")








Note



Within-group moves via static dispatch are currently unsupported — move_column() on a column that lives inside a col_group() will error. Use proxy dispatch or restructure groups.




resize_column()
Set a column’s pixel width.
resize_column(x, field, width)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column


width
Width in pixels (≥ 40)





Code
spec |> resize_column("study", width = 240)




update_column()
Edit properties of an existing column in place.
update_column(x, field, ...)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column


...
Named properties to update



Top-level slots written directly: header, align, header_align, wrap, sortable, width. Any other name is merged into the column’s options list (useful for type-specific knobs such as decimals, format, colors).


Code
spec |>
  update_column("hr",
    header = "Hazard Ratio",
    align = "right",
    decimals = 3
  )

title: Data Operations
text:
Row & Data Operations

sort_rows()
Order rows by a column’s values.
sort_rows(x, by, direction = c("asc", "desc", "none"))







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


by
Field or id of the column to sort by


direction
"asc", "desc", or "none" (clears sort on a proxy)



On static inputs, direction = "none" is a no-op; the spec data frame keeps its existing order.


Code
spec |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "desc")




filter_rows()
Keep rows that match a predicate.
filter_rows(x, field, operator = c("eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains"), value)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field to filter on


operator
"eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", or "contains"


value
Scalar value to compare against





Code
spec |> filter_rows("region", "eq", "North America")
spec |> filter_rows("hr", "lt", 1)


On static inputs, rows where the predicate is NA are dropped.


clear_filters()
Remove any runtime filters.
clear_filters(x)
No-op for static inputs (there is no persisted filter state). On a proxy, sends clearFilter to reset the widget’s filter stack.


select_rows()
Replace the current row selection (proxy only).
select_rows(x, row_ids)



Argument
Description




x
A tabviz_proxy


row_ids
Character vector of row ids to select



On static inputs, warns and returns the input unchanged — selection is a runtime concept with no spec-level representation.


Code
observeEvent(input$highlight, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  select_rows(proxy, c("Trial A", "Trial C"))
})




move_row()
Reposition a row within the data frame.
move_row(x, row_id, to)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


row_id
Row id (the value in the leftmost label/id column)


to
1-based integer index, or another row id





Code
spec |> move_row("Summary", to = 1L)




update_data()
Replace the entire underlying data frame.
update_data(x, data)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


data
A data frame (static inputs) or WebSpec (proxy)



For a proxy you must pass a complete WebSpec, not a raw data frame — column specs, themes, and annotations need to be re-serialized together. Build a fresh spec with tabviz() or web_spec() and pass it in.


Code
# Static: swap the data frame
spec |> update_data(new_df)

# Proxy: full re-specification
observeEvent(input$refresh, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  fresh <- tabviz(reactive_data(), label = "study",
                  columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")))
  update_data(proxy, attr(fresh, "webspec"))
})

title: Data Operations
text:
Group Operations

toggle_group()
Collapse or expand a named group.
toggle_group(x, group_id, collapsed = NULL)







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


group_id
Id of the group to toggle


collapsed
TRUE to collapse, FALSE to expand, NULL to flip current state



Groups are defined via the group argument of tabviz(); if a spec has none, toggle_group() warns for static inputs.


Code
spec |> toggle_group("secondary_endpoints", collapsed = TRUE)

title: Data Operations
text:
Cell Edits (Proxy Only)
These verbs carry changes directly into a running widget. They have no spec-level counterpart — on static inputs they warn and return unchanged.

set_cell()
Overwrite a single cell.
set_cell(x, row_id, field, value)


Code
set_cell(proxy, row_id = "Trial A", field = "hr", value = 0.84)




set_row_label()
Rename a row’s label.
set_row_label(x, row_id, label)


Code
set_row_label(proxy, "Trial A", "Trial A (updated)")




clear_edits()
Discard every in-widget cell and label edit applied since the last full render.
clear_edits(x)

title: Data Operations
text:
Labels and watermark
Title / subtitle / caption / footnote / watermark have dedicated single-slot verbs that mirror the tabviz() arguments. Each accepts NULL to clear the slot.

set_title() / set_subtitle() / set_caption() / set_footnote()
set_title(x, title)         # NULL clears
set_subtitle(x, subtitle)
set_caption(x, caption)
set_footnote(x, footnote)


set_watermark()
set_watermark(x, text = NULL, color = NULL, opacity = NULL)
A diagonally-rotated watermark across the full plot region. Pass text = NULL to clear. color is a CSS color (defaults to theme.colors.foreground); opacity is a number in [0, 1] (default 0.07).

title: Data Operations
text:
Painter (semantic-token stamping)
The painter applies one of the five semantic tokens (emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill) to a row or cell — equivalent to setting the matching row_* flag for that single row, but as a fluent verb suitable for piped one-shot updates and proxy dispatch.

paint_row()
paint_row(x, row_id, token = c("emphasis", "muted", "accent", "bold", "fill"))


paint_cell()
paint_cell(x, row_id, field, token = c("emphasis", "muted", "accent", "bold", "fill"))

title: Data Operations
text:
Pagination

paginate()
Fluent modifier mirroring tabviz(paginate = ...). Useful at the end of a pipe when the pagination policy is decided after the spec has been built, or for toggling on/off without re-running the constructor.
paginate(x, ...)
... accepts either a single positional PaginateSpec / shortcut value (paginate(x, paginate_letter()), paginate(x, 50), paginate(x, TRUE), paginate(x, NULL)) or named arguments forwarded to paginate_spec() (paginate(x, rows = 20, orphan_min = 5)).
spec |> paginate(rows = 30)         # sensible defaults
spec |> paginate(paginate_letter()) # preset
spec |> paginate(NULL)              # turn off

title: Data Operations
text:
Split-by column widths

set_shared_column_widths()
set_shared_column_widths(x, enabled = TRUE)
When the input is a SplitForest (or htmlwidget over one), stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot so columns line up tile-to-tile when screenshots are stacked. Works through the proxy too — equivalent to flipping the Align column widths checkbox in the sidebar footer.

title: Data Operations
text:
Global controls
set_theme() and set_zoom() are documented in the styling reference — they are global presentation modifiers rather than data verbs, but they accept the same input shapes (spec / widget / proxy where applicable).

filter_rows()

update_data()

Groups & cells

Labels, watermark, painter, pagination, splits

Toggle aligned per-column widths across split sub-plots

title: Columns
text:
Reference for every column-defining helper: data columns (col_*()), per-row visualization columns (viz_*()), the low-level web_col() constructor, column groups, and the per-effect helpers used by multi-effect viz columns.
All col_*() helpers require field (the data column name) as the first positional argument. They also accept the cell-styling arguments documented in Cell-level styling below: bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent, fill, plus align, header_align, wrap, sortable, na_text, tooltip, formatter, and flex.

title: Columns
text:
Basic data columns

col_text()
Plain text column.
col_text(field, header = NULL, width = NULL, max_chars = NULL, ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




field
character(1)
required
Data column name.


header
character(1)
NULL (uses field)
Display header.


width
numeric(1)
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels.


max_chars
integer(1)
NULL
Truncate with ellipsis past this length.


...
—
—
Forwarded to web_col() (cell styling, alignment).





col_label()
Same as col_text() but auto-prettifies the default header (e.g. "study_id" → "Study Id"). Useful for the leftmost row-identifier column when you want explicit control.
col_label(field, header = NULL, width = NULL, max_chars = NULL, ...)


col_numeric()
Numeric column with formatting, sig-fig support, large-number abbreviation.
col_numeric(field, header = NULL, width = NULL,
            decimals = 2, digits = NULL,
            thousands_sep = FALSE, abbreviate = FALSE, ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description

character(1)
required
Data column.


header
character(1)
NULL
Header text.


width
numeric(1)
NULL
Width in px.

integer(1)
2
Fixed decimal places. Mutually exclusive with digits.


digits
integer(1)
NULL
Significant figures.


thousands_sep
character(1)/FALSE
FALSE
Pass "," (or other) to enable.


abbreviate
logical(1)
FALSE
Shorten ≥1000 (e.g. 1100 → "1.1K").



col_numeric("estimate")
col_numeric("count", decimals = 0, thousands_sep = ",")
col_numeric("population", abbreviate = TRUE)


col_n()
Sample-size / count column. Defaults to integers with , thousands separator.
col_n(field, header = "N", width = NULL,
      decimals = 0, digits = NULL,
      thousands_sep = ",", abbreviate = FALSE, ...)



Argument
Type
Default
Description

Sample-size column.

character(1)
"N"



decimals
integer(1)
0



thousands_sep
character/FALSE
","



abbreviate
logical(1)
FALSE






col_percent()
col_percent(field, header = NULL, width = NULL,
            decimals = 1, digits = NULL,
            multiply = TRUE, symbol = TRUE, ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




field
character(1)
required
Numeric column.


decimals
integer(1)
1



multiply
logical(1)
TRUE
When TRUE, expects 0–1 proportions. Set FALSE if data is already 0–100.


symbol
logical(1)
TRUE
Show %.





col_pvalue()
col_pvalue(field,
           header = "P-value",
           width = NULL,
           stars = FALSE,
           thresholds = c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001),
           format = c("auto", "scientific", "decimal"),
           digits = 2,
           exp_threshold = 0.001,
           abbrev_threshold = NULL, ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




field
character(1)
required
P-value column.


stars
logical(1)
FALSE

thresholds

c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001)

"auto"

digits
integer(1)
