section: Reading order for someone joining mid-program
text:
Reading order for someone joining mid-program

docs/dev/frontend-split-spec.md — the source of truth for the whole program
~/.claude/plans/encapsulated-snacking-leaf.md — the launch plan (what’s PR1, what’s PR2, etc.)
docs/dev/split-program-diary.md — narrative as we went
This file — current state

The spec is authoritative for what we’re doing and why; the diary is authoritative for what happened and what surprises we hit; this file is authoritative for where we are right now.
Code

Classification
Every field is one of:

General — applies to any rendered table. Reusable in a @tabviz/core published API without renaming or repurposing.
Forest — only fires when the spec has at least one type: "forest" column. Outside that context the field is dead weight on the wire; tolerated for symmetry with the forest path.
Viz — only fires for viz_bar / viz_boxplot / viz_violin columns. Same forest-style “general types, special-case meaning” pattern.
Chrome — viewer / export ornament that doesn’t depend on column types (watermark, pagination, plot labels).
Debug — wire metadata for the “View Source” feature, not a rendering input.

The classification is field-by-field, not type-by-type. A general interface like WebData can have one forest-only field (summaries) and one chrome-only field (overall).

Top-level WebSpec








Field
Kind
Notes




version
General
Wire-format version ("1.0"). See docs/dev/versioning.md.


data
General
Rows + groups (+ forest-only summaries; see below).


columns
General
Column tree (any column type).


extraColumns
General
Hidden-by-default columns surfaced by the configure UI; orthogonal to forest.


availableFields
General
Slot-compatibility hints for the editor; orthogonal to forest.


theme
General
Full theme; applies to every rendered table.


interaction
General
Sort / collapse / filter / paint flags.


layout
Forest
plotWidth is the forest column’s pixel width; ignored when no forest column.


labels
Chrome
Plot title / subtitle / caption / footnote — viewer ornament.


watermark / watermarkColor / watermarkOpacity
Chrome
Free-floating text overlay; viewer ornament.


paginate
Chrome
Page-break config + precomputed page ranges; affects every column type uniformly.


initialState
General
One-shot seed for sort / filter / hidden columns at mount.


targetAspect / targetAspectAnchor
General
Aspect-ratio target for export and the in-widget slider.


originalCall
Debug
deparse(call) from R for the View Source baseline line.

WebData








Field
Kind
Notes




rows
General
Data rows.


groups
General
Group tree (banding, group headers, collapse).


summaries
Forest
Per-group summary diamonds (point/lower/upper). No effect on non-forest renders.


overall
Forest
Whole-table summary diamond. Forest-only.


groupCol
General
Source column used for grouping (display metadata).


weightCol
Forest
Sample-size weight column for diamond width; forest-only.

Row
Row itself is general (id, label, metadata, groupId, style). Every field is reusable.

Group, GroupSummary, OverallSummary








Type / Field
Kind
Notes




Group.id / .label / .parentId / .depth / .collapsed
General
Group hierarchy + UI state.


Group.weight
Forest
Pooled sample size for summary diamond width.


GroupSummary.{ point, lower, upper, groupId }
Forest
Per-group meta-analytic summary. Forest-only.


OverallSummary.{ point, lower, upper, label, weight }
Forest
Whole-table summary diamond. Forest-only.

EffectSpec
EffectSpec (id, pointCol, lowerCol, upperCol, label, color, shape, opacity) is forest-specific. The point/lower/upper triple is the forest-plot interval idiom; reusing this type for a non-forest column would force a misleading naming.
MarkerShape ("square" | "circle" | "diamond" | "triangle") is general (used by forest markers + the paint-tool legend), but in practice all current consumers are forest-flavored.

Column types — ColumnSpec.type discriminator








type literal
Kind
Notes




"text"
General
Plain string cell.


"numeric"
General
Number with format options.


"interval"
Forest
Point + CI display (e.g. "1.2 (0.8, 1.6)"); naming reflects forest heritage.


"percent"
General
Numeric with % suffix.


"events"
Forest
n / N cell with optional rate. Used as a forest sidecar.


"bar"
General
Inline bar (intensity / fraction); not a forest column.


"pvalue"
General
Auto-formatted p-value.


"sparkline"
General
Inline mini-line.


"icon" / "badge" / "stars" / "pictogram" / "ring" / "img" / "reference"
General
Glyph / ornament cells.


"range"
General
Min/max display.


"heatmap"
General
Color-coded numeric.


"progress"
General
Bar with explicit value / max.


"forest"
Forest
The defining viz column; everything in ForestColumnOptions applies.


"viz_bar" / "viz_boxplot" / "viz_violin"
Viz
Focal viz family with their own axes; share VizColumnOptionsBase.

ForestColumnOptions
Every field below is forest-only.







Field
Notes




point / lower / upper
Source columns for the single-effect inline case.


effects
Multi-effect list (replaces the inline triple when present).


scale
"linear" or "log". Log scale shifts nullValue default from 0 to 1.


nullValue
Reference line (vertical 0 / 1).


axisLabel / showAxis / axisRange / axisTicks / axisGridlines
Axis chrome.


width
Pixel width of the forest column (overrides layout.plotWidth).


annotations
Reference lines + freeform labels.


sharedAxis
Split-forest knob: share axis range across splits.

VizColumnOptions*
VizColumnOptionsBase and the three subtypes (VizBarColumnOptions, VizBoxplotColumnOptions, VizViolinColumnOptions) are viz-family-specific. They share scale/nullValue/axisRange/axisTicks/axisGridlines/axisLabel/showAxis with ForestColumnOptions but are not interchangeable — the renderer dispatches on the column type discriminator and treats the shared shape as “axis machinery,” not “forest plot.”
Per-type fields (e.g. barWidth, whiskerType, bandwidth) are subtype-only.

ColumnSpec (general)








Field
Kind
Notes




id / field / header / width / align / headerAlign / wrap / sortable / showHeader
General
Universal column knobs.


type
General
Discriminator (see table above).


options
Mixed
A union over per-type option types; the kind of the option block matches the kind of the column.


format
General
Numeric format hint.

ColumnGroup
General. Pure tree structure (columns, header, depth bookkeeping); no forest semantics.

WebTheme (delegated to WebThemeV2)
The theme is general — every section applies to every render. Within the theme:








Subsection
Kind
Notes




colors / text / spacing / shapes
General
Universal styling.


plot (tick marks, axis line, gridlines)
Forest
Theme block consumed only when an axis is rendered (forest + viz columns).


layout.banding / layout.plotWidth
General / Forest
banding is general; plotWidth is forest-only (mirrors WebSpec.layout.plotWidth).

InteractionSpec
General. Every flag (enableSort, enableFilter, enableCollapse, enableSelect, enableHover, enableResize, enableExport, enableReorderRows, enableReorderColumns, enableEdit, showFilters, showLegend, showGroupCounts) applies to every render.

LayoutSpec








Field
Kind
Notes




plotWidth
Forest
Pixel width of the forest column (or "auto"). Ignored when no forest column is present.

PaginationConfig / PageRange
Chrome. Pagination is a viewer/export concern; it applies uniformly to every column type. The breakOn enum has a "split" value that’s only meaningful for split-widget specs but doesn’t affect the column kind.

PlotLabels
Chrome — title / subtitle / caption / footnote.

Style + semantic types








Type
Kind
Notes




RowStyle / CellStyle
General
Paint / semantic / per-row visual overrides.


MarkerStyle
Forest
Point shape / color / opacity for forest markers.


MarkerShape
General
Enum reused by forest + the paint-tool legend.


StyleMapping
General
Token → style/CellStyle map.


SemanticBundle / SemanticToken / Semantics
General
Selection / paint primitives.


GroupHeaderStyles
General
Per-group-header CSS-shaped overrides.

Wire-format envelopes & events
Not properties of WebSpec; documented separately:

Source-tagging envelope (ShinyEnvelope<T>) — docs/dev/source-tagging.md
Event contract (typed events + Shiny wire-field map) — docs/dev/event-contract.md
Proxy method dispatch — srcjs/src/spec/proxy-args.ts (typed); docs/dev/r-js-sync-points.md §S3 covers the cross-side sync.

What a @tabviz/core consumer should expect
If we publish @tabviz/core as a “general tabular viz” package, the forest-flavored fields stay on the wire but become opt-in:

A non-forest spec omits data.summaries, data.overall, data.weightCol, layout.plotWidth, every ForestColumnOptions block, and every theme.plot.* field that only the axis renderer reads.
The viz column types (viz_bar / viz_boxplot / viz_violin) are independent of forest plotting and ship as first-class types in their own right.
ColumnSpec.type === "interval" and "events" carry forest plot vocabulary in their names but render cleanly without a forest column present (they’re just cell formatters).

Renaming the forest-flavored types for a v2 wire format is a deliberate decision deferred per §1.4 of the spec.

Source of truth

TS types: srcjs/src/types/index.ts, srcjs/src/types/theme-v2.ts
JSON Schema: srcjs/src/spec/v1.0.json
Event names + Shiny wire fields: srcjs/src/spec/events.ts
R-side serializer: R/utils-serialize.R

When any of these change, refresh the relevant row in this document.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Two recently-shipped column types — col_pictogram() (repeated glyphs, count or rating) and col_ring() (donut gauges with thresholds) — together cover the “small visual quantum” niche that bare numbers, percentages, and bars can’t hit cleanly. This page walks every meaningful mode of each, then composes them on a worked dashboard.
For the full argument list see the Reference; for theme cascading into glyph color see Visualization Styling.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_pictogram() — count mode
The default. glyph repeats round(value) times per row. Use it when the metric is a small integer count and the reader benefits from visual heft per item.


Code
counts <- data.frame(
  team    = c("Backend", "Frontend", "Platform", "Data"),
  prs     = c(3, 7, 4, 2),
  reviews = c(12, 18, 9, 5)
)

tabviz(counts, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("prs", header = "PRs merged",
                  glyph = "person"),
    col_pictogram("reviews", header = "Reviews",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer")
  ),
  title = "Sprint summary — count mode"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_pictogram() — rating mode
Pass max_glyphs to switch to rating mode: render exactly max_glyphs glyphs per row, the first value filled, the remainder muted. Use it when the metric is a bounded score.


Code
ratings <- data.frame(
  product = c("Widget Pro", "Widget Lite", "Widget Plus", "Widget Max"),
  quality = c(4.5, 3.8, 4.8, 4.1),
  service = c(5,   3,   5,   4),
  perf    = c(3,   4,   5,   3)
)

tabviz(ratings, label = "product",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("quality", header = "Quality",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5,
                  half_glyphs = TRUE,
                  value_label = "trailing", label_decimals = 1),
    col_pictogram("service", header = "Service",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("perf",    header = "Performance",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5)
  ),
  title = "Product ratings — rating mode (max_glyphs = 5)"
)






half_glyphs = TRUE allows a half-filled boundary glyph for fractional values (4.5 stars renders as four full + one half). Without it, the value rounds.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Range remapping (rating mode + min_value / max_value)
When the input range isn’t [0, max_glyphs], pass both min_value and max_value to remap. Useful for percentages, scores on arbitrary scales, etc.


Code
risk <- data.frame(
  asset       = c("AAA Bonds", "Corp Bond", "Equity Idx", "Emerging", "Crypto"),
  risk_score  = c(8, 22, 48, 67, 92)        # 0–100 scale
)

tabviz(risk, label = "asset",
  columns = list(
    # Map [0, 100] into [0, 5] skulls
    col_pictogram("risk_score", header = "Risk",
                  glyph = "skull", max_glyphs = 5,
                  min_value = 0, max_value = 100,
                  color = "#dc2626", empty_color = "#e5e7eb",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer")
  ),
  title = "Risk score — remapped from [0, 100] into [0, 5]"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Per-row glyph selection via glyph_field
Pass glyph as a named character vector and pair with glyph_field to pick a different glyph per row. Useful when the same metric is best read with category-specific iconography.


Code
energy <- data.frame(
  source     = c("Solar farm", "Wind farm", "Hydro plant",
                 "Geothermal", "Biomass"),
  capacity   = c(8, 12, 6, 4, 3),
  category   = c("solar", "wind", "hydro", "geothermal", "biomass")
)

tabviz(energy, label = "source",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("capacity", header = "Capacity (units)",
                  glyph = c(solar      = "leaf",
                            wind       = "leaf",
                            hydro      = "leaf",
                            geothermal = "flame",
                            biomass    = "leaf"),
                  glyph_field = "category",
                  color = "#16a34a")
  ),
  title = "Renewable capacity — per-row glyph selection"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Layout — row vs stack
Default layout is row — glyphs lay horizontally. layout = "stack" piles them vertically. Best in tall rows (rows that already include a viz_* column) where horizontal real estate is at a premium and vertical space is plentiful.


Code
restaurants <- data.frame(
  spot       = c("Cafe A", "Bistro B", "Diner C", "Trattoria D"),
  meals      = c(3, 4, 2, 5),
  hr         = c(0.85, 1.05, 0.92, 0.78),
  lo         = c(0.62, 0.78, 0.66, 0.55),
  hi         = c(1.16, 1.42, 1.27, 1.10)
)

tabviz(restaurants, label = "spot",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("meals", header = "Meals",
                  glyph = "pie", max_glyphs = 5,
                  layout = "stack",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log",
               axis_label = "Effect"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_height = 56,                               # tall rows so stack fits
  title = "Layout = 'stack' alongside a viz column"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — single color, fraction range
Default: input range [0, 1], single color from the theme accent. Best for “% to target”-style metrics.


Code
goals <- data.frame(
  initiative = c("Onboarding", "Activation", "Retention", "Expansion", "Win-back"),
  q3_target  = c(0.85, 0.72, 0.91, 0.58, 0.31)
)

tabviz(goals, label = "initiative",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("q3_target", header = "% to Q3 target",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  title = "Single-color ring — default theme accent fill"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — one threshold (binary palette)
One thresholds value splits the range into two bins. With no explicit color, the package picks a sensible warning / safe palette automatically.


Code
budgets <- data.frame(
  team       = c("Engineering", "Marketing", "Sales", "Ops", "G&A"),
  spent      = c(0.42, 0.78, 0.91, 0.55, 0.38)
)

tabviz(budgets, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("spent", header = "Budget spent",
             thresholds = 0.80,                # over-budget after 80%
             label_format = "percent")
  ),
  title = "One-threshold ring — auto-palette (under / over)"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — two thresholds (status palette)
Two thresholds → three bins → the cascade resolves to the theme’s status palette: positive for the lowest band, warning for the middle, negative for the highest. No hex literals needed; the colors come from the theme.


Code
risk_ring <- data.frame(
  project   = c("Apollo", "Bedrock", "Cypress", "Drift", "Echo", "Falcon"),
  risk      = c(0.12, 0.35, 0.51, 0.68, 0.78, 0.91)
)

tabviz(risk_ring, label = "project",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("risk", header = "Risk score",
             thresholds = c(0.33, 0.66),       # auto positive / warning / negative
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  title = "Two-threshold ring — auto status palette"
)






Switch the theme to JAMA, Lancet, or any custom theme and the colors restyle automatically — status_* is a Tier 1 input.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — explicit colors with thresholds
Pass an explicit color vector when you want to pin specific shades. Length must equal length(thresholds) + 1.


Code
score_card <- data.frame(
  metric  = c("DAU", "WAU", "MAU", "L7d", "L30d"),
  value   = c(0.18, 0.42, 0.71, 0.55, 0.83)
)

tabviz(score_card, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("value", header = "Engagement",
             thresholds = c(0.30, 0.60),
             color = c("#dc2626", "#f59e0b", "#16a34a"),
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  title = "Explicit colors with thresholds"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — sizes
Three sizes are available: "sm" (≈18 px), "base" (≈24 px, default), "lg" (≈32 px). Larger rings may exceed a normal rowHeight — best used in tall rows that already contain a viz_* column or when row_height is bumped up.


Code
sizes <- data.frame(
  metric    = c("Small ring", "Base ring", "Large ring"),
  value_sm  = c(0.65, 0.65, 0.65),
  value_md  = c(0.65, 0.65, 0.65),
  value_lg  = c(0.65, 0.65, 0.65)
)

tabviz(sizes, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("value_sm", header = "sm",   size = "sm",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_ring("value_md", header = "base", size = "base",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_ring("value_lg", header = "lg",   size = "lg",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  row_height = 44,
  title = "Ring sizes side by side"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
section: Theme cascade — semantic row tokens reach into glyphs
text:
Theme cascade — semantic row tokens reach into glyphs
row_accent, row_emphasis, row_muted, row_bold, row_fill all reach into pictogram and ring fills the same way they reach into forest markers. Tag a row with the semantic intent; the cascade resolves the color from the theme.


Code
escalations <- data.frame(
  account     = c("Acme Corp", "Bedrock LLC", "Cosmic Inc",
                  "Delta Co",  "Eclipse Ltd"),
  health      = c(0.88, 0.42, 0.65, 0.91, 0.18),
  open_tix    = c(2, 12, 6, 1, 18),
  is_critical = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),  # row_accent
  is_top      = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE)  # row_fill
)

tabviz(escalations, label = "account",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("health", header = "Health",
             thresholds = c(0.40, 0.75),
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_pictogram("open_tix", header = "Open tickets",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 12)
  ),
  row_accent = "is_critical",
  row_fill   = "is_top",
  title    = "Account health — semantic tokens reach into glyph cells",
  subtitle = "Eclipse Ltd carries the accent; Delta Co carries the fill."
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Putting it together — survey-feedback dashboard
A six-row dashboard that exercises every mode covered above. Each segment shows: NPS as a ring (auto status palette), promoter / detractor counts as horizontal pictograms, CSAT as a remapped rating-mode pictogram, and a 5-quarter trend sparkline for context.


Code
feedback <- data.frame(
  segment        = c("Enterprise", "Mid-market", "SMB",
                     "Self-serve",  "Education", "Public"),
  nps            = c(0.62, 0.48, 0.31, 0.08, 0.74, 0.44),
  csat_5pt       = c(4.5,  4.1,  3.7,  3.2,  4.7,  3.9),
  promoters      = c(11,   8,    6,    3,    13,   7),
  detractors     = c(2,    3,    8,    14,   1,    4),
  trend          = I(list(
    c(78, 80, 82, 85, 87), c(72, 75, 78, 80, 82),
    c(65, 68, 70, 72, 74), c(60, 62, 64, 65, 66),
    c(82, 85, 87, 88, 89), c(70, 73, 75, 77, 78)
  )),
  is_top         = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE),
  is_concern     = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(feedback, label = "segment",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("nps", header = "NPS",
             thresholds = c(0.20, 0.50),                  # auto status palette
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_pictogram("csat_5pt", header = "CSAT (5pt)",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5,
                  half_glyphs = TRUE,
                  value_label = "trailing", label_decimals = 1),
    col_pictogram("promoters", header = "Promoters",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 14),
    col_pictogram("detractors", header = "Detractors",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 14,
                  color = "#dc2626"),
    col_sparkline("trend", "5q trend")
  ),
  row_fill   = "is_top",
  row_accent = "is_concern",
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title    = "Customer feedback — pictograms + rings end-to-end",
  subtitle = "Education leads (row_fill); Self-serve flagged for response-rate concern (row_accent)."
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
When to reach for what







Metric shape
Reach for




Small integer count (1–15)
col_pictogram(...) count mode


Bounded rating (1–5, 0–10)
col_pictogram(..., max_glyphs = ...)


Fractional metric in [0, 1]
col_ring(...)


[0, 1] metric with “good / bad” cutoff
col_ring(..., thresholds = X)


[0, 1] metric with “low / mid / high” bands
col_ring(..., thresholds = c(X, Y)) (auto status palette)


Anything outside [0, 1]
Add min_value / max_value


Want category-specific iconography
col_pictogram(glyph = c(...), glyph_field = "...")


Need the value next to the glyph
value_label = "trailing" (or "leading")


Glyph piles in a tall row
layout = "stack"

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
See also

Reference → col_pictogram() and col_ring() — every argument.
Domain Dashboards — sports / survey / portfolio examples that use these in context.
LOTR (Easter Egg) — three full-page demos using the bespoke glyph set.
Visualization Styling — the four-layer cascade that drives glyph color.

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
A real meta-analysis often has 50–100+ studies, several cohorts, pooled estimates per cohort, and an overall summary. That’s the case tabviz() was built for: declarative columns, semantic row tokens, and pagination that lets the same spec render as a paginated HTML viewer and as a multi-page PDF without rewriting anything.
This page builds the canonical meta-analysis display step by step. We end up with a 70-row paginated forest plot that breaks across three pages by cohort.

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
A 70-study cardiovascular meta-analysis
Three cohorts, each with its own pooled summary plus an overall pooled diamond:


Code
set.seed(2026)

# Per-cohort row counts; each cohort gets one pooled summary at the end.
cohorts <- c("Phase III RCT", "Real-world evidence", "Registry")
counts  <- c(22, 26, 18)
n_data  <- sum(counts)

# Synthetic data — designed so each cohort lands in the budget when we
# paginate at 30 rows per page later.
trials <- data.frame(
  study  = c(sprintf("Trial %02d", seq_len(counts[1])),
             sprintf("RWE %02d",   seq_len(counts[2])),
             sprintf("Reg %02d",   seq_len(counts[3]))),
  cohort = rep(cohorts, times = counts),
  year   = sample(2010:2024, n_data, replace = TRUE),
  n      = sample(c(120, 240, 480, 960, 1900, 3800), n_data, replace = TRUE),
  hr     = round(rnorm(n_data, 0.92, 0.18), 2),
  se     = round(runif(n_data, 0.06, 0.20), 2)
)
trials$lower <- pmax(0.30, round(trials$hr - 1.96 * trials$se, 2))
trials$upper <- round(trials$hr + 1.96 * trials$se, 2)
trials$row_type <- "data"

# Pooled summary per cohort (diamond marker via row_type = "summary").
pooled <- trials |>
  group_by(cohort) |>
  summarize(
    study  = paste0("Pooled — ", dplyr::first(cohort)),
    year   = NA_integer_,
    n      = sum(n),
    hr     = round(exp(mean(log(hr))), 2),
    se     = NA_real_,
    lower  = round(exp(mean(log(hr)) - 1.96 * 0.04), 2),
    upper  = round(exp(mean(log(hr)) + 1.96 * 0.04), 2),
    row_type = "summary",
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Overall pooled diamond (one row at the very end of each cohort? overall here)
overall <- data.frame(
  study  = "Overall",
  cohort = "Registry",                       # last cohort in display order
  year   = NA_integer_,
  n      = sum(trials$n),
  hr     = round(exp(mean(log(trials$hr))), 2),
  se     = NA_real_,
  lower  = round(exp(mean(log(trials$hr)) - 1.96 * 0.03), 2),
  upper  = round(exp(mean(log(trials$hr)) + 1.96 * 0.03), 2),
  row_type = "summary"
)

# Assemble in display order: each cohort's data → its pooled → next cohort,
# with the overall pooled at the very bottom.
meta <- bind_rows(
  trials  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[1]),
  pooled  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[1]),
  trials  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[2]),
  pooled  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[2]),
  trials  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[3]),
  pooled  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[3]),
  overall
) |>
  mutate(
    is_significant = !is.na(upper) & upper < 1,   # entire CI below 1
    is_overall     = study == "Overall"
  )




Code
tabviz(
  meta,
  label = "study",
  group = "cohort",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("year", "Year", decimals = 0),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_type   = "row_type",
  row_accent = "is_significant",      # bold + theme accent on significant rows
  row_fill   = "is_overall",          # strong fill on the overall pooled row
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title    = "GLP-1 receptor agonists — pooled cardiovascular meta-analysis",
  subtitle = "70 studies across three cohorts, with cohort-level and overall pooled estimates",
  caption  = "Significant studies (CI strictly below 1) are highlighted with the accent token.",
  paginate = paginate_letter()
)












TipWhat’s happening on this plot




Three collapsible cohorts via group = "cohort" — click any header to fold a section.
Pooled diamonds at the bottom of each cohort and at the very end of the table, driven by row_type = "summary" (no diamond on data rows; pooled rows render as diamonds via the marker dispatch).
row_accent on the rows whose CI is strictly below 1 — bold + theme accent color. Themable: changing the theme restyles the highlight automatically.
row_fill on the single Overall pooled row — strong row-fill background with auto-contrasting foreground. The fifth semantic token in the RowCluster set; use it when a row needs to be the conclusion, not just stand out.
Pagination at 30 rows/page via paginate_letter(). Page count appears below the plot (“Page 1 of 3”); use the prev / next buttons or toggle continuous mode for scrolling.
Group integrity keeps each cohort intact across page breaks — keep_groups = TRUE is the default, so a cohort either fits on the current page or moves to the next.

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
Saving as a multi-page PDF
The same spec emits a multi-page PDF where each logical page becomes one PDF page:
save_plot(meta_table, "meta-analysis.pdf", width = 800)
# Multi-page PDF merged via qpdf::pdf_combine()
For the rendering pipeline (V8 → SVG → rsvg → qpdf), the qpdf-missing fallback (numbered series), and the per-render override surface, see Export → Pagination.

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
Adjusting the pagination for slides
The same spec at slide density (16 rows per page) — useful when each cohort needs its own slide deck:


Code
tabviz(meta, ..., paginate = paginate_slide())   # 16 rows / page


Or fold pagination off for a single-image PNG without warning:


Code
save_plot(meta_table, "wall.png", paginate = NULL)

text:

Clinical examples — shorter clinical forest patterns
LOTR — illustrative themes that exercise the full surface

title: Gallery
text: Live tabviz() widgets across nine pages. Every figure renders from the same data-frame-plus-columns API — sort columns, click filter funnels, drag rows or columns, double-click cells to edit, switch themes from the gear icon. Pick the page closest to what you want to build.

title: Gallery
text:
Flagship galleries


Long Meta-Analysis
70 studies across three cohorts with cohort-level and overall pooled summaries, paginated across three pages. Exercises the most-recently-shipped surface end-to-end: pagination, the five-token semantic surface (row_fill on the overall row, row_accent on significant), row_type = "summary" for pooled diamonds.


Domain Dashboards
Information-dense dashboards across sports analytics, customer feedback, portfolio performance, product metrics, airline ops, and climate science. Rings, pictograms, sparklines, heatmaps, badges — each lead picks the column types that fit its domain.


Compose Your Own Brand
End-to-end build of a fictional pharma brand (Acme Cardio Research) one named input at a time. Eight progressive steps from neutrals to a wrapped web_theme_acme() constructor, each with the rendered table at that stage so you see exactly which input controls what.


Pictograms & Rings
Every mode of col_pictogram() and col_ring() with realistic per-mode examples — count vs rating, range remapping, per-row glyph selection, layout = “stack”, auto status-palette thresholds, and the row-token cascade reaching into glyph cells. Closes with a six-row survey-feedback dashboard putting them all together.

title: Gallery
text:
More galleries


Clinical Examples
JAMA / Lancet-style forest plots, subgroup analyses, multi-arm dose-response, nested hierarchical groups.


Interactivity Tour
Live-behavior showcase: sort, filter, drag-and-drop, double-click editing, theme switcher, WYSIWYG SVG/PNG export.


Feature Showcases
Statistical models, distribution viz, conditional styling, fluent customization, publication templates, and the full-monty smoke test.


LOTR (Easter Egg)
Three editorial-register themes (dwarven, elvish, hobbit) with bespoke pictogram glyphs and tongue-in-cheek source data.

title: Gallery
text:
Example sources
Every gallery example is also available as a runnable R script in the package:
# List the curated gallery examples shipped with the package
list.files(system.file("examples", package = "tabviz"), pattern = "^gallery_")

# Run one
source(system.file("examples", "gallery_26_paginate_meta_analysis.R",
                   package = "tabviz"))

title: Gallery
text:
Where to read first



If you’re…
Start here
