Looking for the cheatsheet
One-page cheatsheet.

title: Clinical Examples
text: Publication-style meta-analyses and trial figures. Each example pairs a viz_forest() column with a textual col_interval() summary, then swaps the theme to match a target journal.

title: Clinical Examples
text:
JAMA-Style Forest Plot
Classic medical-journal style with clean typography:


Code
data(glp1_trials)

tabviz(
  glp1_trials,
  label = "study",
  group = "group",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes",
  subtitle = "Major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)"
)












TipTheme variations



The same data structure works with any theme. Try web_theme_lancet(), web_theme_jama(), or web_theme_dark() for different journal styles, or one of the LOTR presets (web_theme_dwarven() / web_theme_elvish() / web_theme_hobbit()) for non-journal contexts. See Feature Showcases for a visual comparison of all seven built-in themes.

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Multi-Arm Dose-Response
Compare three treatment arms across multiple endpoints using col_group to organize results:


Code
dose_response <- data.frame(
  endpoint = c("Overall Survival", "Progression-Free Survival",
               "Objective Response", "Quality of Life"),
  low_hr  = c(0.88, 0.82, 0.85, 0.92),
  low_lo  = c(0.74, 0.68, 0.70, 0.78),
  low_hi  = c(1.05, 0.99, 1.03, 1.08),
  mid_hr  = c(0.76, 0.71, 0.74, 0.83),
  mid_lo  = c(0.62, 0.58, 0.60, 0.69),
  mid_hi  = c(0.93, 0.87, 0.91, 1.00),
  high_hr = c(0.65, 0.60, 0.62, 0.75),
  high_lo = c(0.51, 0.46, 0.48, 0.61),
  high_hi = c(0.83, 0.78, 0.80, 0.92)
)

tabviz(dose_response, label = "endpoint",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(
      effects = list(
        effect_forest("low_hr", "low_lo", "low_hi", label = "Low Dose"),
        effect_forest("mid_hr", "mid_lo", "mid_hi", label = "Standard"),
        effect_forest("high_hr", "high_lo", "high_hi", label = "High Dose")
      ),
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"
    ),
    col_group("Low Dose",
      col_interval("low_hr", "low_lo", "low_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    col_group("Standard",
      col_interval("mid_hr", "mid_lo", "mid_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    col_group("High Dose",
      col_interval("high_hr", "high_lo", "high_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    )
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Dose-Response Analysis",
  subtitle = "Three-arm randomized trial across key endpoints"
)

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Annotated Forest Plot
Combine reference lines with annotations, direction-coded marker colors, and significance-based styling in a single comprehensive example:


Code
annotated_data <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data") |>
  mutate(
    sig_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"
    ),
    sig_shape = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "circle",
      lower > 1 ~ "diamond",
      TRUE ~ "square"
    )
  )

tabviz(annotated_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n", "N"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_gridlines = TRUE,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(1, label = "No effect", style = "solid"),
                 refline(0.85, label = "Pooled estimate", style = "dashed", color = "#2563eb")
               )),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  marker_color = "sig_color",
  marker_shape = "sig_shape",
  row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05,
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Cardiovascular Outcomes with Annotations",
  subtitle = "Direction-coded markers: green = significant benefit, gray = non-significant"
)

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Large-Scale Meta-Analysis
A stress test: 150 synthetic studies split into four regional panels via split_by. With shared_axis = TRUE, all panels share a single x-axis range, and the sidebar becomes a navigable tree — no endless scroll.


Code
set.seed(42)
n_studies <- 150
regions <- c("North America", "Europe", "Asia-Pacific", "Latin America")
region_pool <- sample(regions, n_studies,
                      replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.32, 0.30, 0.25, 0.13))

large_meta <- data.frame(
  study = sprintf("Study-%03d", seq_len(n_studies)),
  region = region_pool,
  n = round(pmax(60, rlnorm(n_studies, meanlog = 6.2, sdlog = 0.7))),
  hr = round(exp(rnorm(n_studies, mean = -0.18, sd = 0.22)), 3)
) |>
  mutate(
    se = 0.08 + 0.18 / sqrt(n / 200),
    lower = round(exp(log(hr) - 1.96 * se), 3),
    upper = round(exp(log(hr) + 1.96 * se), 3),
    weight = round(1 / se^2, 1),
    pvalue = round(2 * pnorm(-abs(log(hr) / se)), 4)
  ) |>
  select(-se)

tabviz(large_meta,
  label = "study",
  split_by = "region",
  shared_axis = TRUE,
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_bar("weight", "Weight"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_range = c(0.3, 2.5),
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)",
               annotations = list(
                 refline(1, label = "No effect", style = "solid"),
                 refline(0.83, label = "Pooled", style = "dashed", color = "#2563eb")
               )),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  marker_color = ~ case_when(
    upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
    lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  theme = web_theme_jama(),
  title = "Large-Scale Regional Meta-Analysis",
  subtitle = "150 synthetic studies across 4 regions, shared axis for cross-panel comparison",
  footnote = "Use the sidebar tree to navigate regions. Pooled estimate shown as blue dashed reference line."
)

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Nested Hierarchical Groups
Three levels of nesting (Region > Country > Site) give multi-center trial programs a compact, collapsible structure. Click any group header to collapse an entire branch and all its children.


Code
nested_data <- tibble(
  site = c(
    "Boston General", "Mass Eye & Ear", "Johns Hopkins", "Cleveland Clinic",
    "UCL Hospital", "Imperial College", "Charite Berlin", "LMU Munich",
    "Tokyo University", "Osaka Medical", "Peking Union", "Shanghai Ruijin"
  ),
  region = c(
    rep("americas", 4),
    rep("europe", 4),
    rep("asia_pacific", 4)
  ),
  country = c(
    "usa", "usa", "usa", "usa",
    "uk", "uk", "germany", "germany",
    "japan", "japan", "china", "china"
  ),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.68, 0.75, 0.71, 0.78, 0.82, 0.69, 0.74, 0.65, 0.70, 0.67, 0.72),
  lower = c(0.58, 0.52, 0.61, 0.56, 0.64, 0.68, 0.54, 0.59, 0.50, 0.55, 0.52, 0.57),
  upper = c(0.89, 0.89, 0.92, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99, 0.88, 0.93, 0.85, 0.89, 0.86, 0.91),
  n = c(245, 189, 312, 278, 156, 134, 298, 267, 445, 389, 512, 478)
)

tabviz(
  nested_data,
  label = "site",
  group = c("region", "country"),  # Hierarchical: region > country
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Nested Hierarchical Groups",
  subtitle = "Region > Country > Site (3 levels)",
  caption = "Click any group header to collapse that branch and all children"
)

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
text:
NotePre-release easter egg



The themes and examples on this page (web_theme_dwarven(), web_theme_elvish(), web_theme_hobbit(), plus a set of bespoke pictogram glyphs — used below: gem, ale_mug, pickaxe, sword, jar, pie, mushroom, plus more in glyph_registry_names()) are pre-release demonstrations. They are likely to be removed before CRAN submission and republished as standalone source on the package blog. Don’t build load-bearing dashboards on top of them.
These three tables exercise: the new col_pictogram() and col_ring() columns, threshold-driven col_badge() shapes, theme-declared web fonts (Cinzel, EB Garamond, Cormorant Garamond, IM Fell English, UnifrakturMaguntia), and bespoke SVG glyphs designed to read at 14 px.
A late evening, an unexpected guest, three ledgers from three peoples. Each table demonstrates a complete editorial-register theme — palette, typography, glyphs — paired with content that winks at its source material.

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
section: Erebor — Vein Yields, Hall of Records
text:
Erebor — Vein Yields, Hall of Records
Iron-clad ledger of every shaft beneath the Lonely Mountain. By order of Thorin son of Thráin. The Drum Chamber, abandon status, has been redacted from the King’s quarterly briefing; he requests no further inquiry.
Pictograms in use: gem (gold carts pulled this quarter, count mode), ale_mug (crew songs per shift, rating mode — five mugs because the Songs of Smithing are five), pickaxe (axes dulled, scaled to a 100-axe ceiling). The Balrog risk column uses a circular col_badge() with a status threshold scale; Drum Chamber’s score of 9 lands firmly in the negative bin.


Code
dwarven_data <- data.frame(
  shaft        = c("Front Hall", "Hall of Thrór", "Black Lode", "King's Vein",
                   "Mithril Seam", "Eastern Drift", "Deep Foundry", "Drum Chamber"),
  depth_m      = c(40, 280, 540, 720, 980, 1240, 1620, 2100),
  q3_tonnage   = c(142, 318, 412, 88, 24, 488, 612, 0),
  gold_carts   = c(3, 7, 6, 9, 4, 5, 2, 0),
  songs        = c(4, 5, 4, 5, 4, 3, 2, 0),
  axes_dulled  = c(12, 18, 15, 11, 22, 28, 35, 99),
  balrog_risk  = c(0L, 0L, 1L, 1L, 2L, 3L, 4L, 9L),
  status       = c("singing", "yielding", "yielding", "yielding",
                   "watch", "watch", "hazardous", "abandon"),
  yield_ratio  = c(1.18, 1.42, 1.31, 0.92, 0.78, 1.65, 1.84, 0.04),
  yield_lo     = c(0.95, 1.20, 1.10, 0.74, 0.55, 1.32, 1.42, 0.01),
  yield_hi     = c(1.42, 1.65, 1.55, 1.12, 1.10, 2.05, 2.40, 0.18)
)

tabviz(
  dwarven_data,
  label = "shaft",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("depth_m", header = "Depth (m)", decimals = 0),
    # Bar without `color` → picks up theme primary (hammered bronze).
    col_bar("q3_tonnage", header = "Q3 tonnage"),
    # Pictograms without `color` → pick up theme secondary (warm gold).
    # No hex literals in the call — the cascade does the work.
    col_pictogram("gold_carts", header = "Gold carts", glyph = "gem"),
    col_pictogram("songs", header = "Songs / shift",
                  glyph = "ale_mug", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("axes_dulled", header = "Axes dulled",
                  glyph = "pickaxe",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 5, min_value = 0, max_value = 100),
    # Threshold badge: 2 thresholds → status palette (positive/warning/negative).
    col_badge("balrog_risk", header = "Balrog risk",
              shape = "circle", thresholds = c(2, 5)),
    # Categorical badge with semantic VARIANTS instead of hex literals —
    # each label maps to a status token and pulls from theme.status.
    col_badge("status",
              variants = c("singing"   = "success",
                           "yielding"  = "info",
                           "watch"     = "warning",
                           "hazardous" = "error",
                           "abandon"   = "muted")),
    viz_forest(point = "yield_ratio", lower = "yield_lo", upper = "yield_hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Yield ratio (Q3 vs Q2)",
               show_header = FALSE)
  ),
  title = "Erebor — Vein Yields, Hall of Records",
  subtitle = "Iron-clad ledger of every shaft. T.A. 2941, Halimath.",
  theme = web_theme_dwarven()
)

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
section: Rivendell — Council of Elrond, Attendee Manifest
text:
Rivendell — Council of Elrond, Attendee Manifest
Eight peoples. One ring. The minutes are in Sindarin and refreshments are at the second eagle. Boromir spoke for forty-two minutes; the minutes are redacted at the request of the chair.
Three pictogram columns each tell a different micro-story: star for wisdom (a rating), sword for valor (also a rating; Boromir is unsurprisingly five-of-five), and person for times interrupted Boromir (a count — Gimli is leading the pack at six). The col_ring() for ring temptation is keyed to 0.33 and 0.66 with no explicit colors — the cascade resolves to the theme’s status palette (positive / warning / negative). Boromir’s 86 % lands well inside the negative arc.


Code
elvish_data <- data.frame(
  delegate    = c("Elrond Half-elven", "Gandalf the Grey", "Aragorn II Elessar",
                  "Legolas Greenleaf", "Gimli son of Glóin", "Boromir of Gondor",
                  "Frodo Baggins", "Samwise Gamgee"),
  race        = c("Elf", "Maia", "Man", "Elf", "Dwarf", "Man", "Hobbit", "Hobbit"),
  wisdom      = c(5, 5, 4, 3, 3, 2, 4, 3),
  valor       = c(4, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 5),
  tempt       = c(0.18, 0.32, 0.12, 0.08, 0.14, 0.86, 0.64, 0.22),
  words_12h   = c(1240, 890, 720, 320, 480, 1880, 380, 220),
  interrupted = c(0, 2, 1, 3, 6, 0, 1, 4),
  lembas      = c(4, 3, 6, 5, 12, 8, 9, 14),
  joins       = c("HOSTS", "YES", "YES", "YES", "YES", "YES", "BEARER", "GARDENER"),
  vote_for    = c(0.95, 1.05, 0.88, 1.10, 0.92, 1.32, 0.78, 0.85),
  vote_lo     = c(0.78, 0.85, 0.72, 0.92, 0.78, 1.10, 0.62, 0.68),
  vote_hi     = c(1.12, 1.25, 1.05, 1.30, 1.08, 1.55, 0.98, 1.05),
  # The Ring-bearer is the single row that fires the accent treatment
  # (theme.row.accent → twilight gold). Everything else stays at theme
  # default — no per-call hex literals.
  row_accent  = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(
  elvish_data,
  label = "delegate",
  row_accent = "row_accent",
  columns = list(
    # Race as plain text — five arbitrary categories don't fit any
    # semantic palette; hardcoding five hexes would defeat the showcase.
    col_text("race", header = "Race"),
    # Pictograms drop their hardcoded `color` — pick up theme secondary
    # (mithril silver). Frodo's row, marked accent, switches the glyphs
    # to twilight gold via the row.accent semantic override.
    col_pictogram("wisdom", header = "Wisdom",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("valor", header = "Valor",
                  glyph = "sword", max_glyphs = 5),
    # Ring tempt: 2 thresholds → 3 stops → status palette. Boromir's
    # 0.86 lands in the danger bin on its own; no per-call hex needed.
    col_ring("tempt", header = "Ring tempt",
             thresholds = c(0.33, 0.66)),
    col_bar("words_12h", header = "Words spoken (12h)"),
    col_pictogram("interrupted", header = "Interrupted Boromir",
                  glyph = "person"),
    col_pictogram("lembas", header = "Lembas eaten",
                  glyph = "leaf",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 8),
    # Joins as variants — info / success status, plus "default" → accent
    # for the Bearer (twilight gold), pinned to a single semantic axis.
    col_badge("joins", header = "Joins fellowship",
              variants = c("HOSTS"    = "info",
                           "YES"      = "success",
                           "BEARER"   = "default",
                           "GARDENER" = "success")),
    viz_forest(point = "vote_for", lower = "vote_lo", upper = "vote_hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Council vote weight",
               show_header = FALSE)
  ),
  title = "Rivendell — Council of Elrond, Attendee Manifest",
  subtitle = "Eight peoples. One ring. The minutes are in Sindarin.",
  theme = web_theme_elvish()
)

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
text:
Hobbiton — Pantry & Meal Audit, Halimath 1418
A respectable hobbit’s larder is never half-full; it is half-empty only between meals (of which there are seven). The Sackville-Baggins household, as always, is showing concerning second-breakfast deficiency. Pity tea-baskets have been dispatched.
jar (pantry stock, rating), pie (second-breakfast quality, rating), mushroom (mushroom haul, count) — three glyphs that ought to be permanently on every hobbit’s letterhead. The adventures badge uses outline mode (outline = TRUE) — a hobbit’s relationship with adventure is, after all, low-emphasis.


Code
hobbit_data <- data.frame(
  household       = c("Baggins of Bag End", "Took of Great Smials",
                      "Brandybuck of Brandy Hall", "Gamgee of Bagshot Row",
                      "Sackville-Baggins", "Cotton of Bywater",
                      "Boffin of Overhill", "Proudfoot of Westfarthing"),
  smial           = c("S.W. #1", "Tuckborough", "Buckland", "S.W. #3",
                      "Sackville", "Bywater", "Overhill", "Westfarthing"),
  pantry          = c(5, 5, 4, 4, 2, 4, 5, 4),
  pipeweed_lb     = c(3.2, 4.0, 2.6, 1.6, 0.5, 1.2, 2.8, 2.0),
  meals_per_day   = c(7.0, 7.0, 6.5, 7.0, 4.5, 6.5, 6.0, 6.5),
  second_brkfst   = c(5, 5, 5, 5, 1, 4, 4, 5),
  mushrooms       = c(8, 12, 6, 9, 0, 4, 5, 7),
  adventures      = c("YES (ONE)", "MANY", "YES (ONE)", "YES (ONE)",
                      "NONE", "NONE", "NONE", "NONE"),
  satiation       = c(1.05, 1.12, 0.98, 1.02, 0.75, 0.95, 1.08, 0.92),
  satiation_lo    = c(0.92, 1.00, 0.85, 0.88, 0.62, 0.82, 0.95, 0.78),
  satiation_hi    = c(1.18, 1.25, 1.10, 1.15, 0.88, 1.08, 1.20, 1.05),
  # Adventurous Tooks pop in accent (autumn rust); the Sackville-Bagginses
  # fade in muted (problematic relatives). Everything else at theme default.
  row_accent      = c(FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE),
  row_muted       = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(
  hobbit_data,
  label = "household",
  row_accent = "row_accent",
  row_muted  = "row_muted",
  columns = list(
    col_text("smial", header = "Smial"),
    # Pictograms without `color` → theme secondary (garden green —
    # Sam's vegetables). Took's row picks up accent override automatically.
    col_pictogram("pantry", header = "Pantry fill",
                  glyph = "jar", max_glyphs = 5),
    # Bar without `color` → theme primary (warm clay — Bag End brick).
    col_bar("pipeweed_lb", header = "Pipe-weed (lb)"),
    col_numeric("meals_per_day", header = "Meals/day", decimals = 1),
    col_pictogram("second_brkfst", header = "2nd brkfst",
                  glyph = "pie", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("mushrooms", header = "Mushrooms",
                  glyph = "mushroom",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 8),
    # Adventures via variants — success for the typical one-time hobbit
    # jaunt, default → accent for the unusual MANY (the Tooks), muted for
    # NONE.
    col_badge("adventures",
              variants = c("YES (ONE)" = "success",
                           "MANY"      = "default",
                           "NONE"      = "muted"),
              outline = TRUE),
    viz_forest(point = "satiation", lower = "satiation_lo", upper = "satiation_hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Satiation index",
               show_header = FALSE)
  ),
  title = "Hobbiton — Pantry & Meal Audit, Halimath 1418",
  subtitle = "A respectable hobbit's larder is never half-full.",
  theme = web_theme_hobbit()
)

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
text:
Bring your own theme
The three themes above are picked up automatically by the in-widget theme switcher (look for the lotr tab) when enable_themes = "default" — package_themes() returns them under the lotr category. To use one explicitly:
tabviz(
  data, label = "x",
  columns = list(...),
  theme = web_theme_dwarven()   # or _elvish() or _hobbit()
)
The themes declare their Google Fonts via web_fonts = list(web_font(family, url), …). The widget injects <link rel="stylesheet"> tags on mount and dedupes across multiple widgets on the same page. rsvg/PNG export uses the system font fallback (Georgia, serif) since rsvg does not fetch web fonts at render time — install the family locally for high-fidelity offline export.
The bespoke pictogram glyphs (pickaxe, harp, mushroom, …) live in glyph_registry_names() alongside the core set (person, dot, star, …) and are usable from any theme — col_pictogram(field, glyph = "harp") works on the JAMA preset just as well, it just doesn’t match.

title: Data Flow
text: Trace one row of a data.frame from R into the rendered Svelte widget. This page explains how column specs resolve, when formula expressions evaluate, and how the result becomes JSON for the frontend.

title: Data Flow
text:
The Pipeline





flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        A["data.frame"]
    end

    subgraph Processing
        B["Column Resolution"]
        C["Style Evaluation"]
        D["JSON Serialization"]
    end

    subgraph Output
        E["Svelte Component"]
        F["Interactive Widget"]
    end

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F

    style D fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff3e00,color:#fff







Stage 1: Column Resolution
tabviz maps your column specifications to data columns:


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85),
  lo = c(0.55, 0.70),
  hi = c(0.95, 1.03)
)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",  # Maps to data$study
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")  # Maps to data$hr, data$lo, data$hi
  )
)


At this stage: - String arguments become column lookups - Missing columns trigger errors - Data types are validated


Stage 2: Style Evaluation
Formula expressions are evaluated against your data:


Code
tabviz(data,
  ...,
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05,  # Evaluates for each row
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
)


The formula ~ pval < 0.05 becomes a vector of TRUE/FALSE values—one per row.


Stage 3: JSON Serialization
The configured specification converts to JSON for the Svelte frontend. serialize_spec() (in R/utils-serialize.R) emits a top-level object with these keys:
{
  "data":          { "rows": [...], "groups": [...], "summaries": [...] },
  "columns":       [...],
  "extraColumns":  [...],
  "theme":         { ... },
  "interaction":   { ... },
  "labels":        { "title": ..., "subtitle": ..., ... },
  "paginate":      { "rows": 30, "pages": [...], "nPages": 3, ... },  // when set
  "layout":        { "plotWidth": "auto" }
}
Per-row styling lives inside each row’s object, not in a parallel array — so a row_bold flag computed by ~ pval < 0.05 lands on row.style.bold:
// One element of data.rows
{
  "id":       "row_1",
  "label":    "Smith 2020",
  "groupId":  null,
  "metadata": { "hr": 0.72, "lower": 0.55, "upper": 0.95, "is_sig": true },
  "style":    { "bold": true }
}
When paginate = paginate_spec(...) is set, breakpoints are computed once R-side over the same flattened display rows the frontend renders, then shipped as 0-based row ranges so the viewer and the PDF export agree on page boundaries:
"paginate": {
  "rows":         30,
  "breakOn":      "split",
  "keepGroups":   true,
  "pageLabel":    "x_of_y",
  "pages":        [{ "startIdx": 0,  "endIdx": 29 },
                   { "startIdx": 30, "endIdx": 59 }],
  "nPages":       2
}


Stage 4: Svelte Rendering
The Svelte component receives the JSON and renders the interactive table with: - Sortable columns (default; opt out per column with sortable = FALSE) - Collapsible groups - Hover tooltips (opt-in via web_interaction(tooltip_fields = ...)) - Page controls when paginate is set - Static export to SVG / PNG / PDF

title: Data Flow
text:
Data Requirements

Required Structure
Your data.frame needs: - One row per item to display - Columns referenced by your column specifications


Code
# Minimal forest plot data
meta_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Smith 2020", "Jones 2021"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03)
)



Row kinds via row_type
By default every row is a data row with a marker. Pass row_type = "<col>" to tabviz() (with a column whose values are "data", "header", "summary", or "spacer") to mix in:








row_type
Behavior
Marker




"data"
Standard row
Point with CI line


"header"
Section header (point/lower/upper should be NA)
None


"summary"
Pooled / subtotal row
Diamond


"spacer"
Empty separator (point/lower/upper should be NA)
None



See Row Styling for the full specification.



Column Types
col_*() family covers cell content; viz_*() family adds shared-axis charts in their own column. tabviz infers a field_category from each data column (numeric, integer, string, logical, date, array-numeric, other) which the interactive column picker uses to filter compatible types.








R Type
Cell columns (col_*)
Visualization columns (viz_*)




character
col_text, col_label, col_badge, col_icon, col_reference
—


numeric
col_numeric, col_n, col_percent, col_currency, col_pvalue, col_progress, col_heatmap, col_stars, col_pictogram, col_ring, col_bar
viz_bar (one value), viz_forest (point + CI), viz_boxplot / viz_violin (per-row arrays via list-columns or pre-computed stats)


integer
col_n
viz_bar


logical
Row / cell semantic flags
— (read indirectly via row_* / cell style_*)


date
col_date
—


list of numeric
col_sparkline
viz_boxplot (raw or quartiles), viz_violin (KDE)


paired numeric
col_interval, col_events, col_range
—





Missing Values
NA handling varies by column type:



Context
Behavior




Forest points
Row displays as label-only (no marker)


Numeric columns
Displays as empty cell


Text columns
Displays as empty cell


Row styling
NA rows skip that style

title: Data Flow
text:
Grouping Data
Grouping creates collapsible sections:





flowchart TD
    A["Flat data"] --> B["group = 'category'"]
    B --> C["Grouped display"]

    subgraph "Grouped display"
        D["Group A Header"]
        E["  Row 1"]
        F["  Row 2"]
        G["Group B Header"]
        H["  Row 3"]
    end







Single-Level Grouping


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C", "Trial D"),
  category = c("Drug X", "Drug X", "Drug Y", "Drug Y"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91, 0.78),
  lo = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75, 0.60),
  hi = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10, 1.00)
)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  group = "category",  # Groups by Drug X, Drug Y
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"))
)




Hierarchical Grouping


Code
data$region <- c("North", "North", "South", "South")

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  group = c("region", "category"),  # Region > Category hierarchy
  columns = list(viz_forest(...))
)

title: Data Flow
text:
Styling Data Flow
Styles resolve in order of specificity:





flowchart TD
    A["Theme defaults"] --> B["Row styling"]
    B --> C["Cell styling"]
    C --> D["Marker styling"]
    D --> E["Final appearance"]

    style E fill:#059669,color:#fff






See Styling Cascade for details.

title: Data Flow
text:
Common Patterns

Pre-computing Style Columns


Code
library(dplyr)

styled_data <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    is_significant = upper < 1,
    direction_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",  # Green: benefit
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",  # Red: harm
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"        # Gray: NS
    )
  )

tabviz(styled_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_bold = "is_significant",
  marker_color = "direction_color"
)








Using Formula Expressions
Equivalent approach without pre-computing:


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_bold = ~ upper < 1,
  marker_color = ~ case_when(
    upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
    lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  )
)






Both approaches produce identical output. Use formulas for concise one-offs; use pre-computed columns when reusing the same logic.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Note



Living document. The cascade reference in Styling Cascade tells you what the tokens are. This document tells you why. Read this before proposing a fourth identity tier, a “trend” slot, a charm recipe, or otherwise expanding the surface area.
Decisions get appended to the Decision log at the bottom — never rewritten — so future maintainers can diagnose drift against original intent.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Why this document exists
A cascade specification is a what. It enumerates inputs, derived tokens, component bindings, and the formulas that connect them. It can’t tell you why the inputs are those inputs and not different ones, or why the cascade ends at three tiers and not four, or why a class of feature requests has been considered and rejected over and over again.
This document is the why companion. It’s also a record: every substantive shape decision is dated and reasoned through here, so when someone proposes re-litigating one of them, the prior conversation is on the page.
Three audiences:

Future contributors evaluating a feature request that touches the cascade. Read the anti-patterns section first.
Theme authors building a new preset. The theme authoring recipe is the practical starting point.
Curious users who want to know what they’re using. The two-knob principle and the three-axis contract are the entry points.

If you want the formal contract, go to Styling Cascade.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Publication-quality as a constraint
tabviz’s product wedge is publication-quality output. Not “looks professional,” not “modern aesthetic” — publication-quality: tables that could appear unmodified in a peer-reviewed journal article, a regulatory submission, an annual report.
This is a constraint, not a label. Publication-quality is downstream of a specific aesthetic discipline:

Restraint over expressivity. A fourth color knob, an emoji recipe, a decorative glyph slot — each one is an opportunity for a user to make their table louder than it should be.
Fewer dimensions, used well. Two coordinated colors that read as intentional beat five independent colors that read as crowded.
Chrome stays neutral. Banding, hairlines, gridlines, axis text — these are scaffolding. Pull them toward the brand at low strength for cohesion; never at high strength for prominence.
Defaults that work everywhere. A user who pins primary and writes one line of R should get a table that wouldn’t look out of place in The Lancet. Anything that requires editorial intervention to look acceptable has failed.

Most of the choices documented below are downstream of this single frame. If you find yourself defending an addition with “but the user could choose to use it well,” check that frame again. Publication-quality is what most users get by default, not what the most disciplined users can achieve with effort.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
The two-knob principle
The theme contract is two knobs: identity and engagement.
Identity is the brand axis — the tone the table opens with, the tone it returns to under any structural emphasis. It mirrors down the chain: secondary defaults to primary unless the author explicitly pins a second color. In a mono theme (most journals), there is one identity color; in a two-color editorial theme (LoTR-style), there are two.
Engagement is the accent — the color that fires when something extra is happening. Hover. Selected. A semantic row callout. The status.info fallback. The selection edge bar. Accent is orthogonal to identity; it never enters the mirror chain. It’s also reserved: it does not land on default content (badges, glyph fills, ornament). Those belong on identity, where they coordinate with the rest of the table.
Why two knobs and not more:

A single identity knob makes mono themes trivial: pin primary, done.
A second identity knob (secondary, mirroring primary) makes two-color editorial themes coherent without adding any complexity to mono themes.
A separate engagement knob (accent) keeps the “look here” signal semantically distinct from the brand surface, so a hovered row can’t read the same color as a regular badge two rows down.
Adding any knob beyond these reopens the question of which knob a new feature should land on. The contract collapses.

Why accent is reserved for engagement:

When accent doubled as both “the engagement color” and “the default fill for content-bearing glyph columns” (badges, pictograms, rings, stars), the same hue did two unrelated jobs and the emphasis signal lost its meaning. A hovered row read the same color as a regular badge. The 2026-04-28 redesign fixed this: glyph col defaults moved to the secondary identity axis (which mirrors primary in mono themes — visually identical to before for journal users — and lights up secondary in two-color themes).
The mental model “identity for what is, accent for what’s happening” is the entire reason themes feel coherent.

If a new role doesn’t cleanly map to one of these two axes, the answer is almost always to refine the role’s purpose, not to add a third axis.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
The three-axis contract
Identity is two tiers, but the actual roles are three. Each axis owns exactly one job:








Axis
Owns
Default behavior




primary (+ primary_deep)
Prominence: title text, bold leaf-header band, container border, series[0] anchor
Required input. _deep auto-darkens by 15% (OKLCH) if NA.


secondary (+ secondary_deep)
Structure: bold col-group band, row-group L1/L2/L3 bg, glyph col defaults (badge/pictogram/ring/stars), AND chrome texture (surface.muted, divider.subtle/strong, alt-row banding, gridline, axis/tick)
NA mirrors primary. secondary_deep mirrors primary_deep when secondary is NA; auto-darkens otherwise.


accent (+ accent_deep)
Engagement: hover, selected, row.accent.fg, accent.tint_subtle/medium, semantic.fill, selection edge color, status.info fallback
Required input. Orthogonal — never enters the identity mirror chain.



The single rule for every theme: pin what’s distinct, mirror the rest. Mono themes pin primary only. Two-color editorial themes pin primary + secondary. Accent is always pinned (engagement is never silent).
The structural axis is doing the most work. In mono themes, you don’t notice — secondary mirrors primary, so chrome and groupings read as primary-tinted variants of the canvas. In two-color themes, secondary lights up and you get coordinated movement across:

The bold column-group band reads in secondary
Row-group bars read in a 16% (or 24% under tint/bold header) mix of secondary into surface
Cell hairlines and dividers carry a faint secondary tint
Alt-row banding picks up the same secondary chrome
Glyph cells (badges, rings, stars, pictograms) default to secondary

All from one pin. No per-theme overrides. That’s the dividend the contract pays.

title: Theming Manifesto
section: Why tertiary was added (2026-04-28) and removed (2026-04-29)
text:
Why tertiary was added (2026-04-28) and removed (2026-04-29)
Honest record. Tertiary was a 24-hour decision and the postmortem teaches the most important lesson in this document.

The 2026-04-28 decision
The previous architecture was brand / brand_deep + accent. Editorial themes (LoTR-style demos) wanted a second color for chrome texture — specifically, the umber/moss/wheat tones that distinguish the three LoTR themes from each other. Without a knob for it, theme authors were either hardcoding hex values into every chrome token (high maintenance, drifts on input change) or accepting a primary-tinted chrome that looked wrong against the bold identity bands.
The proposal: rename brand → primary, add secondary for column-group bg + row-group bg + glyph defaults, add tertiary for chrome texture (surface.muted, dividers, banding, gridlines, axis/tick). Three identity tiers, each mirroring up the chain. Accent stays orthogonal.
This shipped to main on 2026-04-28. Memory entry saved. Themes migrated. Validation green.


The 2026-04-29 reversal
Within 24 hours, two problems surfaced when stress-testing editorial themes:

Three colors usually communicate worse than two. When designers could pin three identity hues, they did — even when the third hue’s only job was “tint the chrome differently from the band.” The result was tables with too much going on. Editorial restraint requires that the architecture not invite the third pin in the first place.
Chrome wants to read structurally, not ornamentally. Routing chrome through its own knob made it feel like a third identity color (because it was). Folding chrome onto the secondary axis — same axis that owns col-group bg, row-group bg, glyph defaults — collapsed it into “the structural color” instead of “the chrome color.” The mental model simplified: secondary is everything that supports the primary band.

The reversal: tertiary and tertiary_deep removed entirely. Chrome texture migrated onto secondary_deep at the same mix strengths. Mono themes are visually unchanged (secondary mirrors primary). Two-color themes get one structural axis instead of two.
The lesson:

Adding expressivity is reversible only if you add it carefully. Tertiary was added with full validation, full migration, full memory trail — which made the reversal cheap. A messier addition would have cost weeks.
A 24-hour postmortem on a fresh decision is healthier than living with the wrong shape for a quarter. The reversal entry is in this log not as embarrassment but as evidence that the loop is short.
The right number of identity tiers is two. Probably forever.

The LoTR themes lost nothing essential. Dwarven kept bronze (primary) + gold (secondary, now tinting chrome too) + ember (accent). The third umber pin folded into the gold cascade — and the chrome in Dwarven now reads as warm-burnished-metallurgy, which is more on-theme than the original cool umber against warm bronze ever was.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Mirror chain semantics
The identity chain is a single rule:

If secondary is NA at construction, it mirrors primary during resolution. If secondary_deep is NA, it mirrors primary_deep (when secondary itself is NA) or auto-darkens from secondary (when secondary is pinned).

A few things to know about how this behaves:
Mirroring happens at resolve time, not at construction. ThemeInputs(primary = "#0891b2") stores secondary = NA literally; the NA only resolves to "#0891b2" after resolve_theme() has run. This means re-resolving an already-resolved theme is idempotent: the resolver only writes into NA-valued fields, so a previously-mirrored secondary stays at primary’s value, and a pinned secondary stays at the user’s pin.
Pinning a tier breaks the mirror for that tier and downstream tiers. If you pin secondary, secondary_deep no longer follows primary_deep — it auto-derives from your pinned secondary instead. This is the intentional break: the moment you say “I want a second color,” the deep companion tracks the second color, not the first.
Accent never mirrors. It’s a required input precisely because engagement is never silent. Even in a mono theme, accent is a deliberate choice (often a deliberate counterpoint — Cochrane pairs cool teal primary with warm coral accent specifically because the contrast lights up the engagement signal).
The mirror is not just visual. It propagates through every derived token. In a mono theme, divider.strong reads secondary_deep which mirrors primary_deep which auto-darkens primary — a single edit to primary cascades through dividers, surface.muted, banding, gridlines, all of it. This is the dividend that justifies the implicit-NA design over having theme authors restate primary in every cascade slot.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Cascade tiers
Three tiers, each with one job:

T1 — Inputs. What the customer chooses. primary, secondary, accent, plus _deep companions, plus slot_style (enum), header_style (enum), neutral ramp, status colors, series anchors, fonts. ~15 fields. This is the only tier most users edit directly.
T2 — Semantic roles. Derived tokens with named semantics: surface.base/muted/raised, content.primary/secondary/muted/inverse, divider.subtle/strong, accent.default/muted/tint_subtle/tint_medium, semantic.fill, status.*, plus per-slot SlotBundle for series marks. The resolver fills these from T1 via OKLCH formulas; users override individual fields to pin a tone.
T3 — Component bindings. Per-cluster overrides: header.{light, tint, bold}.{bg, fg, rule}, column_group.*, row_group.L1/L2/L3.bg, cell.border, plot.axis_line/tick_mark/gridline/reference. These default to T2 values with cluster-local computations (e.g., bold rule is a 40% mix of inverse against the band’s own bg). Users can pin individual leaves; the rest cascade from T2.

Three tiers of overrides means three chances for a user to break a required relationship. Construction-time validation (validate_resolved_theme) catches the obvious failures (bold-band contrast, body-text contrast) at theme construction — not at render — so the error message points at the source of the bad pin, not at a downstream symptom. WCAG AA Large (3.0) for chrome bold text; AA Normal (4.5) for body text. Tests can opt out with .validate = FALSE.
The cascade is fully introspectable from R:


Code
inspect_resolved(web_theme_cochrane(), "header.bold.bg")
#> $path: "header.bold.bg"
#> $value: "#006B9C"
#> $cascade: c("inputs$primary_deep", "inputs$primary")
#> $derivation: "inputs$primary_deep (auto: oklch_darken(primary, 0.15))"


The same provenance feeds the widget’s Theme tab tooltips — single source of truth for “where did this hex come from?”.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Anti-patterns
These are not “things we haven’t gotten around to.” These are things tabviz will refuse to do. If you encounter one in a feature request, push back.

Don’t add a fourth identity tier (or revive tertiary)
Two-knob is canonical. Tertiary was added on 2026-04-28 and removed on 2026-04-29 — see the postmortem. Any future proposal to add a third identity color must either (a) demonstrate a use case that the secondary mirror chain genuinely cannot serve, or (b) explain why the prior reversal’s reasoning no longer applies. Aesthetic preference for three colors is not sufficient — the reversal happened because designers reached for three when two would have read better.


Don’t introduce slot-typed Tier 1 anchors
A previous architectural proposal added slot.trend, slot.distribution, slot.range, etc. as Tier 1 anchors keyed to recipe types. This was considered and retracted. The two-knob model handles every case the slot-typed proposal addressed (via accent-tint and identity-deep derivations). Reintroducing slot-typed anchors adds dimensionality that the cascade has to thread through every component binding for near-zero practical gain.


Don’t mix accent into default content
Accent owns engagement. Hover, selected, callouts, status.info, semantic row treatments. It does not own:

Default badge fills
Glyph cell defaults (rings, stars, pictograms)
Ornament that fires by default

Those belong on the secondary identity axis. The 2026-04-28 redesign documented this explicitly: glyph col defaults moved from accent to secondary precisely so the engagement signal stays meaningful. If you find yourself wanting accent on a content surface that’s always rendered, the correct fix is almost certainly identity, not accent.


Don’t tint chrome toward accent
Chrome (banding, dividers, gridlines, axis text) is structural. It tints toward secondary_deep — which in mono themes mirrors primary_deep, so chrome stays cohesive with the brand. Tinting chrome toward accent makes hovered/selected rows merge into the chrome band. This was wrong before the cascade rework and would still be wrong after.


Don’t add charm recipes
Pictogram arrays, emoji marks, decorative glyph recipes, isotype-style visualizations. These are charming in stress-test demos but distract from publication-quality positioning. The recipe library is intentionally small: it grows when a serious publication-quality use case demonstrably isn’t covered (e.g., gauge-against-target, dots-of-N, stacked-bar proportion compositions are on the roadmap), not when a demo would be fun.
The boundary check: would this recipe appear in The Lancet? If no, hold the line.


Don’t bypass the cascade
Every now and then a feature request asks for a theme override that “skips” the cascade — a one-off color that doesn’t flow through any of the derivations, doesn’t participate in mirroring, can’t be inspected. This signal almost always means the cascade is missing a case. The right response is to extend the cascade (add the slot to T2 or T3, document its derivation, run validation), not to introduce a side door.
If genuinely no T1/T2/T3 location fits, that’s a decision-quality moment. Stop and re-examine the role’s semantics before committing to a new slot.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Authoring a new theme
The recipe is short. Most themes need only Tier 1 inputs.
Mono theme (the journal default — Cochrane, Lancet, JAMA fall here):


Code
my_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "my_journal",
  inputs = list(
    primary = "#0F4C81",     # identity hero
    accent  = "#C8553D",     # warm engagement counterpoint
    series_anchors = c("#0F4C81", "#C8553D", "#5C8A3F", "#7E5A99", "#D49A3A"),
    font_body = "Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif"
  )
)


That’s it. Secondary mirrors primary; chrome reads as primary-tinted; glyph defaults read primary; engagement (hover/selected/info) reads accent. The contract gives you a coherent table with two pinned values.
Two-color editorial theme (LoTR-style):


Code
my_editorial <- web_theme(
  name = "my_editorial",
  inputs = list(
    primary   = "#7A4E22",   # bronze
    secondary = "#D4A955",   # gold — drives col-group bands, row-group bars,
                             #        glyph defaults, AND chrome texture
    accent    = "#E0673A",   # ember — hover, selected, status.info
    series_anchors = c("#D4A955", "#A8A8A0", "#B86C42", "#7A4E22", "#5C7AA0"),
    font_body = "'EB Garamond', Georgia, serif"
  ),
  variants = list(header_style = "bold")
)


One additional pin (secondary) and one variant flip (header_style) — the rest cascades.
Verifying your theme:


Code
# Check what a specific leaf resolved to + why.
inspect_resolved(my_theme, "header.bold.bg")
inspect_resolved(my_theme, "divider.strong")
inspect_resolved(my_theme, "row_group.L1.bg")

# Construction-time validation runs automatically — broken contrast
# aborts at web_theme() with a structured error pointing at the leaf.
# To bypass for experimental / synthetic colors:
my_test <- web_theme(..., .validate = FALSE)


What to override at T2/T3 (and when):

Override at T1 first. 90% of themes never need to touch T2 or T3.
Override at T2 when a family of derived tokens needs adjustment — e.g., divider.strong to pin the rule color across header/group/axis.
Override at T3 only for cluster-specific tweaks — e.g., a particular preset wants the bold-mode header band darker than its primary_deep would give.

The wider you cast the override, the higher up the cascade you should pin it. T3 overrides are bandages; T1 overrides are intent.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Open questions
The architecture is mature enough that big redesigns are unlikely. Outstanding questions are tactical:

Recipe fallbacks at narrow widths. Whether violins below a width threshold should automatically degrade to density strip / boxplot. Could be guidance or runtime fallback. No current opinion.
Slot.style additional variants. Currently three values (fill_with_darker_stroke, flat_fill, outlined). A “stroke_only” variant has been suggested for very dense plots. Not yet implemented; awaiting a real-world need.
Header tint strength. The tint variant is a 12% mix of primary_deep into surface.base. This was chosen by eye; whether 10% or 15% reads better is taste-dependent and may benefit from per-theme override.

When new questions arise, append to the Decision log. When questions get resolved, move them out of this section and into the log entry that resolved them.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Decision log
Append-only. Newest first. Each entry: date, decision, alternatives considered, reasoning. Future maintainers should append, not rewrite.

2026-04-29 — Tertiary removed; chrome migrates to secondary
Decision. Drop tertiary and tertiary_deep from ThemeInputs. Chrome texture (surface.muted, divider.subtle/strong, alt-row banding, gridline, axis/tick) now reads from secondary_deep at the same mix strengths. Identity is two tiers (primary + secondary mirror chain) plus orthogonal accent.
Alternatives considered. - Keep three tiers, lower the bar for what tertiary owns. Rejected: doesn’t solve the “designers reach for three colors” problem. - Add a separate chrome_tint knob (not in identity chain). Rejected: same expressivity creep with extra surface area. - Keep three tiers but make tertiary “advanced-only” (hidden from default panels). Rejected: API surface stays bloated; documentation cost is high; the prompt to use three colors is structural, not UI.
Reasoning. See the postmortem. Two-knob is canonical. Bundled with this rework: slot_style lifted to T1; header_style expanded with tint variant; row-group L1 bg gains header_style-driven subtle/strong strength; construction-time contrast validation; inspect_resolved() introspection API; 7 presets migrated (LoTR trio rebalanced as 2-color).
Migration cost. ~3 hours of focused refactoring. Caught a real bug along the way: dark theme’s auto-derived primary_deep (#5C85C8) had 2.57:1 contrast against its inverse text — pinned darker to #2E5290 (5.33:1).


2026-04-28 — Tertiary added (later reversed)
Decision. Add tertiary and tertiary_deep to ThemeInputs. Three identity tiers in a mirror chain (primary → secondary → tertiary). Tertiary owns chrome texture.
Reasoning at the time. Editorial themes wanted a third color for chrome texture distinct from the structural secondary; pinning it kept chrome cohesive without per-theme hardcoded hex values.
Reversed within 24 hours. See the 2026-04-29 entry above.


2026-04-28 — Accent reserved for layered emphasis
Decision. Glyph col defaults (badge, pictogram, ring, stars) move from accent to secondary identity. Accent is reserved strictly for layered emphasis: roles that fire when something extra is happening (hover, selected, semantic row callouts, selection edge, status.info fallback).
Reasoning. When accent doubled as both “the engagement color” and “the default fill for content-bearing glyph columns,” the same hue did two unrelated jobs and the emphasis signal lost meaning — a hovered row read the same color as a regular badge two rows down.
This decision survives the 2026-04-29 tertiary reversal unchanged. It’s the most important constraint on what accent does.


Earlier — Slot-typed Tier 1 anchors retracted
Decision. Do not introduce slot.trend, slot.distribution, slot.range, etc. as Tier 1 anchors keyed to recipe types.
Reasoning. The two-knob theme architecture (identity + accent with cascade-pinning + element overrides) handles the cases the slot-typed proposal addressed. Reintroducing slot-typed anchors adds dimensionality that the cascade has to thread through every component binding for near-zero practical gain.
This is documented in engineering-priorities.md §“Anti-priorities” as a binding constraint on future architecture work.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
The seven shipped presets
Preset constructors that ship with the package. The four journal presets are restraint-led; the three LOTR presets exercise the full surface (display fonts, secondary tier, distinctive accents) and are useful as worked examples of the manifesto’s principles in non-journal contexts.








Preset
Source
Aesthetic discipline




web_theme_cochrane()
R/themes.R
Package default; teal + warm coral, comfortable density


web_theme_lancet()
R/themes.R
Lancet navy + old-gold, Georgia serif


web_theme_jama()
R/themes.R
Black-and-white, ultra-compact, strong dividers


web_theme_dark()
R/themes.R
Catppuccin Mocha-inspired


web_theme_dwarven()
R/themes-lotr.R
Stone neutrals, forge-glow accent, broad serif display


web_theme_elvish()
R/themes-lotr.R
Twilight surface, mithril silvers, calligraphic serif


web_theme_hobbit()
R/themes-lotr.R
Parchment + green, hand-set body face, hearth accent



package_themes() returns these as a 2-level named list (journals / lotr); set_theme(spec, "<name>") switches by string name.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
See also

Styling Cascade — the formal contract (what)
?inspect_resolved — the introspection API used in examples above
R/utils-theme-validate.R — construction-time validation invariants
R/utils-theme-resolve.R — the resolver that materializes T2/T3
R/themes.R, R/themes-lotr.R — preset themes as canonical examples

title: The Fluent API
text: tabviz exposes a single, unified fluent API for every kind of modification you’d want to make — styling, column structure, row ordering, data, theme, zoom, and cell edits. Every verb returns the modified object and dispatches on input type, so the same call works on a static WebSpec, a rendered htmlwidget, or a live Shiny proxy.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Two ways to style
Styling can live on the tabviz() call or come from fluent modifiers chained after it. Both are fully supported and produce identical output. Pick based on intent:







Use top-level args (tabviz(...))
Use fluent modifiers (|> set_*())




One-shot construction
Post-hoc updates to an existing plot


Everything known upfront
Building variants from a single base


Concise static plots
Live updates inside a Shiny tabviz_proxy()





Code
# Top-level: everything in one call
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold",
  marker_color = "effect_color",
  theme = web_theme_jama()
)

# Fluent: same result, modifier-driven
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
) |>
  set_row_style(type = "rtype", bold = "rbold") |>
  set_marker_style(color = "effect_color") |>
  set_theme("jama")


For Shiny proxies the choice is made for you — incremental updates must go through fluent verbs.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Static vs Proxy Dispatch
Every fluent verb accepts three kinds of input and behaves accordingly:







Input type
What happens




WebSpec (from tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE))
Returns a new WebSpec with the change applied in R.


htmlwidget (from tabviz(...))
Returns a new widget with the change baked into its spec.


tabviz_proxy (from tabviz_proxy("id"))
Sends a custom message to the running widget in Shiny — no re-render.



# Static WebSpec
spec <- tabviz(data, label = "study", .spec_only = TRUE) |>
  sort_rows("hr", "desc") |>
  add_column(col_numeric("n", "N"), after = "study")

# htmlwidget — same verbs
widget <- tabviz(data, ...) |>
  set_theme("jama") |>
  resize_column("hr", width = 140)

# Shiny proxy — same verbs
observeEvent(input$sort_hr, {
  tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
    sort_rows("hr", "desc")
})
A handful of verbs are proxy-only because they depend on row identity post-render (set_cell, set_row_label, clear_edits). On a static input they emit a warning and leave the object unchanged.

title: The Fluent API
text:
The Fluent Pattern
Every verb returns the modified object, so calls chain:
tabviz(data, ...) |>     # Create plot
  set_row_style(...) |>  # Modify rows
  add_column(...) |>     # Add a column
  sort_rows(...) |>      # Reorder rows
  set_theme(...)         # Apply theme






NoteImmutability



Each verb returns a new object; the input is unchanged. Safe to derive multiple variants from a single base:
base <- tabviz(data, ...)
jama   <- base |> set_theme("jama")
lancet <- base |> set_theme("lancet")

title: The Fluent API
text:
Styling Verbs
All styling verbs accept either column names (strings) or formula expressions (~ ...):
# Using column names (pre-computed)
plot |> set_row_style(bold = "is_significant")

# Using formula expressions (computed on the fly)
plot |> set_row_style(bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05)

# Mix both
plot |> set_marker_style(
  color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  shape = "marker_shape_column"
)
See Row Styling and Cell Styling for formula details.

set_row_style()
Row-level styling. Your primary tool for visual hierarchy — distinguishing headers from data rows, highlighting summaries, showing nested structure.
plot |> set_row_style(
  type    = "rtype",    # "header", "data", "summary", "spacer"
  bold    = "rbold",
  italic  = "ritalic",
  color   = "rcolor",   # CSS colors
  bg      = "rbg",
  badge   = "rbadge",   # text label anchored to first column
  icon    = "ricon",    # emoji/unicode anchored to first column
  indent  = "rindent"   # 0, 1, 2, ...
)






Warning



badge and icon render into the first visible text column’s cell. If you reorder columns, they re-anchor to the new first column. See Binding rules for details.




Code
styled_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Primary", "  CV Death", "  MI", "Secondary", "  Stroke"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.82, 0.79, NA, 0.88),
  lower = c(NA, 0.72, 0.68, NA, 0.74),
  upper = c(NA, 0.94, 0.92, NA, 1.05),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "header", "data"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE),
  rindent = c(0, 1, 1, 0, 1)
)

forest_plot(styled_data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper", label = "study",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
) |>
  set_row_style(type = "rtype", bold = "rbold", indent = "rindent") |>
  set_theme("cochrane")








set_marker_style()
Interval marker styling (points and lines for effect estimates):
plot |> set_marker_style(
  color   = "marker_color",  # CSS colors
  shape   = "marker_shape",  # "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"
  opacity = "marker_alpha",  # 0–1
  size    = "marker_size"    # size multiplier
)


Code
marker_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C", "Trial D"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.95, 0.68, 1.15),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.78, 0.52, 0.90),
  upper = c(0.94, 1.15, 0.89, 1.47),
  direction = c("benefit", "neutral", "benefit", "harm")
) |>
  mutate(
    marker_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"
    ),
    marker_shape = ifelse(direction == "benefit", "circle", "square")
  )

forest_plot(marker_data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper", label = "study",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
) |>
  set_marker_style(color = "marker_color", shape = "marker_shape") |>
  set_theme("cochrane")








set_column_style()
Per-cell styling for a specific column:
plot |> set_column_style(column = "hr",
  bold  = "is_sig",
  color = "hr_color",
  bg    = "hr_bg"
)






TipStyling priority



When both row and column styles target the same cell, column styles win. Row styles apply to cells without column overrides — so you can set row-wide defaults with column-specific highlights.




set_theme() and theme modifiers
# Apply by name
plot |> set_theme("jama")

# Apply by object
plot |> set_theme(web_theme_lancet())

# Modify a Tier 1 input
plot |> set_theme(web_theme_lancet() |> set_inputs(primary = "#1a365d"))

# Modify spacing on the active theme (operates on a WebTheme directly)
custom <- web_theme_lancet() |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 24) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
plot |> set_theme(custom)
Theme modifiers operate on a WebTheme object (not a plot directly). Build the theme you want, then attach it via set_theme().







Modifier
Edits




set_inputs()
Tier 1 customer inputs (brand, accent, series, fonts, status)


set_variants()
Per-table variants (density, header_style, first_column_style)


set_spacing()
Per-token spacing override on top of the density preset


set_theme_field(theme, path, value)
Generic deep setter; path walks the theme tree



See Themes guide for the full ontology and Styling reference for parameter tables.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Column Operations
Restructure the columns of a running plot without re-rendering.

add_column()
Insert a new column. Use after to position it; NULL appends.
plot |> add_column(col_numeric("year", "Year"), after = "n")
plot |> add_column(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper"))
Static:


Code
df <- data.frame(
  study = c("A", "B", "C"),
  hr = c(0.8, 1.1, 1.4),
  n = c(100, 200, 150)
)
tabviz(df, label = "study",
  columns = list(col_numeric("hr", "HR"))
) |>
  add_column(col_numeric("n", "N"), after = "hr")






Proxy:
observeEvent(input$show_year, {
  tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
    add_column(col_numeric("year", "Year"), after = "n")
})


remove_column()
plot |> remove_column("notes")
On a proxy this hides the column without dropping data; it can be re-added later.


move_column()
# Numeric index (1-based on R side; verb converts to 0-based for the store)
plot |> move_column("notes", to = 1)

# Or position relative to another column
plot |> move_column("notes", before = "hr")


resize_column()
plot |> resize_column("hr", width = 140)   # pixels


update_column()
Change column properties — header, alignment, wrap, width, or any option in the column’s options list:
plot |> update_column("hr", header = "Hazard Ratio", align = "right")
plot |> update_column("hr", options = list(decimals = 3))
Unknown keys are merged into options, matching R-side semantics.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Row & Data Operations

sort_rows()
plot |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "asc")
plot |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "desc")
plot |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "none")   # restore original order
On a static WebSpec, direction = "none" is a no-op. On a proxy it clears the sort.


filter_rows() and clear_filters()
Operators: "eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains".
plot |> filter_rows("n", operator = "gt", value = 100)
plot |> filter_rows("drug_class", operator = "eq", value = "SGLT2i")

plot |> clear_filters()
On a WebSpec, filter_rows() mutates @data in place. On a proxy it applies a display-only filter — the original data is preserved.


select_rows() and move_row()
plot |> select_rows(c("study_A", "study_B"))       # by row id
plot |> move_row("study_C", to = 1)                # to top
select_rows() on a proxy forwards selection state to any Shiny input bindings tied to the widget.


update_data()
Replace the underlying data. On a proxy, this requires a full WebSpec (not just a data frame) because column types must be re-serialized:
new_spec <- tabviz(new_df, label = "study", columns = list(...),
                  .spec_only = TRUE)
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> update_data(new_spec)

title: The Fluent API
text:
Groups & Zoom

toggle_group()
Collapse or expand a group header:
plot |> toggle_group("North America")               # flip current state
plot |> toggle_group("North America", collapsed = TRUE)
plot |> toggle_group("Europe",        collapsed = FALSE)


set_zoom()
plot |> set_zoom(zoom = 0.8)
plot |> set_zoom(zoom = 1.0, auto_fit = TRUE)
plot |> set_zoom(max_width = 900, max_height = 600)
plot |> set_zoom(show_controls = FALSE)
Any subset of arguments may be passed; unchanged knobs are left alone.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Cell Edits (proxy-only)
These verbs mutate rendered state and require a live widget. On a static WebSpec they warn and return unchanged.
# Change a single cell value
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> set_cell("row_id", "hr", 1.23)

# Rename a row's label
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> set_row_label("row_id", "Renamed")

# Drop all in-browser edits
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> clear_edits()

title: The Fluent API
text:
Conditional Styling with dplyr
Compute style columns with dplyr before plotting:


Code
analysis_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("EMPA-REG", "CANVAS", "DECLARE", "DAPA-HF"),
  hr = c(0.62, 0.86, 0.93, 0.74),
  lower = c(0.49, 0.75, 0.84, 0.65),
  upper = c(0.77, 0.97, 1.03, 0.85),
  pval = c(0.0001, 0.02, 0.17, 0.0001)
)

styled_analysis <- analysis_data |>
  mutate(
    is_sig = pval < 0.05,
    sig_color = ifelse(is_sig, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8")
  )

forest_plot(styled_analysis,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper", label = "study",
  columns = list(col_pvalue("pval")),
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
) |>
  set_marker_style(color = "sig_color") |>
  set_row_style(bold = "is_sig") |>
  set_theme("cochrane")












TipStyle column naming



Use consistent suffixes: _bold, _color, _bg, _badge. Makes it clear which columns are data vs styling.

title: The Fluent API
The Fluent API
text:
Verb Reference
Styling







Verb
Purpose




set_row_style()
Row-level (bold, italic, color, type, indent, badge, icon)


set_marker_style()
Interval marker (color, shape, opacity, size)


set_column_style()
Per-cell styling for one column


set_theme()
Apply theme by name or object


set_inputs(), set_variants(), set_spacing(), set_theme_field()
Theme-level modifiers (operate on a WebTheme, then set_theme() to attach)



Columns



Verb
Purpose




add_column()
Insert at a position


remove_column()
Drop / hide


move_column()
Reorder by index or relative position


resize_column()
Set width in pixels


update_column()
Modify header, alignment, options



Rows & data



Verb
Purpose




sort_rows()
Sort by a column


filter_rows()
Apply a predicate


clear_filters()
Remove all filters


select_rows()
Set the selected-row set


move_row()
Reorder by index


update_data()
Replace underlying data (WebSpec on proxy)



Groups, cells, global



Verb
Purpose




toggle_group()
Collapse/expand a group header


set_cell()
Change a cell value (proxy-only)


set_row_label()
Rename a row’s label (proxy-only)


clear_edits()
Drop in-browser edits (proxy-only)


set_zoom()
Zoom / auto-fit / dimensions



See Styling reference and Data-ops reference for complete parameter documentation.

title: Package Data
text: tabviz includes five datasets for examples, testing, and learning. Each is designed to showcase different features and use cases.

title: Package Data
text:
Available Datasets









Dataset
Rows
Domain
Key Features




glp1_trials
24
Clinical trials
HR/CI, groups, subgroups, summary rows


airline_delays
40
Transportation
Sparkline trends, bar charts, grouped


nba_efficiency
30
Sports
Player stats, conference groups, badges


climate_temps
20
Science
Minimal structure, uncertainty ranges


effect_sizes
20
Stress test
Extreme CIs, split testing, regions/outcomes

title: Package Data
text:
glp1_trials
GLP-1 agonist cardiovascular outcomes trials—ideal for meta-analysis examples.


Code
data(glp1_trials)
glimpse(glp1_trials)


Rows: 24
Columns: 13
$ study    <chr> "SUSTAIN-6", "PIONEER 6", "LEADER", "REWIND", "EXSCEL", "AMPL…
$ drug     <chr> "Semaglutide 1mg", "Oral Semaglutide", "Liraglutide", "Dulagl…
$ year     <dbl> 2016, 2019, 2016, 2019, 2017, 2021, 2015, 2018, 2016, 2024, 2…
$ n        <dbl> 3297, 3183, 9340, 9901, 14752, 4076, 6068, 9463, 4156, 9650, …
$ events   <dbl> 108, 137, 608, 594, 839, 189, 406, 338, 97, 510, 550, 620, 17…
$ hr       <dbl> 0.74, 0.79, 0.87, 0.88, 0.91, 0.73, 1.02, 0.78, 0.98, 0.86, 0…
$ lower    <dbl> 0.58, 0.57, 0.78, 0.79, 0.83, 0.58, 0.89, 0.68, 0.65, 0.76, 0…
$ upper    <dbl> 0.95, 1.11, 0.97, 0.99, 1.00, 0.92, 1.17, 0.90, 1.48, 0.98, 0…
$ pvalue   <dbl> 0.0160, 0.1700, 0.0100, 0.0260, 0.0600, 0.0070, 0.8100, 0.000…
$ endpoint <chr> "MACE", "MACE", "MACE", "MACE", "MACE", "MACE", "MACE", "MACE…
$ row_type <chr> "data", "data", "data", "data", "data", "data", "data", "data…
$ row_bold <lgl> FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE…
$ group    <chr> "Main Trials", "Main Trials", "Main Trials", "Main Trials", "…


Best for: Meta-analyses, subgroup analyses, publication-style forest plots

Example


Code
glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials") |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      col_n("n"),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_lancet(),
    title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes"
  )








Variables



Variable
Description




study
Trial name and year


drug
GLP-1 agonist name


hr, lower, upper
Hazard ratio and 95% CI


n, events
Sample size and event count


pvalue
P-value


group
Trial grouping (Main Trials, Subgroups)


row_type, row_bold
Pre-computed row styling

title: Package Data
text:
airline_delays
Airline carrier performance data with trends—ideal for sparklines and bar charts.


Code
data(airline_delays)
glimpse(airline_delays)


Rows: 40
Columns: 10
$ carrier      <chr> "Alaska", "Alaska", "Alaska", "Alaska", "Allegiant", "All…
$ carrier_type <chr> "Legacy", "Legacy", "Legacy", "Legacy", "Ultra Low-Cost",…
$ month        <chr> "Apr", "Feb", "Jan", "Mar", "Apr", "Feb", "Jan", "Mar", "…
$ delay_vs_avg <dbl> -4.9, -3.2, -5.1, -1.8, 6.4, 2.0, 4.8, 3.6, 3.4, 0.7, 2.9…
$ on_time_pct  <dbl> 0.864, 0.867, 0.834, 0.873, 0.794, 0.745, 0.777, 0.760, 0…
$ satisfaction <dbl> 4.5, 4.1, 4.1, 4.9, 3.0, 2.3, 3.0, 2.6, 3.4, 3.1, 3.2, 2.…
$ flights      <dbl> 19267, 13080, 29086, 25335, 21556, 23170, 19367, 26558, 3…
$ delay_lower  <dbl> -6.89125903, -5.36685429, -7.32565221, -3.39057838, 3.413…
$ delay_upper  <dbl> -2.86032213, -1.56362254, -2.98630186, 0.89789496, 8.6057…
$ trend        <list> <-4.7, -4.7, -4.2, -4.2, -5.9, -5.1, -4.7, -5.0, -4.9, -…


Best for: Sparkline columns, bar charts, grouped comparisons

Example


Code
airline_summary <- airline_delays |>
  group_by(carrier) |>
  summarise(
    delay_vs_avg = mean(delay_vs_avg),
    delay_lower = mean(delay_lower),
    delay_upper = mean(delay_upper),
    on_time_pct = mean(on_time_pct),
    trend = list(unlist(trend[1]))
  )

tabviz(
  airline_summary,
  label = "carrier",
  columns = list(
    col_percent("on_time_pct", "On-Time %"),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12M Trend"),
    col_interval("delay_vs_avg", "delay_lower", "delay_upper", header = "Delay (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "delay_vs_avg", lower = "delay_lower", upper = "delay_upper",
               null_value = 0, axis_label = "Delay vs. Average (minutes)")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Airline Carrier Performance"
)








Variables







Variable
Description




carrier
Airline name


delay_vs_avg, delay_lower, delay_upper
Delay vs industry average and 95% CI


on_time_pct
Proportion of on-time arrivals (0-1)


satisfaction
Customer satisfaction (1-5)


flights
Number of flights


trend
List-column with 12-month delay trend


carrier_type
Carrier category (Major, Regional, Budget)

title: Package Data
text:
nba_efficiency
NBA player statistics—a fun dataset for demonstrating grouped data and badges.


Code
data(nba_efficiency)
glimpse(nba_efficiency)


Rows: 30
Columns: 11
$ player     <chr> "Nikola Jokic", "Luka Doncic", "Giannis Antetokounmpo", "Jo…
$ team       <chr> "DEN", "DAL", "MIL", "PHI", "OKC", "BOS", "MIN", "CLE", "SA…
$ conference <chr> "West", "West", "West", "West", "West", "East", "East", "Ea…
$ position   <chr> "C", "G", "F", "C", "G", "F", "G", "G", "G", "G", "F", "F",…
$ games      <dbl> 79, 70, 73, 39, 75, 74, 79, 55, 74, 69, 71, 75, 74, 68, 9, …
$ ppg        <dbl> 26.4, 33.9, 30.4, 34.7, 30.1, 26.9, 25.9, 26.6, 26.6, 20.1,…
$ per        <dbl> 31.5, 27.8, 29.5, 31.2, 25.4, 23.1, 22.3, 24.2, 22.5, 19.8,…
$ per_lower  <dbl> 29.9, 26.0, 26.9, 28.4, 22.7, 21.1, 20.7, 22.3, 20.6, 17.7,…
$ per_upper  <dbl> 34.5, 29.6, 32.4, 33.5, 27.9, 26.0, 25.3, 27.1, 25.4, 22.1,…
$ all_star   <lgl> TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE,…
$ award      <chr> "MVP", "All-NBA 1st", "All-NBA 1st", "All-NBA 2nd", "All-NB…


Best for: Grouped data, badges, non-medical examples

Example


Code
tabviz(
  nba_efficiency,
  label = "player",
  group = "conference",
  columns = list(
    col_text("team", "Team", width = 60),
    col_numeric("ppg", "PPG"),
    col_interval("per", "per_lower", "per_upper", header = "PER (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "per", lower = "per_lower", upper = "per_upper",
               null_value = 15, axis_label = "Player Efficiency Rating")
  ),
  row_badge = "award",
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "NBA Player Efficiency",
  subtitle = "2023-24 Season",
  caption = "PER: 15 = league average"
)








Variables







Variable
Description




player
Player name


team, position
Team abbreviation and position


conference
Eastern or Western


per, per_lower, per_upper
Player Efficiency Rating and 95% CI


ppg, games
Points per game and games played


award
Major awards (MVP, DPOY, etc.) for badge display

title: Package Data
text:
climate_temps
Regional temperature anomalies—minimal dataset for clean examples.


Code
data(climate_temps)
glimpse(climate_temps)


Rows: 20
Columns: 7
$ region    <chr> "Global", "Northern Hemisphere", "Southern Hemisphere", "Arc…
$ period    <chr> "2023", "2023", "2023", "2023", "2023", "2023", "2023", "202…
$ anomaly   <dbl> 1.45, 1.62, 1.28, 2.85, 0.45, 1.78, 2.10, 1.65, 1.35, 1.20, …
$ lower     <dbl> 1.38, 1.52, 1.18, 2.55, 0.30, 1.58, 1.88, 1.45, 1.18, 1.02, …
$ upper     <dbl> 1.52, 1.72, 1.38, 3.15, 0.60, 1.98, 2.32, 1.85, 1.52, 1.38, …
$ certainty <chr> "High", "High", "High", "Medium", "Medium", "High", "High", …
$ category  <chr> "Global", "Hemisphere", "Hemisphere", "Polar", "Polar", "Con…


Best for: Simple examples, minimal themes, uncertainty visualization

Example


Code
climate_temps |>
  filter(category %in% c("Global", "Hemisphere", "Continental")) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "region",
    group = "category",
    columns = list(
      col_text("certainty", "Confidence"),
      col_interval("anomaly", "lower", "upper", header = "Anomaly (95% CI)"),
      viz_forest(point = "anomaly", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 null_value = 0, axis_label = "Temperature Anomaly (°C)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_jama(),
    title = "Regional Temperature Anomalies",
    subtitle = "2023 vs. Pre-Industrial Baseline"
  )








Variables



Variable
Description




region
Geographic region name


category
Region type (Global, Hemisphere, Continental, Ocean)


anomaly, lower, upper
Temperature anomaly and uncertainty


certainty
Confidence level (High, Medium, Low)

title: Package Data
text:
effect_sizes
A stress-test dataset designed to test forest plot axis rendering with varying confidence interval widths.


Code
data(effect_sizes)
glimpse(effect_sizes)


Rows: 20
Columns: 12
$ study       <chr> "Major Medical Center A", "University Hospital B", "Nation…
$ region      <chr> "North America", "North America", "Europe", "Europe", "Asi…
$ outcome     <chr> "Primary", "Primary", "Primary", "Primary", "Primary", "Pr…
$ treatment   <chr> "Drug A", "Drug A", "Drug A", "Drug A", "Drug A", "Drug A"…
$ phase       <chr> "Phase 3", "Phase 3", "Phase 3", "Phase 3", "Phase 3", "Ph…
$ n           <dbl> 2500, 1800, 3200, 1500, 2100, 450, 380, 520, 290, 410, 85,…
$ hr          <dbl> 0.72, 0.78, 0.81, 0.76, 0.84, 0.68, 0.92, 0.71, 1.05, 0.88…
$ lower       <dbl> 0.702, 0.762, 0.793, 0.737, 0.821, 0.643, 0.873, 0.681, 0.…
$ upper       <dbl> 0.739, 0.798, 0.827, 0.784, 0.860, 0.719, 0.970, 0.741, 1.…
$ se          <dbl> 0.01302166, 0.01169697, 0.01067924, 0.01573598, 0.01172054…
$ significant <lgl> TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRU…
$ direction   <chr> "Favors Treatment", "Favors Treatment", "Favors Treatment"…


Best for: Testing axis calculation, split views, extreme CIs, multi-forest columns

Example


Code
# Group by region and show effect sizes with marker styling
effect_sizes |>
  filter(outcome == "Primary") |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    group = "region",
    columns = list(
      col_n("n"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 200)
    ),
    marker_color = ~ ifelse(significant, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8"),
    theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
    title = "Effect Sizes by Region"
  )








Variables



Variable
Description




study
Study or site name


region
Geographic region (North America, Europe, Asia Pacific)


outcome
Outcome type (Primary, Secondary, Exploratory)


treatment
Treatment arm


hr, lower, upper
Hazard ratio and 95% CI


significant
Whether CI excludes 1.0


direction
Effect direction (Favors Treatment, Favors Control)

title: Package Data
text:
Using Datasets
All datasets are lazy-loaded. Simply reference them after loading the package:
library(tabviz)

# Datasets are available immediately
head(glp1_trials)

# Or explicitly load
data(glp1_trials)

?glp1_trials

?climate_temps
?effect_sizes

title: Shiny Integration
text: tabviz works seamlessly with Shiny through the htmlwidgets framework. This page covers basic integration, the proxy API for live updates, and split-table outputs.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Output and Render








UI
Server
Used for




tabvizOutput()
renderTabviz()
A single tabviz widget


splitTabvizOutput()
renderSplitTabviz()
A SplitForest (tabviz + sidebar of subgroups)



library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Forest Plot Explorer"),
  tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(meta_data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

Sizing
Both output functions accept standard CSS sizing:



Argument
Default
Examples




width
"100%"
"800px", "50vw"


height
"400px" ("600px" for split)
"600px", "80vh"

title: Shiny Integration
Shiny Integration
text:
The Proxy API
Proxy functions update the plot without a full re-render — scroll position, selection state, and layout stay stable.







Function
Purpose




tabviz_proxy(id)
Reference a tabvizOutput for live updates


split_tabviz_proxy(id)
Reference a splitTabvizOutput for live updates


split_tabviz_select(proxy, key)
Switch a split table to a specific subgroup









ImportantAlways create proxies inside reactive contexts



Never create a proxy at the top level of your app. Both tabviz_proxy() and split_tabviz_proxy() require an active Shiny reactive domain.



Fluent verbs dispatch on proxies
Every fluent modifier — every set_*, add_*, remove_*, sort_*, filter_*, plus set_cell() and clear_edits() — dispatches on tabviz_proxy and sends a custom message to the running widget instead of mutating a static spec. The same call site works on a WebSpec, an htmlwidget, and a proxy:







Category
Verbs




Columns
add_column(), remove_column(), move_column(), resize_column(), update_column()


Rows
sort_rows(), filter_rows(), clear_filters(), select_rows(), move_row()


Data
update_data() (proxy requires a fresh WebSpec)


Groups
toggle_group()


Cells
set_cell(), set_row_label(), clear_edits() (proxy-only)


Global
set_theme(), set_zoom()





Sorting and filtering
observeEvent(input$sort_asc,  sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", "asc"))
