observeEvent(input$sort_desc, sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", "desc"))

observeEvent(input$filter_n, {
  filter_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "n", "gt", input$min_sample_size)
})

observeEvent(input$clear_filters, {
  clear_filters(tabviz_proxy("plot"))
})
Filter operators: "eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains".


Replacing the dataset
update_data() on a proxy takes a WebSpec, not a raw data frame. Build one with tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE):
observeEvent(input$switch_dataset, {
  new_spec <- tabviz(
    get_dataset(input$dataset_name),
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ),
    .spec_only = TRUE
  )
  update_data(tabviz_proxy("plot"), new_spec)
})


Chaining
Every proxy verb returns the proxy invisibly, so calls chain:
observeEvent(input$reset_view, {
  tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
    clear_filters() |>
    sort_rows("study", "none") |>
    toggle_group("all", collapsed = FALSE)
})

title: Shiny Integration
Shiny Integration
text:
Complete Example App
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)
library(dplyr)

meta_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("ADVANCE", "SPRINT", "ACCORD", "ONTARGET", "HOPE-3",
            "EMPA-REG", "CANVAS", "DECLARE", "DAPA-HF", "EMPEROR"),
  drug_class = c("ACEi", "Intensive BP", "Intensive BP", "ARB", "Statin",
                 "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i"),
  hr    = c(0.91, 0.75, 0.88, 0.94, 0.90, 0.62, 0.86, 0.93, 0.74, 0.75),
  lower = c(0.83, 0.64, 0.76, 0.86, 0.82, 0.49, 0.75, 0.84, 0.65, 0.65),
  upper = c(1.01, 0.87, 1.01, 1.02, 0.98, 0.77, 0.97, 1.03, 0.85, 0.86),
  n     = c(11140, 9361, 10251, 25620, 12705, 7020, 10142, 17160, 4744, 3730)
)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Interactive Forest Plot"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      width = 3,
      h4("Filters"),
      selectInput("drug_class", "Drug Class",
        choices = c("All", unique(meta_data$drug_class)), selected = "All"),
      sliderInput("min_n", "Minimum Sample Size",
        min = 0, max = 20000, value = 0, step = 1000),
      hr(),
      h4("Display"),
      selectInput("theme", "Theme",
        choices = c("Default" = "default", "JAMA" = "jama", "Lancet" = "lancet")),
      actionButton("sort_hr", "Sort by HR"),
      actionButton("reset",   "Reset View")
    ),
    mainPanel(width = 9, tabvizOutput("plot", height = "600px"))
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  plot_data <- reactive({
    d <- meta_data
    if (input$drug_class != "All") d <- dplyr::filter(d, drug_class == input$drug_class)
    dplyr::filter(d, n >= input$min_n)
  })

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(plot_data(),
      label = "study",
      group = "drug_class",
      columns = list(
        col_numeric("n", "N"),
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")
      )
    )
  })

  observe({
    set_theme(tabviz_proxy("plot"), switch(input$theme,
      "jama" = "jama", "lancet" = "lancet", "default"))
  })

  sort_state <- reactiveVal("none")
  observeEvent(input$sort_hr, {
    new_dir <- switch(sort_state(), "none" = "asc", "asc" = "desc", "desc" = "none")
    sort_state(new_dir)
    sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", new_dir)
  })

  observeEvent(input$reset, {
    updateSelectInput(session, "drug_class", selected = "All")
    updateSliderInput(session, "min_n", value = 0)
    sort_state("none")
    tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
      clear_filters() |>
      sort_rows("hr", "none")
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Two-way Integration: Reading Widget State
The widget emits every interactive dimension back to Shiny as named inputs. This lets a dashboard mirror what the user is doing inside the widget — sort clicks, filter changes, column show/hide, paint tokens, group collapses, even zoom and pan state.

The contract
Every dimension is emitted as input$<id>_<dimension> with a uniform envelope:
list(value = ..., source = "user" | "proxy", ts = <ms>)
source distinguishes user-driven mutations from proxy-pushed ones, so an observer can ignore its own writes:
observeEvent(input$plot_sort, {
  req(input$plot_sort$source == "user")  # don't echo proxy writes
  current_sort <- input$plot_sort$value  # list(column =, direction =)
  # ...react to user-driven sort change
})


tabviz_state() — read everything at once
tabviz_state(input, "plot") pulls every dimension into a single named list, unwrapped. Use tabviz_state_envelope() if you need the source and ts.
observe({
  state <- tabviz_state(input, "plot")
  str(state$sort)              # list(column = "hr", direction = "desc")
  str(state$filters)           # list(arm = list(operator = "eq", value = "A"))
  str(state$selected)          # c("study_1", "study_4")
  str(state$hidden_columns)    # c("se", "ci_lo")
  str(state$collapsed_groups)  # c("group_drug_class")
})


Dimensions emitted







Dimension
Shape




sort
list(column, direction) or NULL


filters
named list, key = field, value = list(operator, value)


selected
character() of row ids


hover
row id or NULL


collapsed_groups
character() of group ids


hidden_columns
character() of column ids


column_order
character() of column ids in display order


column_widths
named numeric


cell_edits
list(cells, groups) map of user-typed values


label_edits
named list (title/subtitle/caption/footnote)


row_styles, cell_styles
paint-token maps


paint_tool
list(token, scope) — the active brush


zoom
list(zoom, autoFit, maxWidth, maxHeight, showZoomControls)


axis_zooms
per-forest-column pan/zoom domains


banding
list(mode, startsWithBand) or NULL


plot_width
numeric or NULL


visible_rows
character() of row ids actually visible after filter+sort+collapse



There is also input$<id>_state, a debounced (~150 ms) bundle of every dimension above. Useful for bookmarking or debugging — observe(str(input$plot_state)) shows the whole world.


Round-trip example
ui <- fluidPage(
  selectInput("col", "Sort column", c("hr", "n")),
  selectInput("dir", "Direction", c("asc", "desc", "none")),
  tabvizOutput("plot"),
  verbatimTextOutput("status")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(meta_data, label = "study",
      columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lower", "upper")))
  })

  # Shiny → Widget: dropdowns drive the widget's sort.
  observe({
    sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), input$col, input$dir)
  })

  # Widget → Shiny: clicking a header in the widget updates the dropdowns.
  observeEvent(input$plot_sort, {
    req(input$plot_sort$source == "user")  # ignore our own pushes
    s <- input$plot_sort$value
    updateSelectInput(session, "col", selected = s$column)
    updateSelectInput(session, "dir", selected = s$direction)
  })

  output$status <- renderPrint(tabviz_state(input, "plot"))
}
The source == "user" guard is the key — without it, the proxy’s own writes would echo back into the dropdowns and you’d risk a feedback loop.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Initial State at First Paint
tabviz() accepts three initial_* args so the first paint already reflects the dashboard’s expected state — no flash of unsorted/unfiltered content before a proxy push lands:
output$plot <- renderTabviz({
  tabviz(meta_data, label = "study",
    initial_sort = list(column = "hr", direction = "desc"),
    initial_filters = list(arm = list(operator = "eq", value = "A")),
    initial_hidden_columns = c("se", "ci_lo"),
    columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lower", "upper"))
  )
})
These are the constructor counterparts of sort_rows(), filter_rows(), and remove_column() — same shape, applied before mount.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Proxy vs Re-render








Use case
Approach
Reason




Sort / filter existing rows
Proxy
Smooth, no flicker


Change theme / zoom
Proxy
set_theme() / set_zoom() route through the store


Add, remove, resize columns
Proxy
Column verbs are wired through the store


Completely different dataset (new columns/types)
Re-render or update_data() with a fresh WebSpec



Toggle groups
Proxy
UI-state only

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Best Practices

Create proxies inside reactive contexts, not at app top level.
Debounce slider-driven inputs with shiny::debounce().
Prefer proxy verbs for interactive updates — they preserve scroll and selection.
Reach for full re-render only when the column structure or data schema changes.
Chain proxy calls for atomic UI updates.

title: Columns
text: Columns are the unit of display in tabviz. You compose a table by passing a list of column specifications to tabviz(columns = ...). Each column helper (col_*(), viz_*()) picks a display type and exposes formatting knobs; the underlying web_col() constructor is there if you need something unusual.

title: Columns
text:
Universal arguments
Every col_*() helper accepts the same arguments for layout and styling. These behave identically regardless of column type.



Argument
Default
Purpose




Primary data (see per-helper table)
required
Which column(s) in data provide values


header
NULL (auto)
Column header text; defaults to the field name or a type-specific default


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


align
"left" (numeric types default to "right")
Body cell alignment: "left", "center", "right"


header_align
follows align
Header alignment; override to decouple


show_header
auto
TRUE/FALSE to force; NULL shows when header is non-empty


wrap
FALSE
Multi-line cell text. FALSE/0 = single line (ellipsis on overflow). TRUE/1 = up to 2 lines (1 extra). Integer n = up to n + 1 lines. Author \n is honoured first, then long segments word-wrap within the column width.


sortable
TRUE
Allow sort on header click


na_text
NULL
Text to display for NA/missing values (default is blank)


tooltip
NULL
Column whose values become per-cell hover text


formatter
NULL
R function function(x) ... applied to values; output rendered as text. See Custom formatters below.


bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent
NULL
Per-cell styling driven by another column — see Cell Styling









TipEverything routes through web_col()



The col_*() helpers are thin, opinionated wrappers over web_col(). When a helper doesn’t cover your case (custom type, non-standard option), call web_col() directly.

title: Columns
text:
Catalog at a glance








Helper
Use for
Type-specific arguments




col_text(field)
Plain text, categoricals
max_chars


col_label(field)
Row-identifier column
max_chars


col_numeric(field)
General numbers
decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_n(field)
Sample sizes
decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_percent(field)
Proportions / percentages
decimals, digits, multiply, symbol


col_currency(field)
Money
symbol, position, decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_pvalue(field)
P-values
stars, thresholds, format, digits, exp_threshold, abbrev_threshold


col_interval(point, lower, upper)
Point estimate + CI
decimals, separator, imprecise_threshold


col_events(events, n)
Events / N ratio
separator, show_pct, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_range(low, high)
Low – High range
separator, decimals, show_bar


col_date(field)
Dates
format


col_bar(field)
Inline magnitude bar (no axis)
max_value, show_label, color, scale


col_sparkline(field)
Trend charts
type, height, color


col_heatmap(field)
Color-scale values
palette, min_value, max_value, decimals, show_value, scale


col_progress(field)
Percentage progress bar
min_value, max_value, show_label, height, color


col_icon(field)
Icons / emoji
mapping, size, color


col_badge(field)
Status chips
variants, colors, size


col_stars(field)
Rating (1–N stars)
max_stars, color, empty_color, half_stars, min_value, max_value, size


col_pictogram(field)
Glyph counts or rating piles
glyph, glyph_field, max_glyphs, half_glyphs, min_value, max_value, color, empty_color, size, layout, value_label, label_format, label_decimals


col_ring(field)
Donut gauge with optional thresholds
min_value, max_value, color, thresholds, track_color, size, show_label, label_format, label_decimals


col_img(field)
Inline images
height, max_width, fallback, shape


col_reference(field)
Truncated text + optional link
href_field, max_chars, show_icon


col_group(header, ...)
Group child columns under one header
—



For the four axis-backed visualization columns (viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin), see Visualizations.

title: Columns
text:
Composing columns
A tabviz() call takes a list of column specs in display order. The special label argument produces an implicit leftmost identifier column; you can also declare it explicitly with col_label() if you want finer control.


Code
data(glp1_trials)

meta <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4)

tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
  )
)

title: Columns
text:
Headers and widths
Headers default to the field name if omitted; pass a second positional argument (or named header) to override, and an empty string or show_header = FALSE to hide.
Widths are auto-sized by default. Pass a pixel value to fix a column.


Code
col_text("drug")                          # header: "drug", auto width
col_text("drug", "Drug")                  # header: "Drug"
col_text("drug", header = "", width = 80) # no header, 80px wide
col_text("drug", show_header = FALSE)     # header cell hidden

title: Columns
text:
Alignment
col_* helpers that render numeric content right-align by default (col_numeric, col_n, col_percent, col_currency, col_pvalue, col_range). Everything else left-aligns. Override with align; header alignment follows the body unless you set header_align explicitly.


Code
col_numeric("value", align = "left")                      # force body left
col_numeric("value", header_align = "center")             # center header only

title: Columns
text:
Numeric formatting
The numeric formatting vocabulary is shared across col_numeric, col_n, col_percent, col_currency, col_pvalue, col_heatmap, col_events, and col_interval. Use whichever helper fits your data — you get the same formatting primitives.







Argument
Meaning




decimals
Fixed number of decimal places. Mutually exclusive with digits.


digits
Significant figures. Mutually exclusive with decimals.


thousands_sep
Separator string (",", " ", ".", …) or FALSE to disable


abbreviate
TRUE to shorten large numbers: 1,234 → "1.2K", 2.5e6 → "2.5M"





Code
options_data <- data.frame(
  item  = c("Small", "Medium", "Large"),
  n     = c(125, 4520, 1250000),
  rate  = c(0.156, 0.892, 0.034),
  value = c(1.234, 12.34, 123.4)
)

tabviz(options_data, label = "item",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n",                              "Count"),
    col_n("n",        abbreviate = TRUE,    "Abbreviated", id = "n_abbr"),
    col_numeric("value", decimals = 2,      "2 decimals"),
    col_numeric("value", digits   = 3,      "3 sig figs",   id = "value_sigfig"),
    col_percent("rate",                     "Percent")
  )
)












NoteDecimals vs. digits



decimals = 2 always prints 2 places (0.10, 3.14, 125.00). digits = 3 prints 3 meaningful figures (0.156, 3.14, 125). Use digits when values span orders of magnitude.

title: Columns
text:
Handling missing data
Set na_text on any column to replace NA cells with custom text.


Code
col_numeric("response", na_text = "—")
col_pvalue("pvalue", na_text = "n/a")
col_events("events", "n", na_text = "—")


The default is a blank cell.

title: Columns
text:
Per-cell tooltips
Pass tooltip = "<col>" to any column helper to drive the cell’s hover-text from another column in the data. This overrides the default behavior of using the cell’s own value as the tooltip.


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("status", tooltip = "status_explanation"),  # custom hover text
    col_n("n", tooltip = "n_source")                      # e.g. "Per-protocol N"
  )
)


tooltip accepts a column name; the value at that cell becomes the rendered title attribute.

title: Columns
text:
Custom formatters
When the built-in formatting vocabulary doesn’t fit (decimals/digits/abbreviate/thousands_sep), pass a formatter function. The function is applied to the column’s values at render time and the output is treated as text — the column is rendered verbatim.


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("A", "B", "C"),
  estimate = c(1.234, 0.85, 2.4),
  se       = c(0.12, 0.08, 0.31)
)

tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    web_col("estimate",
      formatter = function(x) sprintf("%.2f ± %.2f", x, data$se))
  )
)


The formatter receives the raw column vector; return a character vector of the same length. Use this for unit suffixes, conditional formatting, or composite values that don’t fit any single col_*() helper.






NoteWhen to reach for formatter



For routine formatting (decimals, sig figs, percentages, currencies, abbreviations), prefer the built-in arguments — they’re discoverable, declarative, and fast. Reach for formatter only when the desired output can’t be expressed via the built-in vocabulary.

title: Columns
Columns
text:
Column groups
col_group(header, ...) wraps a set of columns under a shared header row — useful for multiple outcomes, time points, or analyses.


Code
outcomes <- data.frame(
  study          = c("PARADIGM-HF", "DAPA-HF", "EMPEROR-Reduced"),
  primary_hr     = c(0.80, 0.74, 0.75),
  primary_lower  = c(0.73, 0.65, 0.65),
  primary_upper  = c(0.87, 0.85, 0.86),
  primary_p      = c(0.001, 0.001, 0.001),
  secondary_hr   = c(0.84, 0.70, 0.69),
  secondary_p    = c(0.01, 0.001, 0.001)
)

tabviz(outcomes, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "primary_hr", lower = "primary_lower",
               upper = "primary_upper", null_value = 1, scale = "log"),
    col_group("Primary",
      col_interval("primary_hr", "primary_lower", "primary_upper",
                   header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("primary_p", "P")
    ),
    col_group("Secondary",
      col_numeric("secondary_hr", "HR", decimals = 2),
      col_pvalue("secondary_p", "P")
    )
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
)

title: Columns
text:
Visualization columns
Four viz_*() columns render axis-backed plots inline: viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin. They share axis-control arguments (axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label, scale) and are documented separately:

Forest plots — point + CI with annotations
Bar charts — horizontal bars with an axis
Boxplots — quartiles, median, outliers
Violin plots — kernel-density distributions

Unlike col_bar, the viz columns share a consistent axis across rows.
All four viz columns honor the marker-styling cascade: row semantic classes (row_accent/row_emphasis/row_muted) and per-row marker_color from tabviz() reach into the viz cell automatically. Single-effect viz columns get a fill replacement; multi-effect columns get an outline that preserves per-effect color identity. See Row Styling → Cascading into viz cells for the full picture.

title: Columns
text:
Per-helper detail

Text and identifiers

col_text() and col_label()
Plain text with optional truncation. col_label() is the convenience helper for the row-identifier column; it also prettifies the field name into a Title Case header by default.


Code
col_text("drug")
col_text("notes", "Notes", max_chars = 50)      # truncate with ellipsis
col_text("description", wrap = TRUE, width = 220)   # wrap (up to 2 lines)
col_text("notes", wrap = 3, width = 200)            # up to 4 lines

col_label("study_id")                           # header auto: "Study Id"
col_label("study_id", header = "Study")          # explicit header





Numbers

col_numeric()
General-purpose numeric column with decimals / digits / thousands_sep / abbreviate.


Code
col_numeric("estimate")                          # 2 decimals
col_numeric("count", decimals = 0,
            thousands_sep = ",")                 # "12,345"
col_numeric("value", digits = 3)                 # 3 sig figs
col_numeric("population", abbreviate = TRUE)     # "1.2M"




col_n()
Convenience wrapper for integer counts: defaults to decimals = 0, thousands_sep = ",", header "N".


Code
col_n("n")
col_n("enrolled", header = "Enrolled", abbreviate = TRUE)




col_percent()
Formats proportions or percentages.








Arg
Default
Effect




multiply
TRUE
TRUE when input is [0, 1] (multiply by 100); set FALSE if your data is already on the 0–100 scale


symbol
TRUE
Append % to the formatted number





Code
col_percent("rate")                           # 0.05 → "5.0%"
col_percent("accuracy", multiply = FALSE)     # data is already 0–100
col_percent("share", symbol = FALSE)          # drop the "%"




col_currency()
Money with currency symbol prefix/suffix.








Arg
Default
Effect




symbol
"$"
Currency glyph (e.g. "€", "¥", "R$")


position
"prefix"
"suffix" for post-fixed currencies


thousands_sep
","
String or FALSE; European users often pass "."


abbreviate
FALSE
TRUE for "$1.2M"-style short form





Code
col_currency("price")
col_currency("amount", symbol = "€", thousands_sep = ".", position = "suffix")
col_currency("revenue", abbreviate = TRUE)    # "$1.2M"




col_pvalue()
P-values with smart formatting and optional significance stars.








Arg
Default
Effect




stars
FALSE
Append */**/*** based on thresholds


thresholds
c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001)
Star cutoffs


format
"auto"
"auto" / "scientific" / "decimal"


digits
2
Significant figures


exp_threshold
0.001
Below this, switch to scientific notation


abbrev_threshold
NULL
Below this, render as e.g. "<0.0001"





Code
col_pvalue("pval")
col_pvalue("pval", stars = TRUE)
col_pvalue("pval", abbrev_threshold = 0.0001)





Paired values

col_interval()
Renders a point estimate with its confidence interval: 0.85 (0.70, 1.03).








Arg
Default
Effect




point, lower, upper
required
Field names


decimals
2
Decimal places


separator
" "
Separator between point and CI block


imprecise_threshold
NULL
If upper/lower > threshold, render "—" instead





Code
col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
col_interval("or", "or_lo", "or_hi", decimals = 3, separator = ", ")
col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", imprecise_threshold = 10)




col_events()
Events / N ratio, optionally with percentage.


Code
col_events("events", "n")                           # "45/120"
col_events("events", "n", show_pct = TRUE)          # "45/120 (37.5%)"
col_events("events", "n", separator = " of ",
           abbreviate = TRUE)                       # "1.1K of 12K"




col_range()
Low – high range, optionally with an inline bar.


Code
col_range("age_min", "age_max", "Age")
col_range("ci_lower", "ci_upper", decimals = 1)
col_range("low_price", "high_price", show_bar = TRUE)





Dates

col_date()
Dates formatted with strftime-style tokens. R-side formatting, so the browser sees pre-formatted strings.


Code
col_date("enrollment_date", format = "%Y-%m-%d")
col_date("published",       format = "%b %Y")          # "Jan 2024"
col_date("visit",           format = "%A, %d %B %Y")    # full





Inline visuals

col_bar()
Small horizontal bar inside the cell — use for visual weight without a shared axis. For axis-backed bars across rows, reach for viz_bar().


Code
col_bar("weight")
col_bar("score", max_value = 100, color = "#2563eb")
col_bar("value", scale = "log")




col_sparkline()
Mini line/area/bar chart per row. Requires a list-column of numeric vectors.


Code
col_sparkline("trend")                          # default: line
col_sparkline("trend", type = "area")
col_sparkline("trend", type = "bar", height = 30, color = "#16a34a")








NotePreparing sparkline data



Create a list-column with mutate(trend = list(c(1, 2, 3))) or tibble(trend = I(list(...))). Vectors do not need to be the same length across rows.




col_heatmap()
Color-scale cell background with optional numeric overlay.


Code
col_heatmap("score")                                   # default palette
col_heatmap("score", palette = c("#fef3c7", "#92400e"),
            min_value = 0, max_value = 100)
col_heatmap("magnitude", scale = "log", show_value = FALSE)




col_progress()
Horizontal progress bar with numeric label.


Code
col_progress("completion", max_value = 100)
col_progress("share", max_value = 1, show_label = FALSE)
col_progress("fill", color = "#8b5cf6", height = 6)





Status and categories

col_badge()
Colored chips keyed to row values.







Arg
Effect




variants
Named list mapping values → semantic color ("success", "warning", "error", "info", "muted")


colors
Named list mapping values → literal CSS colors (overrides variants)


size
"base" or "sm"





Code
col_badge("status")                              # plain chips
col_badge("status", variants = list(
  Published = "success", Preprint = "warning", Retracted = "error"
))
col_badge("priority", colors = list(
  high = "#dc2626", medium = "#f59e0b", low = "#22c55e"
))




col_icon()
Icons, emoji, or unicode glyphs.


Code
col_icon("status_icon")                          # emoji already in data
col_icon("result", mapping = list(
  pass = "✔", fail = "✖", pending = "…"
))
col_icon("flag", size = "lg", color = "#16a34a")




col_stars()
Visual rating (filled/empty stars).


Code
col_stars("quality")                              # 1–5 default
col_stars("quality", max_stars = 10, half_stars = TRUE)
col_stars("score_0_100", min_value = 0, max_value = 100)   # remap to star count
col_stars("rating", size = "lg")                            # larger glyphs




col_pictogram()
Repeated glyphs as either a count (render round(value) glyphs) or a rating (fill value of max_glyphs). Useful for “isotype”-style displays — three persons for n = 3, four-of-five skulls for a risk score.







Arg
Effect




glyph
Glyph name ("person", "skull", "leaf", "flame", "coin", …) or a named character vector for per-row glyph selection (paired with glyph_field)


glyph_field
Column name whose value picks the key in a named glyph vector


max_glyphs
NULL → count mode; integer → rating mode


half_glyphs
Allow half-fill on the boundary glyph


min_value / max_value
Remap input range into [0, max_glyphs] (rating mode)


color / empty_color
Filled / unfilled glyph colors (defaults follow the active theme)


size
"sm" / "base" / "lg"


layout
"row" (default) or "stack" — vertical pile, best in tall rows


value_label
FALSE / TRUE (= "trailing") / "leading" / "trailing"


label_format, label_decimals
"integer" or "decimal"; decimal places when decimal





Code
# Count mode — three persons for n = 3
col_pictogram("score", glyph = "person", value_label = TRUE)

# Rating mode — three of five skulls
col_pictogram("perf", glyph = "skull", max_glyphs = 5,
              color = "#f97316", empty_color = "#3f3f46")

# Per-row glyph selection — leaf on "low" rows, flame on "high"
col_pictogram("count", glyph = c("low" = "leaf", "high" = "flame"),
              glyph_field = "category")

# Vertical stack with trailing label (use in tall rows next to a viz column)
col_pictogram("eaten", glyph = "coin", layout = "stack",
              value_label = "trailing")




col_ring()
Donut gauge — fills (value - min_value) / (max_value - min_value) of a circle, with optional threshold-driven color shifts. Pairs well with proportional or score-style data.







Arg
Effect




min_value / max_value
Input range (default 0 / 1 — fraction)


color
Filled arc color. Single color, or character vector of length length(thresholds) + 1


thresholds
Numeric breakpoints (raw input units) where arc color shifts


track_color
Unfilled track color (default theme muted)


size
"sm" (~18 px) / "base" (~24 px) / "lg" (~32 px)


show_label
Show numeric value to the right of the ring


label_format, label_decimals
"percent" (default) / "decimal" / "integer"





Code
# Single-color ring, value as fraction [0, 1]
col_ring("temptation", color = "#d4a955")

# Two-color threshold (gold below 50%, red above)
col_ring("temptation",
         color = c("#d4a955", "#a855f7"), thresholds = 0.5)

# Three-color status: positive / warning / negative (auto-defaulted colors)
col_ring("risk", thresholds = c(0.33, 0.66))

# Integer score on [0, 100]
col_ring("score", min_value = 0, max_value = 100,
         label_format = "integer")





Media and links

col_img()
Inline image cells. Images come from URL/path in the data column.


Code
col_img("logo_url")
col_img("avatar", shape = "circle", width = 40)
col_img("thumbnail", fallback = "No image")




col_reference()
Truncated text (e.g. citation) with optional clickable link and external-link icon.


Code
col_reference("citation")
col_reference("title", href_field = "doi_url",
              max_chars = 40)
col_reference("source", href_field = "url", show_icon = FALSE)





Grouping

col_group()
Wraps child columns under a shared header row. See Composing columns above.

title: Columns
text:
See also

Cell Styling — conditional formatting, formula pronouns
Row Styling — row-level bold/italic/color/bg/badge/icon
Visualizations — viz_*() axis-backed columns
web_col() reference — the low-level constructor

title: Export
text: Export tabviz widgets to static files for publications, presentations, and sharing. SVG is built in via the V8 engine; PNG and PDF go through the optional rsvg package.

title: Export
text:
Quick Example
Build a forest plot, then write it to SVG:


Code
data(glp1_trials)

p <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_jama(),
    title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes"
  )

save_plot(p, "export-examples/forest_example.svg", width = 700)





Exported SVG

title: Export
text:
save_plot() Arguments
save_plot() operates in three sizing modes — see the reference for the full table. The short version: which args you pass determines the mode, not the values.








Argument
Default
Purpose




x
required
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or SplitForest


file
required
Output path; format inferred from extension (.svg, .png, .pdf)


width
NULL
Display width in pixels. NULL preserves natural; with height or ratio triggers relayout.


height
NULL
Display height in pixels. Same semantics as width.


ratio
NULL
Target aspect ratio (width / height). Mode-3 trigger.


anchor
NULL
When only ratio is given, picks "width" / "height" / "auto" for how dims fall out of it.


flex
TRUE
Cap on flex-column absorption (TRUE = 2× / 0.5×; FALSE = no flex; numeric = custom).


auto_wrap
FALSE
Bump wrap on long-text columns to fill tall-aspect height with content.


scale
2
Raster fidelity multiplier for PNG (warns on .svg / .pdf).



Natural mode (no width / height / ratio) renders at the spec’s intrinsic dimensions — exactly what tabviz_natural_dimensions() reports:


Code
# Natural — let the spec decide its own shape
save_plot(p, "natural.svg")

title: Export
text:
Format Comparison








Format
Use case
Dependency




SVG
Vector format, infinite scalability, journals, editing
None (built in)


PNG
Raster format, presentations, screenshots
rsvg package


PDF
Vector format, print publications
rsvg package









NotePNG and PDF require rsvg



save_plot() writes SVG using V8 alone. PNG and PDF formats convert that SVG via rsvg::rsvg_png() / rsvg::rsvg_pdf(). Install with install.packages("rsvg").

title: Export
text:
SVG Export


Code
bar_data <- data.frame(
  department = c("Cardiology", "Oncology", "Neurology", "Pediatrics"),
  enrollment = c(245, 312, 189, 156)
)

p_bar <- tabviz(bar_data, label = "department",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("enrollment", color = "#3b82f6"),
            header = "Enrollment", width = 180, axis_range = c(0, 350)),
    col_numeric("enrollment", "N")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "Trial Enrollment by Department"
)

save_plot(p_bar, "export-examples/bar_example.svg", width = 500)





Bar Chart Export

title: Export
text:
Themed Exports
The active theme is preserved on export.


Code
p_jama <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_jama(),
    title = "Figure 1. Primary Endpoint"
  )

save_plot(p_jama, "export-examples/jama_theme.svg", width = 500)





JAMA Theme




Code
p_dark <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_dark(),
    title = "Dark Theme Export"
  )

save_plot(p_dark, "export-examples/dark_theme.svg", width = 500)





Dark Theme

title: Export
text:
PNG and PDF Export
# PNG at 2x resolution
save_plot(p, "figure.png", width = 800, scale = 2)

# PDF for print
save_plot(p, "figure.pdf", width = 800)

Resolution Guide for PNG



scale
Effective DPI
Use case




1
72 DPI
Quick preview


2
144 DPI
Web / screen


3
216 DPI
Presentations


4
288 DPI
High-res print

title: Export
section: Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
text:
Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
When a table is taller than a printed page (long meta-analyses, multi-cohort registries), attach paginate = paginate_spec() (or one of the presets) to break it into pages. The HTML viewer renders one page at a time with prev/next controls; save_plot(*.pdf) emits a multi-page PDF.


Code
data(glp1_trials)
# Synthesize a longer dataset for demonstration
big <- do.call(rbind, replicate(4, glp1_trials |>
  dplyr::filter(row_type == "data"), simplify = FALSE))
big$study <- sprintf("%s %02d", big$study, seq_len(nrow(big)))

p_long <- big |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    group = "group",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_jama(),
    title = "Long meta-analysis (paginated)",
    paginate = paginate_letter()
  )

save_plot(p_long, "export-examples/paginated.pdf", width = 700)


The result is a multi-page PDF — one PDF page per logical page, merged via qpdf::pdf_combine():


Code
qpdf::pdf_length("export-examples/paginated.pdf")


[1] 4



Configuring pagination
paginate_spec() exposes the full configuration; presets (paginate_letter(), paginate_a4(), paginate_slide()) pick sensible row counts for common formats.








Argument
Default
Purpose




rows
30
Maximum data rows per page


break_on
"split"
Where forced breaks occur: "split", "group", or "none"


keep_groups
TRUE
Never break inside a group — push to next page instead


orphan_min
3
Minimum rows on a trailing page; pulls rows back if too thin


repeat_header / repeat_legend / repeat_title
TRUE
Repeat page furniture on every PDF page


footnotes_on
"last"
"last" (final page only) or "every"


page_label
"x_of_y"
TRUE/FALSE/"x"/"x_of_y" or a function(page, total)


oversized_group_policy
"overflow"
What to do when a single group is larger than rows: "overflow", "warn", "error"





Shortcuts
The paginate argument on tabviz() and save_plot() accepts several shortcut forms:
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = TRUE)              # default paginate_spec()
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = 50)                # rows-per-page shorthand
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_letter()) # preset
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = NULL)              # explicit "no pagination"
A fluent modifier paginate() is also available for use after construction:
tabviz(data, ...) |> paginate(rows = 20, orphan_min = 5)
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = TRUE) |> paginate(NULL)  # turn off


Override at save time
save_plot() accepts its own paginate argument that overrides whatever is on the spec — useful for one-off renders without rebuilding:
# Force a denser page count for one render
save_plot(p_long, "out.pdf", paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 50))

# Flatten a paginated spec for a single-image format
save_plot(p_long, "out.png", paginate = NULL)






NoteSingle-image formats flatten by default



.png and .svg are single-image formats and cannot represent multiple pages. When you save a paginated tabviz to one of these, the spec is flattened to a single image and a cli warning fires. Pass paginate = NULL at the call site to silence the warning, or save to .pdf for true paginated output.








NoteMulti-page PDF needs qpdf



Each page is rendered separately to SVG → PDF (via rsvg), then the per-page PDFs are merged with qpdf::pdf_combine(). If qpdf is not installed, save_plot() falls back to writing a numbered series (out_p01.pdf, out_p02.pdf, …) and emits a warning. Install with install.packages("qpdf").




Pagination + split_by
When split_by is active, paginate cascades to every subview — each subview owns its own page set with its own row-count budget. Saving emits one combined multi-page PDF per split leaf:
sf <- tabviz(data, ..., split_by = "region", paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 20))
save_plot(attr(sf, "splitforest"), "out/region.pdf", width = 700)
# Writes: out/North.pdf, out/South.pdf, ... — each with its own pages

title: Export
text:
Exporting Split Tables
Pass a SplitForest (the output of split_table()) to save_split_table() to write one file per subgroup. The directory structure mirrors the split hierarchy.
split_p <- tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))

save_split_table(split_p, "output/plots", format = "svg")
# Writes: output/plots/Male/Male_Young.svg, output/plots/Female/Female_Old.svg, ...
save_split_table() accepts the same width, height, scale arguments as save_plot(). format is one of "svg", "pdf", "png".






Tipsave_plot() accepts SplitForest too



If you call save_plot(split_p, "output/plots") it dispatches to save_split_table() automatically. Pass a path with an extension to control the format.

title: Export
text:
See Also

Themes — Style for publication
Reference: save_plot() — Full API

title: Interactivity
text: Every tabviz widget is fully interactive by default. This page is the authoritative reference for web_interaction() — the spec object that toggles which UI chrome appears and which user capabilities are enabled.

title: Interactivity
text:
Default Interactions
Every tabviz widget includes these interactions out of the box:







Interaction
Action




Sort
Click column headers to sort


Select
Click rows to select/deselect


Hover
Hover to highlight rows


Collapse
Click group headers to collapse/expand


Resize
Drag column borders to resize


Filter
Per-column filter popovers in headers


Reorder
Drag rows or columns to reorder


Edit
Double-click cells to edit — also works on existing title / subtitle / caption / footnote


Paint
Paint-brush button opens a popover for stamping emphasis / muted / accent onto rows or cells


Export
Download menu in header (hover to reveal)


Themes
Theme switcher in header





Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_jama(),
    footnote = "Try: click headers to sort, hover over rows, drag column borders"
  )

title: Interactivity
text:
The show_* vs enable_* Rule
web_interaction() uses two prefixes deliberately:

show_* controls static UI visibility — whether a piece of chrome appears at all (show_legend, show_group_counts). Nothing the user does at runtime brings it back.
enable_* controls user capabilities — whether a behavior is available (enable_sort, enable_filters, enable_edit, …). The associated UI is rendered when the capability is on, hidden when off.

When in doubt: render-or-not is show_; can-the-user-do-it is enable_.

title: Interactivity
text:
web_interaction() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




show_legend
TRUE
Show legend for multi-effect plots


show_group_counts
FALSE
Show (n) after each row-group label


tooltip_fields
NULL
Character vector of column names to show in marker hover tooltip


enable_sort
TRUE
Click headers to sort


enable_collapse
TRUE
Click group headers to collapse/expand


enable_select
TRUE
Click rows to select


enable_hover
TRUE
Highlight rows on hover


enable_resize
TRUE
Drag column borders to resize


enable_export
TRUE
Show download button in header


enable_filters
TRUE
Per-column filter popovers in column headers


enable_reorder_rows
TRUE
Drag to reorder rows (within a group)


enable_reorder_columns
TRUE
Drag to reorder columns (within a column group)


enable_edit
TRUE
Double-click inline editing for text/numeric/label cells


enable_themes
"default"
Theme menu: "default", NULL, or list of WebTheme

title: Interactivity
Interactivity
Interactivity
text:
Presets
tabviz ships three preset configurations.







Preset
Description




web_interaction()
Full interactivity (the default)


web_interaction_minimal()
Hover only — no sorting, selection, resize, export, filtering, reorder, edit


web_interaction_publication()
Fully static — every interaction off, no legend


web_interaction_full()
Explicit “everything on” — same as web_interaction() defaults




Minimal Interaction


Code
glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160)
    ),
    interaction = web_interaction_minimal(),
    theme = web_theme_jama()
  )








Publication Mode
web_interaction_publication() removes all interactivity — suitable for print, static documents, or screenshots.


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(...),
  interaction = web_interaction_publication()
)

title: Interactivity
text:
Theme Menu
The enable_themes argument controls the theme switcher. Three values are accepted:







Value
Effect




"default"
Show all themes returned by package_themes()


NULL
Hide the theme menu entirely


list(...)
Show only the listed WebTheme objects (named entries override displayed names)





Code
# Hide the theme menu
tabviz(data, interaction = web_interaction(enable_themes = NULL), ...)

# Curate a specific shortlist
tabviz(data, interaction = web_interaction(
  enable_themes = list(
    web_theme_jama(),
    web_theme_cochrane(),
    web_theme_cochrane()
  )
), ...)


selectable_themes() is a fluent sibling that does the same thing post-construction:


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study") |>
  set_theme("jama") |>
  selectable_themes(list(
    Classical = web_theme_jama(),
    Modern    = web_theme_cochrane()
  ))


The active theme is always auto-included in the menu so the user can revert.

title: Interactivity
text:
Pan & Zoom on Visualization Columns
Every viz column (viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin) supports direct-manipulation pan and zoom on its x-axis:







Gesture
Action




Mouse wheel over the viz area
Zoom in/out, anchored on the cursor


Click and drag
Pan the axis


Double-click
Reset to the default domain



State is per column — two forest columns side-by-side can be inspected independently. It is session-only (refreshing the page resets everything); there is no R-side argument to persist a zoom level.
Exports are WYSIWYG: zooming or panning in the browser changes what save_plot() writes to SVG/PNG and what the in-widget download button produces. Confidence intervals that fall outside the zoomed window are rendered with boundary arrows; marks on bar/boxplot/violin columns are clipped to the visible region.
On log-scale forests (viz_forest(scale = "log")), gestures that would push the lower bound to zero or below are ignored.

title: Interactivity
text:
Aspect-ratio slider
The toolbar’s zoom button (the magnifier icon) opens a dropdown with three sizing controls: zoom level, fit-to-width, and an aspect-ratio slider that drives the same lever ladder that save_plot(ratio = ...) uses for static export.







Control
Action




Zoom slider (50–200 %)
Scales the entire rendered widget. Same as Cmd +/–.


Fit-to-width button
Resets to a scale that matches the container width.


Aspect-ratio slider
Reshapes the layout — flex columns absorb width changes (capped at 2× of natural), row heights absorb height changes. Sticky detent at the spec’s natural aspect; drag-and-release returns to natural cleanly.


Readable rows toggle
Below the aspect slider. When on, the slider uses anchor = "auto": wide ratios grow output width and preserve row legibility, scaling content to fit the canvas. When off, behaves like anchor = "width": narrows row heights to fit a strict aspect within the canvas. The toggle auto-flips on first slider drag so interactive use is “ambitious” by default.


Max-width / max-height selects
Container constraints, independent of the aspect slider.



Behind the scenes, the slider mutates the spec’s target_aspect and target_aspect_anchor fields. The “View source” panel emits the corresponding set_aspect_ratio(N, anchor = "auto") call so any slider position round-trips into reproducible R code. The settings panel’s Spacing tab also shows a banner and live row-height value when the slider is active (see Themes — live spacing values).
For the static-export side of the same machinery, see save_plot(..., ratio = , anchor = ) and set_aspect_ratio().

title: Interactivity
text:
Row Selection
Selected rows are tracked internally and persist during sorting and filtering.


Code
sample_data <- data.frame(
  item = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta"),
  value = c(10, 25, 15, 30),
  change = c(0.05, -0.12, 0.08, 0.22)
)

tabviz(sample_data, label = "item",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("value", "Value"),
    col_percent("change", "Change")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  footnote = "Click rows to select (indicated by left border)"
)

title: Interactivity
text:
Paint Tool (v0.15+)
The paint-brush button on the widget toolbar opens a small popover where authors can stamp semantic classes onto rows or cells interactively. This is the in-widget equivalent of the R-side row_emphasis = <col> / row_muted = <col> / row_accent = <col> arguments, but applied by clicking rather than coded up front.
Flow:

Open the paint popover (brush icon in the top-right toolbar).
Pick a token: Emphasis, Muted, or Accent.
Flip the Scope switch between Row (whole row) and Cell (just one cell).
Click any row or cell to toggle the active flag on/off. The cursor turns into a crosshair while paint is active so you can spot where the next click lands.
Click the prominent Exit paint mode button at the top of the popover (or press Esc) when done. The brush icon stays lit in the toolbar’s accent color as long as paint is armed.

Persistence: painted flags merge into exportSpec, so save_plot() picks them up, and the flags round-trip through the “View source” button in the settings panel. You can also clear all paint with the “Clear all paint” button if you want to start over.
Painted flags coexist with R-side row_emphasis / etc. columns: the R column sets the baseline, paint overrides per row.

title: Interactivity
text:
Configure Columns (v0.18+)
Every col_* and viz_* column has a Configure… entry in its header context menu (right-click the column header, or use the column-menu chevron). The popover exposes the column’s relevant authoring arguments so you can tweak them without rebuilding the widget in R:

col_numeric — decimals, format.
col_interval — decimals, separator, imprecise threshold.
col_text — max chars, wrap.
viz_forest — scale, null value, axis label, and a full Axis section (range, tick count/values, gridlines, null tick, marker margin, symmetric).
viz_bar / viz_boxplot / viz_violin — full CRUD on effects (add / remove / reorder / rename / recolor / opacity / data field), plus the same Axis section. Boxplot can toggle between array-shaped and pre-computed stats data.

Double-clicking a viz column’s axis opens the configure popover pre-scrolled to the Axis section.
Changes merge into exportSpec and round-trip through the “View source” button — save_plot() picks them up. The interactive Axis theme tab was retired in v0.18 since axis values are now per-column.

title: Interactivity
text:
Title / Subtitle / Caption / Footnote Editing (v0.15+)
When enable_edit is on:

Double-click any existing title, subtitle, caption, or footnote to edit it inline (Enter to commit, Esc to cancel).
Add a missing label via the Basics tab in the settings panel — it exposes four text fields (Title / Subtitle / Caption / Footnote). Populating any of them makes the matching slot appear.

Edits merge into exportSpec.labels, so save_plot() reflects them.

title: Interactivity
Interactivity
text:
See Also

Reference: Interaction Settings — Full API reference
Export — Exporting interactive tables
Shiny Integration — Interactivity in Shiny apps

title: Row Styling
text: Control the visual appearance of individual rows using row_* parameters. This gives you precise control over text styling, indentation, colors, and badges—essential for creating publication-ready meta-analysis figures.

title: Row Styling
text:
Text Styling
Control text appearance with row_bold, row_italic, and row_color:


Code
# Use glp1_trials which has pre-computed row_bold
data(glp1_trials)

text_demo <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials", row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  mutate(
    ritalic = !row_bold,
    rcolor = ifelse(row_bold, "#0369a1", "#64748b")
  )

tabviz(text_demo,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  row_italic = "ritalic",
  row_color = "rcolor"
)

title: Row Styling
text:
Indentation
Create visual hierarchy with row_indent. Values represent nesting levels (0, 1, 2, …):


Code
indent_demo <- data.frame(
  label = c(
    "All Patients",
    "  Age < 65",
    "    Male", "    Female",
    "  Age >= 65",
    "    Male", "    Female"
  ),
  hr = c(0.78, 0.72, 0.70, 0.75, 0.85, 0.82, 0.88),
  lower = c(0.68, 0.58, 0.54, 0.60, 0.72, 0.66, 0.72),
  upper = c(0.89, 0.89, 0.91, 0.94, 1.00, 1.02, 1.08),
  rindent = c(0, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2),
  rbold = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(indent_demo,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_indent = "rindent",
  row_bold = "rbold",
  theme = web_theme_cochrane()
)

title: Row Styling
text:
Badges
Add small labels to highlight specific rows with row_badge:


Code
# nba_efficiency has an 'award' column perfect for badges
data(nba_efficiency)

nba_efficiency |>
  filter(conference == "East") |>
  head(6) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "player",
    columns = list(
      col_text("team", "Team", width = 60),
      col_numeric("ppg", "PPG"),
      viz_forest(point = "per", lower = "per_lower", upper = "per_upper",
                 null_value = 15, axis_label = "PER")
    ),
    row_badge = "award",
    row_bold = "all_star",
    theme = web_theme_cochrane()
  )






Badges are useful for marking awards, flagging key results, or indicating data quality.






WarningBadge and icon anchor to the first column



row_badge and row_icon render into the leftmost visible text column’s cell — they do not belong to the row itself. If you call move_column() or insert a new column with add_column(..., after = "__start__"), the badge will re-attach to the new first column without warning. To keep a stable anchor, keep your label/study column in position 1. See Binding Rules below.








TipSee Also



For more examples with badge data, see Package Data.

title: Row Styling
text:
Background Colors
Highlight rows with row_bg for background colors:


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_bg = "background_color"  # Column with hex colors like "#f0f9ff"
)


Use sparingly—subtle tints work best.

title: Row Styling
text:
Semantic Styling
For theme-aware styling that adapts automatically when you change themes, use one of the five semantic tokens. Each names a visual treatment that the theme paints in its own colors — a row “tagged” with row_accent will pick the active theme’s accent color, so the same code restyles itself when you swap from JAMA to Lancet.








Parameter
Treatment
Use for




row_emphasis
Bold + primary-color text and marker
Key results, primary endpoints


row_muted
Lighter color, reduced prominence
Secondary results, exploratory data


row_accent
Bold + accent-color text and marker (most common)
Highlighted findings, callouts


row_bold
Just a weight bump, no color change
Distinguish without coloring


row_fill
Bold + a strong row fill (auto-contrasting fg)
Summary / pooled rows





Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_emphasis = "is_primary",   # Column with TRUE/FALSE
  row_muted    = "is_secondary", # Column with TRUE/FALSE
  row_fill     = "is_pooled"     # Column with TRUE/FALSE — strong row fill
)


These adapt automatically to any theme, so you don’t need to update color values when switching from web_theme_jama() to web_theme_lancet(). See Themes — Semantic tokens for the underlying RowSemantic bundles and per-token customization.

Cascading into viz cells
row_accent, row_emphasis, and row_muted aren’t just text styling — they also reach into any viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, or viz_violin column on the same row. The intent (“this row is special”) is declared once and styles both the row text and its visualization.
The behavior depends on whether the viz column has one effect or several:







Viz column
Behavior when row is accented/emphasized/muted




Single-effect (viz_forest(point=..., lower=..., upper=...), viz_bar(effect_bar(...)), etc.)
The marker / bar / box / violin fill is replaced with the theme’s accent / foreground / muted color.


Multi-effect (viz_forest(effects = list(...)), viz_bar(effect_bar(a), effect_bar(b)), etc.)
The per-effect color is preserved (so users don’t lose their visual encoding). An outline in the theme color is added around each glyph in the row.



Single-effect example — a forest where rows are colored by significance:


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  row_accent   = ~ pval < 0.01,         # accent fill on strong results
  row_emphasis = ~ pval >= 0.01 & pval < 0.05,
  row_muted    = ~ status == "exploratory",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)


Multi-effect example — same row_accent keeps the per-effect colors but adds an outline:


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  row_accent = "is_key",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",  color = "#16a34a"),
      effect_forest("saf_hr", "saf_lo", "saf_hi", color = "#f59e0b")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)


The same cascade works on viz_bar, viz_boxplot, and viz_violin. See the styling cascade reference for the full 4-layer precedence model and gallery_17–gallery_21 for rendered examples covering each viz type.






TipWhen to use marker_color vs semantic classes




Semantic classes (row_accent, row_emphasis, row_muted) — for “this row is special” intent. Themable: changing the theme automatically restyles markers. Reach for these first.
marker_color = "col" \| ~ formula — for exact-color control (categorical palettes, colorblind-safe encodings, primary colors). Wins over the semantic cascade. Returns NA to fall through to the semantic layer.

title: Row Styling
text:
Expression-Based Styling
Instead of creating style columns in your data, you can use formula expressions that are evaluated on the fly. This is especially useful for conditional styling based on data values:


Code
expr_demo <- data.frame(
  study = c("Alpha 2020", "Beta 2021", "Gamma 2022", "Delta 2023"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91, 0.68),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75, 0.52),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10, 0.89),
  pval = c(0.01, 0.1, 0.3, 0.005)
)

tabviz(expr_demo,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  # Formula expressions - no need for pre-computed columns
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05,
  row_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.05, "#166534", "#64748b"),
  title = "Expression-Based Styling",
  subtitle = "Bold + green color for significant results (p < 0.05)"
)







How It Works

Use the tilde (~) to start a formula expression
The expression is evaluated against your data frame
All column names in your data are available in the expression
Results should be logical (TRUE/FALSE) for boolean styles, or strings for colors



Multiple Conditions
Combine conditions using & (and) or | (or):


Code
# Bold if significant AND large effect
row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05 & hr < 0.8

# Emphasize if significant OR key study
row_emphasis = ~ pval < 0.05 | is_primary

# Color based on effect direction and significance
row_color = ~ case_when(
  hr < 1 & pval < 0.05 ~ "#166534",  # Significant benefit - green
  hr > 1 & pval < 0.05 ~ "#dc2626",  # Significant harm - red
  TRUE ~ "#64748b"                    # Non-significant - gray
)








TipWhen to Use Expressions vs Columns



Use formulas when:

Styling is based on simple conditions (pval < 0.05)
You want concise, readable code
Style logic is tightly coupled to the visualization

Use columns when:

Style values are pre-computed or loaded from data
Same styling is reused across multiple plots
Complex logic is better computed separately

title: Row Styling
text:
Row Types
The row_type parameter controls fundamental row behavior. This is a manual approach for creating structured layouts with headers, summaries, and spacers—useful when you need precise control over the document structure.



Type
Behavior
Marker




"data"
Standard data row
Point with CI line


"header"
Section header, spans full width
None


"summary"
Pooled/subtotal row
Diamond shape


"spacer"
Empty row for separation
None





Code
# glp1_trials has pre-computed row_type column
glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 null_value = 1, scale = "log")
    ),
    row_type = "row_type"
  )












ImportantNA Values for Non-Data Rows



Rows with row_type = "header" or "spacer" should have NA for point, lower, and upper. Summary rows need valid values to display the diamond.








TipRow Types vs Row Groups



For automatic grouping based on a column in your data, consider using the group parameter instead—it creates collapsible sections without manually specifying row types. See Row Groups.








NoteBuilt-in Dataset



The glp1_trials dataset includes pre-computed row_type and row_bold columns. See Package Data for details.

title: Row Styling
text:
Complete Example: Meta-Analysis Figure
Here’s a publication-ready meta-analysis combining all row styling features:


Code
meta_analysis <- data.frame(
  label = c(
    "Cardiovascular Outcomes",
    "  EMPA-REG 2015", "  CANVAS 2017", "  DECLARE 2019", "  Subtotal", "",
    "Renal Outcomes",
    "  CREDENCE 2019", "  DAPA-CKD 2020", "  Subtotal", "",
    "Overall"
  ),
  hr = c(NA, 0.86, 0.86, 0.93, 0.88, NA, NA, 0.70, 0.61, 0.66, NA, 0.78),
  lower = c(NA, 0.74, 0.75, 0.84, 0.82, NA, NA, 0.59, 0.51, 0.57, NA, 0.73),
  upper = c(NA, 0.99, 0.97, 1.03, 0.95, NA, NA, 0.82, 0.72, 0.76, NA, 0.84),
  n = c(NA, 7020, 10142, 17160, 34322, NA, NA, 4401, 4304, 8705, NA, 43027),
  rtype = c(
    "header", "data", "data", "data", "summary", "spacer",
    "header", "data", "data", "summary", "spacer", "summary"
  ),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE),
  rindent = c(0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0),
  rcolor = c("#0369a1", NA, NA, NA, "#0369a1", NA, "#0369a1", NA, NA, "#0369a1", NA, "#166534"),
  rbadge = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, "n=3", NA, NA, NA, NA, "n=2", NA, "n=5")
)

tabviz(meta_analysis,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("n", "N"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log", axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold",
  row_indent = "rindent",
  row_color = "rcolor",
  row_badge = "rbadge",
  theme = web_theme_cochrane(),
  title = "SGLT2 Inhibitors Meta-Analysis",
  subtitle = "Cardiovascular and renal outcomes"
)

title: Row Styling
text:
Binding Rules
Not every row-level parameter is anchored the same way. Some attach to the row (stable under column reordering); others render into a specific cell (can re-attach when columns move). Knowing which is which prevents surprises when you use move_column(), add_column(..., after = "__start__"), or remove_column().








Parameter
Bound to
Stable under column reorder?




row_bold, row_italic, row_color, row_bg
Row CSS class
✅ Yes


row_emphasis, row_muted, row_accent, row_bold, row_fill
Row CSS class (theme-aware)
✅ Yes


row_indent
Row CSS class (applies to label column)
✅ Yes


row_type ("data", "header", "summary", "spacer")
Row CSS class
✅ Yes


row_badge, row_icon
First visible text column’s cell
⚠️ No — re-attaches if first column changes


marker_color, marker_shape, marker_size, marker_opacity
SVG marker on the forest column
✅ Yes


set_row_label() edits (proxy)
Label column
⚠️ Lost if label column is removed




Why badge and icon are special
Badges and icons visually extend the row label — they always need a specific cell to anchor inside. rather than hard-coding them to a named column, tabviz resolves their target at serialization time by picking the first visible text column. This keeps the API simple (no extra column = argument on set_row_style()) at the cost of quiet coupling to column order.
In practice:

Keep your label column in position 1. This is the common case and nothing will surprise you.
If you call move_column("tier", to = 1) or add_column(col_icon("flag"), after = "__start__"), expect badges/icons to re-render on the new leftmost column.
If you rebuild a plot via update_data() with a WebSpec whose columns are in a different order, the same re-anchoring applies.



Stable anchoring
If you need a badge that stays with a specific column regardless of layout, set badge = "col" directly on the target column constructor instead of using row_badge. Column-level badges are bound to their column and unaffected by row-level re-anchoring.


Code
# Row-level: anchors to the leftmost visible column (may move)
tabviz(data, label = "study", row_badge = "sig", columns = list(...))

# Column-level: anchors to the "study" column (stable)
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  col_text("study", badge = "sig"),
  ...
))

title: Row Styling
text:
Parameter Reference
All row_* parameters accept either a column name (string) or a formula expression (~ ...).








Parameter
Column Contains
Description




row_bold
TRUE/FALSE
Bold text


row_italic
TRUE/FALSE
Italic text


row_color
CSS color (e.g., "#1a365d")
Text color


row_bg
CSS color
Background color


row_indent
Integer (0, 1, 2, …)
Indentation level


row_badge
Text
Badge label


row_icon
Emoji/unicode
Icon before label


row_emphasis
TRUE/FALSE
Bold + theme primary text/marker


row_muted
TRUE/FALSE
Lighter color, reduced prominence


row_accent
TRUE/FALSE
Bold + theme accent text/marker


row_bold (semantic)
TRUE/FALSE
Weight bump only, no color change


row_fill
TRUE/FALSE
Bold + strong row fill (auto-contrasting fg)


row_type
"data", "header", "summary", "spacer"
Row behavior and marker type









NoteFormula Examples



# Using column name
row_bold = "is_significant"

# Using formula expression
row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05

title: Row Styling
text:
See Also

Row Groups — Automatic collapsible sections from column values
Visualization Styling — How row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted cascade into viz cells
Fluent API — Pipe-based equivalents (set_row_style()) for programmatic workflows
Styling Overview — Where this layer fits in the cascade
tabviz() — All arguments

title: Themes
text: A theme decides every visual choice in your table that isn’t data: surface colors, text tones, divider weights, spacing, fonts, marker fills. tabviz themes are designed so you can pick a preset, change one or two things, and never think about it again — or build a fully branded theme from a dozen inputs when you need to.

title: Themes
section: Three steps from “pick a preset” to “fully bespoke”
text:
Three steps from “pick a preset” to “fully bespoke”
Each step builds on the previous. Most users live at step 1.





flowchart LR
    A["1. Pick a preset"] --> B["2. Tweak inputs"]
    B --> C["3. Override component fields"]

    style A fill:#dbeafe
    style B fill:#bfdbfe
    style C fill:#93c5fd







Step 1 — theme = web_theme_lancet(). Done. Seven presets cover the common publication, dashboard, and illustrative surfaces.
Step 2 — Swap a primary color, change density, switch to a serif font. The preset stays the foundation; you nudge a few inputs.
Step 3 — Reach into a specific component (e.g. the L1 row-group bar background) for surgical control. Rare; the preset cascade handles 95% of cases.

title: Themes
text:
Step 1: Pick a preset


Code
tabviz(
  meta_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_lancet()
)


Seven presets ship in the package — four journal-style themes designed for publication, and three illustrative LOTR themes that exercise the full surface for non-journal contexts (talks, blog posts, internal reports). Each leans into a clear identity rather than trying to be everything.








Preset
Style
Designed for




web_theme_cochrane()
The package default. Cochrane teal + warm coral, Inter sans-serif, comfortable density
General purpose, systematic reviews


web_theme_lancet()
Lancet navy + refined old-gold + warm cream, Georgia serif, comfortable
Lancet-style editorial layouts


web_theme_jama()
Pure black-and-white, ultra-compact, strong dividers throughout, Arial
JAMA / print journal submissions


web_theme_dark()
Catppuccin Mocha-inspired dark canvas with pastel marker palette
Dark-mode viewing


web_theme_dwarven()
Stone neutrals, forge-glow accent, broad serif display
Talks, blog posts, themed reports


web_theme_elvish()
Twilight surface, mithril silvers, calligraphic serif
Talks, blog posts, themed reports


web_theme_hobbit()
Parchment + green, hand-set body face, hearth accent
Talks, blog posts, themed reports



Switch presets at any point with set_theme():


Code
my_table |> set_theme("jama")


The full registry is in package_themes() (returns a 2-level list keyed journals / lotr).

Curating the in-widget switcher
The in-widget settings panel includes a theme dropdown that lets readers swap between presets without reloading. By default it shows every package theme; pass enable_themes = list(...) to web_interaction() or selectable_themes() to curate the menu:


Code
# Flat list — no tabs, just a single column of choices.
selectable_themes(my_table, list(
  Cochrane = web_theme_cochrane(),
  Lancet   = web_theme_lancet()
))

# 2-level named list — each top-level key becomes a tab in the dropdown.
selectable_themes(my_table, list(
  Editorial = list(
    Cochrane = web_theme_cochrane(),
    Lancet   = web_theme_lancet(),
    JAMA     = web_theme_jama()
  ),
  Other = list(Dark = web_theme_dark())
))


The active theme is auto-included so readers can always revert. For categorized lists, if the active theme isn’t in any user-supplied category, a Current tab is prepended.

title: Themes
text:
Step 2: Tweak inputs
Every theme — including every preset — is determined by a small set of customer-facing inputs on ThemeInputs. They split into three logical groups:








Group
Input
What it controls




Chrome
neutral
5-step ramp, lightest → darkest. Drives surfaces, dividers, content.



primary + primary_deep
Primary identity (markers, accents) and its dark companion (text, strokes, bold-mode header band). primary_deep auto-derives by darkening primary when left NA.



secondary + secondary_deep
Secondary identity. Owns chrome texture (surface.muted, dividers, banding, gridlines) and structural defaults (column-group bold band, L1/L2/L3 row-group bg, glyph-column defaults). Mirrors primary when left NA.



accent + accent_deep
Engagement layer — orthogonal to identity. Drives hover, selected, semantic row callouts, selection edge.



status_positive / status_negative / status_warning / status_info
Semantic status colors.


Data
series_anchors
Per-effect anchor colors (variable length). The first anchor also drives the pooled-effect diamond.



slot_style
How each SlotBundle’s fill / stroke pair relate. One of "fill_with_darker_stroke" (default), "flat_fill", "outlined".


Type
font_body
Primary face for cells, headers, labels.



font_display
Optional title face. Mirrors font_body when NA.



font_mono
Optional monospace face (e.g. for IDs).



Build a custom theme by overriding any of these:


Code
my_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "my-brand",
  inputs = list(
    primary          = "#1F3A5F",   # navy
    accent         = "#B08938",   # warm gold
    series_anchors = c("#1F3A5F", "#B08938", "#6B8E7F"),
    font_body      = "Charter, Georgia, serif"
  )
)


web_theme() starts from web_theme_cochrane() unless you pass a different base_theme. Anything you don’t set inherits from the base.
The deep companions and secondary tier follow predictable defaults:

primary_deep auto-derives by darkening primary when left NA (override when you want a richer bold-mode header band or a journal-specific stroke).
secondary mirrors primary when NA; secondary_deep mirrors secondary’s deep companion.
accent_deep mirrors accent.
font_display mirrors font_body.

The pooled-effect diamond reads from the first series slot bundle (theme@series[[1]]). Override series_anchors[1] to recolor it; reach into theme@series[[1]]@stroke directly for a journal-specific outline.

What primary_deep actually drives
primary_deep is the dark identity tone — meant to land on text and rules where you want the primary to read as serious, not decorative. The resolver threads it into:

Plot title color (text@title@fg)
Forest-axis label and tick-label colors (plot@axis_label@fg, plot@tick_label@fg)
Cell-hairline tint — divider@subtle is neutral[3] mixed with 8% primary_deep, so cell borders pick up a faint identity color without muddying out
Bold-mode header band (header@bold@bg) and column-group bold band
Bold-mode header rule (header@bold@rule) via the new divider@strong_on_dark helper — a 40%-deep / 60%-inverse mix that contrasts against the band instead of disappearing into it
L1 group bar — but only when the active variant is header_style = "bold". Under light-mode the L1 bar stays on accent.tint_subtle (chrome/data wall: primary only “leaks” into chrome when you’ve chosen brand-forward chrome).

To pin any of these, set the field directly via set_theme_field() and your value survives re-resolution. The interactive panel surfaces the same override semantics as a per-field reset icon.


L1 group header and banding
When theme@row@banding cycles at the L1 level (the default for every preset — banding = "group" with one nesting level), the banding cycle already paints alternating fills across the L1 group rows. The renderer detects this and drops the explicit theme@row_group@L1@bg paint on the affected rows so the banded color reads as continuous and the group-bar doesn’t double-paint on top.
If you want a custom L1 bar paired with a banded background, switch banding modes (e.g. set_theme_field(c("row", "banding"), "row") or "none") so the L1 paint isn’t subsumed.
At deeper banding levels (e.g. banding = "group-2" with nested groups), the L1 bar’s explicit fill is preserved unchanged — only the banded level loses its explicit fill.


Semantic tokens — naming visual treatments
Tabviz ships with five semantic tokens that name visual treatments you can apply to a row or cell. Each token is a RowSemantic bundle on theme.row: bg, fg, border, marker_fill, font_weight, font_style. The renderer picks one token per row/cell and paints its bundle.







Token
Default treatment




emphasis
Bold + primary text and marker


muted
Lighter color, reduced prominence


accent
Bold + accent-color text and marker (most common)


bold
Just a weight bump — no color change


fill
Bold + a strong row fill (defaults to a derived theme.semantic.fill); fg auto-contrasts



Two paths to apply a token to a row:

Data column — pass a logical column to tabviz():


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")),
  row_accent = "is_primary",      # rows where is_primary == TRUE → accent token
  row_fill   = "is_overall"       # rows where is_overall == TRUE → fill token
)


Painter (interactive) — click the paintbrush icon, pick a chip (emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill), then click rows or cells to stamp the token on. Hover before clicking shows a semi-transparent preview of the would-be visual. The painter defaults to accent + row-scope on first open. Toggle Row/Cell scope from the popover.


Customizing token visuals
Two ways to override:

Tier-2 named color for the fill background — pin a specific color for the fill token instead of letting it derive from accent:


Code
set_theme_field(t, c("semantic", "fill"), "#1F3A5F")  # custom fill navy


Or interactively: Settings panel → Tokens tab → Token colors.
Per-bundle override for any field on any token:


Code
set_theme_field(t, c("row", "fill",   "fg"),     "#FFFFFF")  # force white text on fill rows
set_theme_field(t, c("row", "accent", "border"), "#C8553D")  # red border on accent rows


Or interactively: Settings panel → Tokens tab → expand a token → edit any field. All bundle fields are override-aware (reset reverts to the resolved default).




Variants — three knobs every table can flip
Variants are per-table choices that don’t change the theme’s identity. They’re orthogonal to inputs and freely composable:


Code
web_theme(
  inputs = list(primary = "#1F3A5F"),
  variants = list(
    density            = "comfortable",   # compact | comfortable | spacious
    header_style       = "bold",          # light  | bold
    first_column_style = "bold"           # default | bold (Excel-style)
  )
)



density drives every spacing token (row height, padding, gaps). The three presets cover almost every screen and print scenario.
header_style picks between a light header on the muted surface and a bold header on primary_deep with inverse text. Both options are always available on every theme — you flip the variant per table.
first_column_style gives the row-label column an Excel-style emphasized treatment (background tint + heavier weight). Useful when your first column is genuinely a label, not data.

You can also set variants directly on the tabviz() call as overrides (see tabviz reference).

title: Themes
section: How it cascades under the hood
text:
How it cascades under the hood
A theme has three tiers. You only ever interact with Tier 1; the other tiers are computed.





flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["Tier 1 — Inputs (you edit these)"]
      neutral & primary & accent & series & fonts & density
    end

    subgraph T2C["Tier 2 — Chrome roles (derived)"]
      surface[surface.base / muted / raised]
      content[content.primary / secondary / muted / inverse]
      divider[divider.subtle / strong]
      tints[accent.default / muted / tint.subtle / tint.medium]
    end

    subgraph T2D["Tier 2 — Data roles (derived)"]
      slots[per-series SlotBundle: fill, stroke, fill.muted, ...]
    end

    subgraph T3["Tier 3 — Component bindings"]
      header & row & cell & rowGroup & plot & marks
    end

    T1 --> T2C
    T1 --> T2D
    T2C --> T3
    T2D --> T3

    style T1 fill:#dbeafe
    style T2C fill:#dcfce7
    style T2D fill:#fef3c7
    style T3 fill:#f3e8ff






Two cascades, sharing only Tier 1. Chrome derivation reads only chrome inputs (neutrals + primary + accent). Data derivation reads only data inputs (series anchors + status). Series anchors never affect surface tones; primary colors never bleed into row-group divider tints. This isolation makes themes predictable: changing series_anchors cannot accidentally shift the column-header background.
OKLCH derivation. All blends, lightenings, and darkenings happen in OKLCH (perceptually uniform). The result: tints stay readable across the brightness range, and high-chroma brands don’t mud out when blended with surfaces.
Slot bundles. Each series anchor expands into a 7-field bundle: fill, stroke, fill_muted, stroke_muted, fill_emphasis, stroke_emphasis, text_fg. The renderer picks the right field based on row state (default / muted / emphasized) without you having to define every state by hand. Override individual fields if you need to.
If you want to inspect a resolved theme:


Code
t <- web_theme_lancet()
t@surface@base       # "#FDFCFB"
t@content@primary    # "#1E3A5F"
t@accent@tint_subtle # tinted accent for L1 group bar
t@series[[1]]@fill   # first series anchor
t@series[[1]]@stroke # auto-darkened companion
t@row@hover@bg       # accent.muted blend

title: Themes
Themes
text:
Going deeper: spacing and typography

Density preset values



Token
compact
comfortable
spacious




row_height
20
24
30


header_height
26
32
40


padding
8
12
16


axis_gap
8
12
16


cell_padding_x
8
10
14


column_group_padding
6
8
12


title_subtitle_gap
10
13
18


bottom_margin
12
16
22


indent_per_level
14
16
20



set_spacing() overrides any of these per-token; the rest fall back to the active density preset. Read Density for how spacing tokens flow into the renderer.


Aspect-aware spacing
When the in-widget aspect-ratio slider is engaged (or set_aspect_ratio() is pinned on the spec), the lever ladder scales row_height to absorb the height delta. The Spacing settings tab reflects this live:
