A small banner at the top of the tab explains: “Row height is currently driven by the aspect-ratio slider. Editing it will clear the aspect target.”
The Row height field reads its effective value (post-lever-ladder) rather than the raw spec value, and is labelled Row height (aspect) to flag its provenance.
Editing the field clears target_aspect (so your typed value pins, the slider returns to natural). View-source emits the new set_spacing(row_height = ...) call cleanly, with no overlap with set_aspect_ratio().

Other spacing tokens (header_height, axis_gap, header_gap, footer_gap, etc.) keep their authored values in the live widget, so the slider’s effect is concentrated on row_height. Static export via save_plot() honours the full lever ladder including chrome scaling — when achieved aspect deviates from target by > 5 %, a cli::cli_warn() surfaces the residual.


Typography roles
A theme exposes eight named text roles:



Role
Used for




title
Plot title (display family, large, semibold)


subtitle
Subtitle (body family, medium)


caption
Italic caption above the plot


footnote
Footnote below the plot


body
Default body text (cells, headers when not bold)


cell
Data cell text (defaults to body)


label
Axis labels


tick
Axis tick labels (smaller)



Edit individual fields with set_theme_field():


Code
web_theme_cochrane() |>
  set_theme_field(c("text", "title", "weight"), 700) |>
  set_theme_field(c("text", "title", "italic"), TRUE)

title: Themes
section: The seven presets — when to use which
text:
The seven presets — when to use which
The four journal presets are the workhorses; the three LOTR presets exercise the full surface (display fonts, secondary tier, distinctive accents) and are great for talks, blog posts, or themed reports where you want personality without hand-rolling a brand theme.


Code
forest_args <- list(
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

# --- Journal presets --------------------------------------------------------

# Cochrane — package default; teal + warm coral, comfortable density
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args, list(theme = web_theme_cochrane())))

# Lancet — navy + old-gold + warm cream, Georgia serif
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args, list(theme = web_theme_lancet())))

# JAMA — black-and-white, ultra-compact, strong dividers, Arial
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args, list(theme = web_theme_jama())))

# Dark mode — Catppuccin Mocha-inspired
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args, list(theme = web_theme_dark())))

# Slide deck variant — any preset can flip to spacious density
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args,
                  list(theme = web_theme_cochrane() |> set_variants(density = "spacious"))))

# --- LOTR illustrative presets ---------------------------------------------

# Dwarven — stone neutrals, forge-glow accent, broad serif display
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args, list(theme = web_theme_dwarven())))

# Elvish — twilight surface, mithril silvers, calligraphic serif
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args, list(theme = web_theme_elvish())))

# Hobbit — parchment + green, hand-set body face, hearth accent
do.call(tabviz, c(list(meta_data), forest_args, list(theme = web_theme_hobbit())))


See the Gallery → LOTR Easter Egg page for a worked LOTR example.

title: Themes
text:
Editing themes interactively
Every rendered table carries a settings gear. The in-widget panel exposes the v2 theme model under five tabs:







Tab
Edits




Theme
Tier 1 inputs — primary, secondary, accent, status, series anchors, fonts


Layout
Variants (density / header style / first column style), banding, container border


Spacing
Per-token overrides on top of the density preset (collapsed Advanced section)


Marks
Series slot bundles (anchor + expandable 7-field editor), summary slot, plot mark sizes


Text
Eight text-role bundles + L1/L2/L3 row-group accordions



The “View source” button exports your edits as v2 R code (web_theme(inputs = list(...)) or a chain of set_*() calls), so panel exploration translates back into reproducible theme construction.

title: Themes
section: Saving a custom theme as a function
text:
Saving a custom theme as a function
For team-wide use, wrap your custom theme in a named function:


Code
web_theme_acme <- function() {
  web_theme(
    name = "acme",
    inputs = list(
      neutral        = c("#FFFFFF", "#FAFAFA", "#EEEEEE", "#888888", "#222222"),
      primary          = "#FF5500",
      primary_deep     = "#CC3300",
      accent         = "#0066CC",
      series_anchors = c("#FF5500", "#0066CC", "#00AA66", "#993399", "#666666"),
      font_body      = "Inter, system-ui, sans-serif",
      font_display   = "'Inter Display', Inter, system-ui, sans-serif"
    ),
    variants = list(density = "comfortable", header_style = "bold")
  )
}

# Now share it across the team
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(...), theme = web_theme_acme())


Pass to set_theme() either by string (when registered as a preset) or by handing the constructed WebTheme object directly:


Code
my_table |> set_theme(web_theme_acme())

title: Themes
text:
Reference summary

web_theme() — custom-theme constructor (inputs + variants + base).
web_theme_*() — 7 preset constructors (cochrane, lancet, jama, dark, dwarven, elvish, hobbit).
package_themes() — 2-level named list (journals / lotr) of all preset constructors.
set_theme(spec, theme) — switch a WebSpec to a different theme.
set_inputs(theme, ...) — update Tier 1 inputs.
set_variants(theme, ...) — update per-table variants.
set_spacing(theme, ...) — per-token spacing overrides.
set_theme_field(theme, path, value) — generic deep setter.
selectable_themes(spec, themes) — curate the in-widget theme switcher.

For complete argument lists and class definitions see the Themes reference.

title: Boxplots
text: viz_boxplot() summarizes distributions with median, quartiles, and outliers. It accepts either raw arrays of values or pre-computed summary statistics.

title: Boxplots
text:
Mode 1 — From Array Data
When each row contains an array of values, boxplot statistics are computed automatically:


Code
set.seed(42)
boxplot_data <- data.frame(
  treatment = c("Control", "Low Dose", "Medium Dose", "High Dose"),
  response = I(list(
    rnorm(50, mean = 100, sd = 15),
    rnorm(50, mean = 110, sd = 18),
    rnorm(50, mean = 125, sd = 20),
    rnorm(50, mean = 140, sd = 25)
  )),
  n = c(50, 50, 50, 50)
)

tabviz(boxplot_data, label = "treatment",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "response", color = "#8b5cf6"),
      header = "Response Distribution",
      width = 250,
      show_outliers = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Dose-Response Distribution"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
Mode 2 — From Pre-computed Statistics
If you already have quartile statistics, map them directly:


Code
summary_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
  min = c(45, 52, 38, 60),
  q1 = c(62, 68, 55, 72),
  median = c(78, 82, 71, 85),
  q3 = c(89, 94, 86, 95),
  max = c(105, 112, 98, 108)
)

tabviz(summary_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(
        min = "min", q1 = "q1", median = "median", q3 = "q3", max = "max",
        color = "#22c55e"
      ),
      header = "Score Distribution",
      width = 220
    ),
    col_numeric("median", "Median")
  ),
  title = "Study Score Distributions"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
Multiple Effects
Compare distributions across conditions with multiple effect_boxplot() items:


Code
set.seed(123)
multi_box_data <- data.frame(
  timepoint = c("Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 12"),
  placebo = I(list(
    rnorm(40, 50, 12), rnorm(40, 52, 13), rnorm(40, 51, 14), rnorm(40, 53, 12)
  )),
  treatment = I(list(
    rnorm(40, 50, 12), rnorm(40, 62, 14), rnorm(40, 70, 15), rnorm(40, 75, 13)
  ))
)

tabviz(multi_box_data, label = "timepoint",
  columns = list(
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "placebo", label = "Placebo", color = "#94a3b8"),
      effect_boxplot(data = "treatment", label = "Treatment", color = "#3b82f6"),
      header = "Score by Treatment Arm",
      width = 280,
      show_outliers = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Treatment Effect Over Time"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
effect_boxplot() Arguments







Argument
Purpose




data
Column containing array of values (mode 1)


min, q1, median, q3, max
Column names for pre-computed stats (mode 2)


outliers
Column containing outlier values (optional, mode 2)


label
Display label for legend


color
Box fill color


opacity
Box fill opacity (default 0.7)

title: Boxplots
text:
See Also

Visualization Styling — How row_accent / marker_color cascade into box fills
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, common features
Bar Charts — Single values with viz_bar()
Violin Plots — Full distributions with viz_violin()

title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots display point estimates with confidence intervals — the standard visualization for meta-analyses and clinical trial results. Build them with the viz_forest() column inside tabviz(), or use the forest_plot() shortcut for quick one-liners.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Data Mapping
viz_forest() uses a column-mapping pattern: name the columns that hold your data, not the values themselves.


Code
data(effect_sizes)

effect_sizes |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ))







Required Mappings








Argument
What it is
Example




point
Point estimate column
Hazard ratio, odds ratio, mean difference


lower
Lower CI bound column
95% CI lower


upper
Upper CI bound column
95% CI upper





Handling Missing Values
tabviz uses NA strategically for structured layouts:

Header rows: row_type = "header" with NA for effect estimates
Spacer rows: row_type = "spacer" for visual separation
Missing estimates: data rows where the effect couldn’t be calculated



Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", row_type = "row_type", row_bold = "row_bold",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    )
  )

title: Forest Plots
text:
viz_forest() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




point / lower / upper
required (single-effect mode)
Column names for the point estimate and CI bounds


effects
NULL
List of effect_forest() items for multi-effect mode


header
NULL
Column header text


width
auto
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log"


null_value
NULL
Position of the dashed reference line (e.g. 1 for ratios, 0 for differences)


axis_range
auto
c(min, max) overriding the auto-calculated range


axis_ticks
auto
Custom tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines at tick positions


axis_label
"Effect"
X-axis label


annotations
NULL
List of refline() / forest_annotation() items


shared_axis
FALSE
Share axis range with sibling forest columns


show_axis
TRUE
Show the axis

title: Forest Plots
text:
Scale and Null Value

Log Scale
Use for ratios (hazard ratio, odds ratio, risk ratio) — effects are multiplicative around null_value = 1:


Code
effect_sizes |>
  filter(outcome == "Primary") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)")
  ))












WarningLog Scale Requires Positive Values



All values in point, lower, and upper must be positive. Zero or negative values will error.




Linear Scale
Use for differences (mean difference, risk difference, SMD) — effects are additive around null_value = 0:


Code
diff_data <- data.frame(
  comparison = c("Treatment A", "Treatment B", "Treatment C"),
  mean_diff = c(-2.5, 1.3, -0.8),
  lower = c(-4.1, -0.2, -2.1),
  upper = c(-0.9, 2.8, 0.5)
)

tabviz(diff_data, label = "comparison", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "mean_diff", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "linear", null_value = 0,
             axis_label = "Mean Difference (95% CI)")
))

title: Forest Plots
text:
Multiple Effects
Display multiple effect estimates per row using effects = list(effect_forest(...), ...). Useful for comparing analyses or sensitivity scenarios:


Code
multi_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("PIONEER", "SUMMIT", "HORIZON"),
  n = c(2450, 1890, 3200),
  itt_or = c(0.72, 0.78, 0.65),
  itt_lo = c(0.58, 0.64, 0.52),
  itt_hi = c(0.89, 0.95, 0.81),
  pp_or = c(0.68, 0.73, 0.61),
  pp_lo = c(0.53, 0.58, 0.47),
  pp_hi = c(0.87, 0.92, 0.79)
)

tabviz(multi_data, label = "study", title = "Sensitivity Analysis Comparison",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_interval("itt_or", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", header = "Primary OR"),
    viz_forest(
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      effects = list(
        effect_forest("itt_or", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
        effect_forest("pp_or", "pp_lo", "pp_hi", label = "Per-Protocol", color = "#16a34a")
      )
    )
  )
)







effect_forest() Arguments







Argument
Purpose




point
Column name for point estimates


lower
Column name for lower bounds


upper
Column name for upper bounds


label
Display label for legend


color
Override color for this effect


shape
Override shape: "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"





Side-by-Side Forest Columns
When comparing entirely different outcomes, separate viz_forest() columns are often clearer than overlaid effects:


Code
comparison_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Cardiovascular", "Mortality", "Hospitalization", "Composite"),
  na_hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.78, 0.76),
  na_lo = c(0.58, 0.71, 0.64, 0.65),
  na_hi = c(0.89, 1.02, 0.95, 0.89),
  eu_hr = c(0.68, 0.79, 0.82, 0.74),
  eu_lo = c(0.52, 0.62, 0.67, 0.61),
  eu_hi = c(0.89, 1.01, 1.00, 0.90)
)

tabviz(comparison_data, label = "study",
  title = "Regional Comparison: North America vs Europe",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "na_hr", lower = "na_lo", upper = "na_hi",
               header = "North America", scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180),
    viz_forest(point = "eu_hr", lower = "eu_lo", upper = "eu_hi",
               header = "Europe", scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180)
  )
)












TipSide-by-Side vs Multi-Effect




Side-by-side viz_forest() columns — comparing different outcomes or populations on potentially different scales.
Multi-effect overlays via effect_forest() — comparing the same outcome across analyses (sensitivity, ITT vs PP) on a single shared scale.





shared_axis
Set shared_axis = TRUE on multiple viz_forest() columns to lock them to a common axis range so the eye can compare across columns directly.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Marker Customization
Customize marker appearance based on data values using formula expressions on tabviz():


Code
effect_sizes |>
  filter(outcome == "Primary") |>
  head(6) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", title = "Marker Styling with Formulas",
    marker_shape = ~ ifelse(phase == "Phase 3", "circle", "square"),
    marker_color = ~ ifelse(significant, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8"),
    marker_opacity = ~ ifelse(n > 200, 1, 0.7),
    columns = list(
      col_text("phase", "Phase"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    )
  )














Argument
Type
Effect




marker_color
string / formula
CSS color for marker fill


marker_shape
string / formula
"square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"


marker_opacity
numeric / formula
Transparency (0–1)


marker_size
numeric / formula / "col"
Size multiplier (1 = default), or column name for proportional sizing



All marker arguments accept either a column name or a formula expression (~ ...). Pass marker_size = "col_name" to scale markers by a column (e.g. study weight in a meta-analysis).

title: Forest Plots
text:
Axis Control
Axis arguments live on viz_forest() itself — they’re per-column.

Axis Range


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_range = c(0.3, 2.0))
))








Custom Tick Values


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1,
             axis_range = c(0.3, 2.0),
             axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0))
))








Gridlines


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_gridlines = TRUE)
))

title: Forest Plots
text:
Reference Lines and Annotations
null_value draws a single dashed reference line. For more, pass annotations = list(...) of refline() / forest_annotation() items.


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", title = "With Custom Reference Line",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(0.8, label = "Threshold", style = "dashed", color = "#e11d48")
               ))
  )
)








Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", title = "Clinical Thresholds",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(0.5, label = "Strong", style = "solid", color = "#16a34a"),
                 refline(0.8, label = "Moderate", style = "dashed", color = "#f59e0b")
               ))
  )
)







refline() Arguments



Argument
Purpose




value
X-axis position


label
Optional label text


style
"solid", "dashed", "dotted"


color
Line color


width
Line width (default 1.5)


opacity
Line opacity 0–1 (default 0.6)



forest_annotation() adds richer annotations (regions, text callouts) — see the reference for the full surface.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Working with Meta-Analysis Results


Code
library(metafor)

res <- rma(yi = log_or, sei = se, data = studies, method = "REML")

forest_data <- studies |>
  mutate(
    or = exp(log_or),
    lower = exp(log_or - 1.96 * se),
    upper = exp(log_or + 1.96 * se)
  ) |>
  bind_rows(
    tibble(
      study = "Pooled Estimate",
      or = exp(res$b),
      lower = exp(res$ci.lb),
      upper = exp(res$ci.ub),
      row_type = "summary",
      row_bold = TRUE
    )
  )

tabviz(forest_data,
  label = "study", row_type = "row_type", row_bold = "row_bold",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Violin Plots
text: viz_violin() shows the full distribution shape using kernel density estimation. Use it when distribution shape matters — bimodality, skewness, asymmetry — rather than just summary statistics.

title: Violin Plots
text:
Basic Usage


Code
set.seed(456)
violin_data <- data.frame(
  group = c("Young Adults", "Middle Age", "Seniors"),
  scores = I(list(
    c(rnorm(80, 72, 8), rnorm(20, 90, 5)),     # bimodal
    rnorm(100, 68, 12),                          # normal
    c(rnorm(70, 60, 10), runif(30, 40, 80))     # skewed
  )),
  n = c(100, 100, 100)
)

tabviz(violin_data, label = "group",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_violin(
      effect_violin(data = "scores", color = "#f59e0b"),
      header = "Score Distribution",
      width = 250,
      show_median = TRUE,
      show_quartiles = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Score Distributions by Age Group"
)

title: Violin Plots
text:
Comparing Distributions
Use multiple effect_violin() items to overlay distributions:


Code
set.seed(789)
compare_violin <- data.frame(
  condition = c("Healthy", "Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"),
  biomarker_a = I(list(
    rnorm(60, 25, 5),
    rnorm(60, 35, 8),
    rnorm(60, 50, 12),
    rnorm(60, 70, 15)
  )),
  biomarker_b = I(list(
    rnorm(60, 30, 6),
    rnorm(60, 40, 7),
    rnorm(60, 55, 10),
    rnorm(60, 65, 18)
  ))
)

tabviz(compare_violin, label = "condition",
  columns = list(
    viz_violin(
      effect_violin(data = "biomarker_a", label = "Biomarker A", color = "#3b82f6"),
      effect_violin(data = "biomarker_b", label = "Biomarker B", color = "#ef4444"),
      header = "Biomarker Distributions",
      width = 280,
      show_median = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Biomarker Profiles by Disease Severity"
)

title: Violin Plots
text:
viz_violin() Arguments



Argument
Default
Purpose




header
NULL
Column header text


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log"


axis_range
auto
Fixed axis range c(min, max)


axis_ticks
auto
Custom tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines


bandwidth
NULL
KDE bandwidth (NULL uses Silverman’s rule of thumb)


show_median
TRUE
Show the median line


show_quartiles
FALSE
Show Q1/Q3 dashed lines


axis_label
"Value"
Label text below the axis


show_axis
TRUE
Show the axis


null_value
NULL
Optional reference value — paints a dashed line at this position


annotations
NULL
List of refline() items

title: Violin Plots
text:
effect_violin() Arguments



Argument
Purpose




data
Column containing array of values (required)


label
Display label for legend


color
Violin fill color


opacity
Fill opacity (default 0.5)
